question: What is Spring Boot and how does it differ from the Spring Framework?
answer:
Spring Boot is an opinionated layer on top of Spring. Spring gives you the IoC container, DI, and MVC but leaves assembly to you (XML, servlet config, dependency versions, a servlet container to deploy into). Spring Boot removes that assembly work with four things:

  • Auto-configuration — it inspects the classpath and configures sensible beans automatically (see a DataSource driver, get a DataSource).
  • Starters — one dependency (spring-boot-starter-web) pulls a curated, version-aligned set.
  • Embedded server — Tomcat/Jetty/Undertow ship inside the jar, so it's java -jar, not a WAR deployed to an external container.
  • Production-ready features — Actuator health, metrics, and externalized config out of the box.

Spring Boot does not replace Spring — every bean, proxy, and annotation is still core Spring underneath.
tip: 'Convention over configuration.' The strongest version of this answer names auto-configuration as the core idea and adds that Boot is not a new framework — it's Spring with the assembly pre-done.
deep:
What Spring Boot actually removes

Pre-Boot, a trivial Spring MVC app needed a web.xml, a DispatcherServlet declaration, a component-scan element, a view resolver bean, a hand-picked set of dependency versions that don't conflict, and a Tomcat install to deploy the WAR into. Boot collapses all of it into one annotation and one main method.

Opinionated, not restrictive

This is the follow-up that separates seniors from juniors. Every auto-configured bean is conditional on you not having defined your own:

@Bean
@ConditionalOnMissingBean   // <- the escape hatch
public DataSource dataSource() { ... }

Define your own DataSource and Boot silently backs off. So the defaults are a starting point you can always override — that's what "opinionated" means here.

The layer cake

Spring Boot      -> auto-config, starters, embedded server, Actuator
Spring Framework -> IoC container, DI, AOP, MVC, @Transactional
Java / Servlet API

How to say it in an interview

“Spring Boot is Spring with the boilerplate pre-solved. It auto-configures beans based on what's on the classpath, gives you version-aligned starters and an embedded Tomcat so the app is a runnable jar, and adds Actuator for production. It's opinionated but not restrictive — every auto-configuration backs off the moment you define your own bean.”

question: Explain the core stereotype annotations.
answer:
• @Component — the generic Spring-managed bean; the other three are specialisations of it.
  • @Service — business logic. Semantically identical to @Component; it documents intent.
  • @Repository — data access. Not just a marker: it enables exception translation, converting vendor-specific SQLExceptions into Spring's DataAccessException hierarchy.
  • @Controller / @RestController — web endpoints. @RestController = @Controller + @ResponseBody, so returns are serialised to JSON instead of resolved as view names.
  • @Configuration — a class declaring @Bean methods.

All are picked up by component scanning and registered in the ApplicationContext.
tip: The trap: 'are @Service and @Component the same?' Yes functionally — but @Repository genuinely adds exception translation, so it's the one that isn't purely cosmetic.
deep:
Why the distinction exists at all

To the container, @Service and @Component are interchangeable — both are meta-annotated with @Component and both get scanned. The value is threefold: readability, targeted pointcuts (@within(org.springframework.stereotype.Service) in AOP), and the one real behavioural difference below.

The @Repository exception translation

PersistenceExceptionTranslationPostProcessor proxies @Repository beans and rewrites persistence exceptions into Spring's consistent, unchecked hierarchy:

SQLException (vendor code 23505)   -> DataIntegrityViolationException
Hibernate ConstraintViolation      -> DataIntegrityViolationException

Your service layer catches one portable exception type instead of vendor codes. (With Spring Data JPA the proxy is created for you, so the annotation on the interface is optional.)

@Controller vs @RestController

@Controller
String home() { return "index"; }        // resolved as a VIEW named "index"

@RestController
String home() { return "index"; }        // response BODY is the string "index"

A classic bug: using @Controller for a REST API and getting a 404 view-resolution error rather than JSON.

How to say it in an interview

“They're all @Component underneath, so scanning treats them the same. @Service and @Controller mainly express intent and give AOP something to target. @Repository is the exception — it triggers persistence exception translation into Spring's DataAccessException hierarchy, so it does real work.”

question: What does @SpringBootApplication do?
answer:
It's a convenience meta-annotation combining three:

  • @SpringBootConfiguration — a @Configuration class; the primary source of bean definitions.
  • @EnableAutoConfiguration — turns on the classpath-driven auto-configuration machinery.
  • @ComponentScan — scans the annotated class's package and all sub-packages for components.

That last point has a practical consequence: the main class's package placement defines your scanning root.
tip: Very common follow-up: 'why isn't my bean being injected?' — usually the class sits outside the main class's package tree, so it was never scanned.
deep:
Package placement is the whole game

Put the main class in the root package, above everything else. If it lives in com.example.app but your service is in com.example.services, the service is never registered and you get NoSuchBeanDefinitionException — the single most common Boot beginner bug.

Tuning it

@SpringBootApplication(
  scanBasePackages = {"com.example.app", "com.shared.lib"},
  exclude = { DataSourceAutoConfiguration.class }   // drop one auto-config
)

If you need different attributes on each part, drop the shortcut and use the three annotations separately — they aren't mutually exclusive.

Why component scanning can hurt startup

Scanning is reflective classpath work. On a huge codebase, narrowing scanBasePackages measurably cuts startup time; that's a good senior-level detail to volunteer.

How to say it in an interview

“It's three annotations in one: @SpringBootConfiguration, @EnableAutoConfiguration, and @ComponentScan. The practical thing to remember is that component scanning starts from that class's package downward, so the main class belongs in the root package — beans outside that tree silently never register.”

question: What are Spring Boot starters?
answer:
Starters are curated dependency descriptors — a single POM that transitively pulls in a coherent, version-compatible set of libraries for one capability. spring-boot-starter-web brings Spring MVC, Jackson, validation, and embedded Tomcat in one line.

They solve two problems: you stop hunting for the right artifacts, and the parent BOM guarantees the versions actually work together, so you never specify a version yourself.
tip: Starters are mostly empty — they're pure dependency aggregators containing almost no code. Naming convention: official ones are spring-boot-starter-*, third-party ones use *-spring-boot-starter.
code:
<parent>
  <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
  <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-parent</artifactId>
  <version>3.2.0</version>
</parent>

<dependency>
  <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
  <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-web</artifactId>
  <!-- no version: the parent BOM decides -->
</dependency>
deep:
The dependency-hell problem they solve

Manually assembling Spring MVC + Jackson + Hibernate Validator + Tomcat means picking versions that are mutually compatible, then re-checking on every upgrade. spring-boot-dependencies (the BOM the parent imports) pins hundreds of versions that were tested together. Upgrading Boot upgrades the whole tested set at once.

Common starters

spring-boot-starter-web        MVC + Jackson + Tomcat
spring-boot-starter-webflux    reactive stack (Netty)
spring-boot-starter-data-jpa   Hibernate + Spring Data + HikariCP
spring-boot-starter-security   Spring Security
spring-boot-starter-test       JUnit 5, Mockito, AssertJ, MockMvc
spring-boot-starter-actuator   health, metrics, info

Swapping a transitive piece

<dependency>
  <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-web</artifactId>
  <exclusions><exclusion>
    <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-tomcat</artifactId>
  </exclusion></exclusions>
</dependency>
<dependency><artifactId>spring-boot-starter-undertow</artifactId></dependency>

Exclude Tomcat, add Undertow — auto-configuration follows the classpath and wires Undertow instead. That's starters and auto-config working together.

Without the parent

Can't use spring-boot-starter-parent (corporate parent POM)? Import the BOM in dependencyManagement with <scope>import</scope> and keep the version management.

How to say it in an interview

“A starter is a curated dependency bundle — one artifact that transitively pulls everything needed for a capability, with versions pinned by Boot's BOM so they're guaranteed compatible. They contain almost no code; they exist to kill dependency hell and to give auto-configuration the classpath signals it reacts to.”

question: What is auto-configuration and how does Spring Boot decide what to configure?
answer:
Auto-configuration means Boot registers beans for you based on what it finds on the classpath and in your config, rather than you declaring them. Add H2 and it configures an in-memory DataSource; add spring-boot-starter-web and it configures a DispatcherServlet and embedded Tomcat.

The decisions are made by @Conditional annotations on auto-configuration classes:

  • @ConditionalOnClass — is this type on the classpath?
  • @ConditionalOnMissingBean — has the user not defined their own?
  • @ConditionalOnProperty — is this property set?
tip: The magic word is @ConditionalOnMissingBean — it's why your own bean always wins over Boot's default. Run with --debug to print the auto-configuration report showing matches and why others didn't.
deep:
Seeing it work

Run the app with --debug (or set debug=true) and Boot prints the Condition Evaluation Report: which auto-configurations matched, which didn't, and the exact condition that failed. This is the single best debugging tool for "why is this bean here / not here", and mentioning it reads as real experience.

Positive matches:
  DataSourceAutoConfiguration matched:
    - @ConditionalOnClass found 'javax.sql.DataSource'

Negative matches:
  MongoAutoConfiguration:
    - @ConditionalOnClass did not find 'com.mongodb.client.MongoClient'

Turning one off

@SpringBootApplication(exclude = SecurityAutoConfiguration.class)
// or:  spring.autoconfigure.exclude=org.springframework...SecurityAutoConfiguration

Ordering

Auto-configurations run after your own @Configuration classes — that ordering is what makes @ConditionalOnMissingBean reliable. Among themselves they order with @AutoConfigureBefore / @AutoConfigureAfter / @AutoConfigureOrder.

How to say it in an interview

“Boot ships auto-configuration classes guarded by @Conditional annotations. At startup it evaluates them against the classpath, existing beans, and properties — so adding a driver jar is enough to get a DataSource. @ConditionalOnMissingBean makes them back off when I define my own, and --debug prints the evaluation report when I need to know why something did or didn't configure.”

question: application.properties vs application.yml — what's the difference?
answer:
Purely a format choice; both feed the same Environment. Properties are flat key=value; YAML is hierarchical, less repetitive, and supports lists and multiple documents (profile blocks) in one file.

Two practical notes: if both exist, .properties wins for conflicting keys; and YAML is whitespace-sensitive, so indentation bugs are a real cost. Neither format supports the other's edge cases perfectly — YAML has surprises like on/off parsing as booleans.
tip: YAML is the norm on real projects because profile-specific blocks live in one file. Know that .properties takes precedence if both are present — that's the gotcha they're fishing for.
code:
# application.yml
spring:
  datasource:
    url: jdbc:postgresql://localhost/db
    username: app
server:
  port: 8081

# equivalent .properties
spring.datasource.url=jdbc:postgresql://localhost/db
spring.datasource.username=app
server.port=8081
deep:
Multi-document YAML for profiles

One file, per-profile overrides — the main reason teams pick YAML:

spring:
  application:
    name: orders
---
spring:
  config:
    activate:
      on-profile: dev
  datasource:
    url: jdbc:h2:mem:test
---
spring:
  config:
    activate:
      on-profile: prod
  datasource:
    url: jdbc:postgresql://prod-db/orders

(spring.config.activate.on-profile replaced the old spring.profiles key in Boot 2.4+.)

Lists

# YAML
app:
  origins:
    - https://a.com
    - https://b.com

# properties
app.origins[0]=https://a.com
app.origins[1]=https://b.com

YAML gotchas worth knowing

  • Tabs are illegal — spaces only.
  • Unquoted on, off, yes, no can become booleans.
  • A version like 1.10 unquoted is a number and loses the trailing zero.
  • @PropertySource does not load YAML — a genuinely surprising limitation.

How to say it in an interview

“Same Environment, different syntax. YAML wins on hierarchy, lists, and multi-document profile blocks in one file, which is why most projects use it. Properties wins on being dumb and whitespace-proof. If both exist, properties takes precedence for the same key.”

question: How does Spring Boot resolve config when the same property is set in several places?
answer:
Boot layers PropertySources in a defined precedence order and the highest-priority one wins. Roughly, highest to lowest:

  1. Command-line arguments (--server.port=9090)
  2. SPRING_APPLICATION_JSON
  3. OS environment variables / JVM system properties
  4. Profile-specific config outside the jar (application-prod.yml)
  5. Profile-specific config inside the jar
  6. Plain application.yml outside the jar, then inside the jar
  7. @PropertySource
  8. SpringApplication.setDefaultProperties

The design intent: the same artifact runs in every environment, with the environment supplying overrides from outside.
tip: The rule of thumb: the more external and the more specific, the higher it wins. This is why 'it works locally but prod has the wrong value' is almost always an env var overriding your yml.
code:
# all three set server.port; the CLI arg wins
java -jar app.jar --server.port=9090

SERVER_PORT=8888 java -jar app.jar      # env var (relaxed binding)

# application.yml inside the jar
server.port: 8080
deep:
Relaxed binding

An env var can't contain dots, so Boot maps them: SPRING_DATASOURCE_URL binds to spring.datasource.url. Uppercase + underscores is the canonical env form, and it's how you configure containers and Kubernetes without touching the image.

spring.datasource.url  ==  SPRING_DATASOURCE_URL
app.max-retry-count    ==  APP_MAXRETRYCOUNT  /  APP_MAX_RETRY_COUNT

Why this design matters

Build once, deploy everywhere. The jar is immutable; the environment injects DB URLs and secrets via env vars or a mounted application.yml. Baking prod config into the jar defeats it and is a real red flag in an interview.

Where Boot looks for files

./config/          (highest)
./
classpath:/config/
classpath:/         (lowest)

A file at ./config/application.yml next to the jar beats the one packaged inside — the standard way to override without rebuilding. Override the search path with spring.config.location or add to it with spring.config.additional-location.

Debugging it

Actuator's /actuator/env shows every PropertySource in order and which one supplied the winning value — the fastest way to settle "where is this value coming from?"

How to say it in an interview

“Boot stacks PropertySources by precedence — command line, then env vars and system properties, then profile-specific files, then plain application.yml, outside the jar beating inside. The point is one immutable artifact per build with the environment supplying overrides. When a value surprises me, /actuator/env tells me which source won.”

question: @Value vs @ConfigurationProperties — when do you use each?
answer:
@Value("${...}") injects a single property, supports SpEL and defaults, and is fine for one-off values. @ConfigurationProperties binds a group of properties onto a typed object.

For anything beyond a value or two, @ConfigurationProperties is the better answer, because it gives you: type safety, relaxed binding, nested objects and lists, JSR-303 validation, and IDE auto-completion via the metadata processor. @Value gives none of those and scatters string keys across the codebase.
tip: Say you prefer @ConfigurationProperties for anything grouped, and note that it validates at startup with @Validated — a typo'd config fails fast instead of NPE-ing at 2am.
deep:
Registering it

Three options — pick one:

@EnableConfigurationProperties(MailProps.class)   // on a @Configuration class
@ConfigurationPropertiesScan                      // on the main class, scans for them
@Component                                        // on the props class itself

Fail-fast validation

With @Validated, a bad or missing value throws at startup, not on first use:

APPLICATION FAILED TO START
Binding to target MailProps failed:
  Property: app.mail.host
  Reason: must not be blank

That is the argument that wins this question: config errors surface at deploy time, in the pipeline, instead of as a runtime NPE under load.

Relaxed binding — only @ConfigurationProperties gets it

app.mail.recipient-list  ==  APP_MAIL_RECIPIENTLIST  ==  app.mail.recipientList

@Value requires the exact key. This bites when moving to env-var config in containers.

What only @Value can do

@Value("#{systemProperties['user.region']}")     // SpEL
@Value("#{T(java.lang.Math).random() * 100}")
@Value("${missing.key:fallback}")                // inline default

@ConfigurationProperties has no SpEL — defaults come from field initialisers instead.

Immutable binding

Records or constructor binding give you final fields and no setters. With records it's automatic; with classes use @ConstructorBinding (implicit in Boot 3 for a single constructor).

How to say it in an interview

“@Value for a single value or when I need SpEL. @ConfigurationProperties for anything grouped — type-safe binding onto a record, relaxed binding so env vars work in containers, nested objects and lists, and @Validated so a bad config fails the app at startup rather than at 2am.”

question: What are bean scopes, and why is the singleton default dangerous?
answer:
The scope controls how many instances the container creates:

  • singleton (default) — one instance per container, shared by every injection point.
  • prototype — a new instance on every injection/lookup.
  • request, session, application, websocket — web-aware scopes.

The danger: a singleton is shared across all concurrent request threads. Any mutable instance field on a @Service is shared state and a race condition. Spring beans must be stateless — that's the rule the scope default silently depends on.
tip: This is the question behind the question: 'are Spring singletons thread-safe?' No — Spring guarantees one instance, not safety. Your stateless design is what makes it safe.
deep:
Singleton != thread-safe

Spring's singleton scope is about instance count, not synchronisation. One UserService instance serves 200 Tomcat threads at once. Mutable fields are shared mutable state.

Safe field types: the injected collaborators (assigned once at construction, then read-only) and immutable config. Unsafe: counters, accumulating lists, a "current user" field, a reused SimpleDateFormat (the classic — it's not thread-safe).

Injecting a prototype into a singleton — the trap

@Service
class Singleton {
  @Autowired private Prototype p;   // injected ONCE at startup!
  // every call reuses the SAME prototype -> scope silently ignored
}

The singleton is created once, so its dependency is resolved once. Fixes: inject ObjectProvider<Prototype> and call getObject() per use, or use @Lookup, or a scoped proxy:

@Bean
@Scope(value = "prototype", proxyMode = ScopedProxyMode.TARGET_CLASS)

Prototype lifecycle gap

Spring does not manage a prototype's full lifecycle — @PreDestroy is never called on one. The container hands it over and forgets it; cleanup is yours.

Request scope

@Component
@Scope(value = "request", proxyMode = ScopedProxyMode.TARGET_CLASS)
public class RequestContext { private String correlationId; /* safe here */ }

The proxy is what lets you inject a request-scoped bean into a singleton: the injected object is a proxy that resolves the real instance from the current thread's request on each call.

How to say it in an interview

“Singleton by default — one instance shared by every request thread — plus prototype and the web scopes. The important consequence is that singleton means one instance, not thread-safe: mutable fields on a service are a race condition, so beans stay stateless. And injecting a prototype into a singleton silently resolves once unless I use ObjectProvider or a scoped proxy.”

question: Walk through the Spring bean lifecycle.
answer: For each bean the container: instantiates it (constructor, with constructor-injected deps), populates the remaining dependencies (fields/setters), runs *Aware callbacks, runs BeanPostProcessor.postProcessBeforeInitialization, calls initialisation callbacks (@PostConstruct → InitializingBean.afterPropertiesSet → custom initMethod), then runs postProcessAfterInitialization — this is where AOP proxies are created. The bean is then in use, and on shutdown @PreDestroy → DisposableBean.destroy → custom destroyMethod run (singletons only).
tip: The payoff detail: proxies are applied in postProcessAfterInitialization — which is exactly why @Transactional doesn't work inside a constructor or @PostConstruct. The proxy doesn't exist yet.
deep:
The full order

1. Instantiate (constructor)
2. Populate properties / inject dependencies
3. BeanNameAware / BeanFactoryAware / ApplicationContextAware
4. BeanPostProcessor.postProcessBeforeInitialization
5. @PostConstruct
6. InitializingBean.afterPropertiesSet()
7. @Bean(initMethod = "...")
8. BeanPostProcessor.postProcessAfterInitialization   <- PROXIES created here
   ---- bean is live ----
9. @PreDestroy
10. DisposableBean.destroy()
11. @Bean(destroyMethod = "...")

Why @Transactional in @PostConstruct silently does nothing

At step 5 the raw object exists but the transactional proxy is only created at step 8. Self-calls in @PostConstruct bypass the proxy entirely — no transaction, no exception, just silence.

@PostConstruct
@Transactional          // NOT transactional — proxy doesn't exist yet
void warmCache() { repo.findAll(); }

Fix: use ApplicationReadyEvent or CommandLineRunner, which fire after the context is fully built and proxied.

Prefer @PostConstruct over InitializingBean

InitializingBean couples your class to the Spring API; @PostConstruct is a plain JSR-250 annotation. (In Boot 3 it comes from jakarta.annotation.)

Destroy callbacks aren't guaranteed

They run on a graceful shutdown hook. kill -9, an OOM kill, or a power loss skips them — so never rely on @PreDestroy for data integrity. And prototypes never get destroy callbacks at all.

How to say it in an interview

“Instantiate, inject, Aware callbacks, BeanPostProcessor-before, @PostConstruct, afterPropertiesSet, init method, BeanPostProcessor-after — and that last step is where AOP proxies get created, which is why @Transactional or @Async in a @PostConstruct does nothing. Then @PreDestroy and destroy on graceful shutdown, singletons only.”

question: How does dependency injection work in Spring, and why is constructor injection preferred?
answer:
The IoC container owns object creation: it reads bean definitions, resolves the dependency graph, instantiates beans, and injects collaborators — you never new a service. Injection comes in three forms: constructor, setter, and field.

Constructor injection is the recommendation, because it makes dependencies explicit in the signature, allows final fields (immutable, thread-safe), fails fast at startup on a missing bean, exposes design smells (a 10-arg constructor screams that the class does too much), and lets you instantiate the class in a plain unit test with new — no Spring, no reflection.

Since Spring 4.3, a single constructor needs no @Autowired at all.
tip: The killer argument against field injection: you cannot construct the object in a unit test without reflection or a Spring context. Also note @Autowired on a lone constructor is redundant — omitting it shows current knowledge.
deep:
Why field injection is discouraged

@Service
class BadService {
  @Autowired private UserRepo repo;   // can't be final
}
// test: new BadService() -> repo is null. You need reflection or a context.
  • Untestable without Spring — the field is private and set reflectively.
  • Not immutable — can't be final, so it can be reassigned.
  • Hides bloat — 15 @Autowired fields look fine; a 15-arg constructor doesn't. The pain is a feature.
  • Circular deps compile silently and blow up (or get lazily patched) at runtime.

IntelliJ flags it with "Field injection is not recommended" for exactly these reasons.

Constructor injection kills circular dependencies at startup

A ↔ B via constructors can't be built, so Boot fails immediately with a clear cycle report. With field injection Spring can often paper over it — which just defers the design problem. The failure is the useful outcome.

Setter injection's niche

Genuinely optional dependencies, or reconfigurable ones. Rare in practice; ObjectProvider or an Optional<T> parameter usually reads better:

public Svc(ObjectProvider<Tracer> tracer) {
  this.tracer = tracer.getIfAvailable(NoopTracer::new);
}

How to say it in an interview

“The container builds the graph and injects for me. I use constructor injection: fields can be final, dependencies are explicit in the signature, missing beans fail at startup instead of runtime, circular dependencies surface immediately, and I can unit test with plain new and mocks. Field injection can't be final and can't be constructed in a test without reflection. Since 4.3 a single constructor doesn't even need @Autowired.”

question: Two beans implement the same interface — how does Spring know which to inject?
answer:
It doesn't, and startup fails with NoUniqueBeanDefinitionException. You disambiguate with:

  • @Primary — marks the default winner when nothing more specific applies. Good for "one obvious default".
  • @Qualifier("name") — names the exact bean at the injection point. Beats @Primary.
  • Parameter name matching — a fallback: if the parameter is named after the bean, Spring resolves it.
  • Inject List<T> or Map<String,T> — take all implementations (the basis of the strategy pattern in Spring).
tip: Injecting Map<String, Handler> to get every implementation keyed by bean name is the clean strategy-pattern answer — it beats a switch statement and reads as senior.
deep:
Resolution order

1. @Qualifier at the injection point   (most specific — always wins)
2. @Primary on a bean definition
3. Parameter/field name == bean name
4. -> NoUniqueBeanDefinitionException

The strategy pattern, done properly

Instead of a growing switch, let the container collect implementations:

public interface PaymentHandler {
  boolean supports(PaymentType type);
  void handle(Payment p);
}

@Service
class PaymentService {
  private final List<PaymentHandler> handlers;   // Spring injects ALL of them
  PaymentService(List<PaymentHandler> handlers) { this.handlers = handlers; }

  void pay(Payment p) {
    handlers.stream()
      .filter(h -> h.supports(p.type()))
      .findFirst()
      .orElseThrow(() -> new IllegalStateException("no handler for " + p.type()))
      .handle(p);
  }
}

Adding a payment type = adding one @Service class. No existing code changes — open/closed principle for free. Order the list with @Order or Ordered.

Custom qualifier annotations

Stringly-typed qualifiers are typo-prone. Make a typed one:

@Qualifier @Retention(RUNTIME)
public @interface Sms { }

@Service @Sms  class SmsNotifier implements Notifier { }
OrderService(@Sms Notifier n) { }   // compile-time checked

@Primary's blast radius

@Primary is global — it wins at every ambiguous injection point, so it's easy to make a surprising default land somewhere you didn't intend. @Qualifier is local and explicit. Prefer @Primary only when there really is one canonical default.

How to say it in an interview

“@Primary for the default, @Qualifier at the injection point when I need a specific one — qualifier beats primary. And if I actually want all of them, I inject List<T> or Map<String,T>, which is how I'd implement a strategy pattern: each strategy is a bean, adding one requires no changes to the dispatcher.”

question: How do you build a REST endpoint, and how do you bind inputs?
answer:
Annotate the class @RestController, map methods with @GetMapping/@PostMapping/@PutMapping/@PatchMapping/@DeleteMapping (all shorthands for @RequestMapping(method=...)), and put the shared prefix on the class.

Input binding:

  • @PathVariable — part of the path (/users/42): identifies a resource.
  • @RequestParam — query string (?page=2): filters, paging, optional bits.
  • @RequestBody — the JSON body, deserialised by Jackson.
  • @RequestHeader, @CookieValue — headers and cookies.

Return a DTO (auto-serialised to JSON) or ResponseEntity when you need to control status and headers.
tip: The distinction they want: path variable = which resource, request param = how to filter/shape it. Also, POST should return 201 Created with a Location header — a detail most candidates skip.
deep:
Choosing status codes

GET    -> 200 OK            / 404 Not Found
POST   -> 201 Created + Location header
PUT    -> 200 OK or 204 No Content
DELETE -> 204 No Content
validation failure -> 400 Bad Request
unauthenticated    -> 401   |  authenticated but forbidden -> 403
conflict/duplicate -> 409

Returning 200 for everything (including errors, with a "success": false body) is a common anti-pattern to be ready to criticise.

Return the DTO or ResponseEntity?

Return the plain object when the status is always 200 — it's cleaner and easier to test. Use ResponseEntity when the status varies or you must set headers. Don't use ResponseEntity<?> everywhere "just in case" — it erases the type from the signature and from generated OpenAPI docs.

Never expose entities

@GetMapping("/{id}")
User get(@PathVariable Long id) { return repo.findById(id).orElseThrow(); }
// leaks passwordHash, triggers lazy-loading during serialisation (LazyInitializationException),
// and couples your public API to your DB schema

Map to a DTO/record. This is a very common senior-level follow-up.

Optional and required params

@RequestParam(required = false) String name      // null when absent
@RequestParam(defaultValue = "0") int page       // 0 when absent
@RequestParam Optional<String> filter            // explicit optionality

How to say it in an interview

“@RestController plus the mapping shorthands, prefix on the class. Path variables identify the resource, request params filter it, @RequestBody is the JSON payload. I return DTOs rather than entities — exposing an entity leaks fields and couples the API to the schema — and I use ResponseEntity when I need the status or headers, like 201 with a Location on create.”

question: How do you validate request payloads?
answer:
Bean Validation (Jakarta Validation / Hibernate Validator, via spring-boot-starter-validation). Annotate the DTO's fields (@NotBlank, @Email, @Min, @Size, @Pattern) and mark the controller parameter @Valid. A violation throws MethodArgumentNotValidException → 400.

Two important distinctions:

  • @Valid (Jakarta) triggers validation and cascades to nested objects. @Validated (Spring) adds validation groups and, on a class, enables method-level validation on non-controller beans.
  • Nested objects are only validated if the field itself carries @Valid.

Pair it with a @RestControllerAdvice to turn violations into a clean field-level error response.
tip: Two things that impress: (1) you need @Valid on nested fields or they're silently skipped, (2) the default 400 body is ugly — a real API maps violations to a field->message structure.
deep:
The starter is not automatic

Since Boot 2.3, spring-boot-starter-web no longer includes validation. Without spring-boot-starter-validation your @NotNull annotations compile and are silently ignored — a nasty real-world bug worth naming.

Validation groups

Same DTO, different rules per operation — id must be null on create, present on update:

interface OnCreate {}
interface OnUpdate {}

record UserDto(
  @Null(groups = OnCreate.class) @NotNull(groups = OnUpdate.class) Long id,
  @NotBlank String name) {}

@PostMapping void create(@Validated(OnCreate.class) @RequestBody UserDto d) {}
@PutMapping  void update(@Validated(OnUpdate.class) @RequestBody UserDto d) {}

Only @Validated supports groups — @Valid doesn't.

Validating service method params

@Service
@Validated                                  // class-level: enables method validation
class UserService {
  void register(@Email String email, @Min(18) int age) { }
  // violation -> ConstraintViolationException (NOT MethodArgumentNotValidException)
}

Different exception type, so your advice needs a handler for both.

Custom constraint

@Constraint(validatedBy = UniqueEmailValidator.class)
@Target(FIELD) @Retention(RUNTIME)
public @interface UniqueEmail {
  String message() default "email already registered";
  Class<?>[] groups() default {};
  Class<? extends Payload>[] payload() default {};
}

class UniqueEmailValidator implements ConstraintValidator<UniqueEmail, String> {
  private final UserRepo repo;                    // validators can be injected!
  UniqueEmailValidator(UserRepo repo) { this.repo = repo; }
  public boolean isValid(String v, ConstraintValidatorContext c) {
    return v == null || !repo.existsByEmail(v);
  }
}

@NotNull vs @NotEmpty vs @NotBlank

@NotNull   ""  -> valid    "  " -> valid    null -> invalid
@NotEmpty  ""  -> invalid  "  " -> valid    null -> invalid
@NotBlank  ""  -> invalid  "  " -> invalid  null -> invalid

For strings you almost always want @NotBlank. This exact comparison gets asked verbatim.

How to say it in an interview

“Jakarta Bean Validation — constraints on the DTO, @Valid on the controller param, and a @RestControllerAdvice that maps MethodArgumentNotValidException into a field-to-message map so clients get a usable 400. @Validated when I need groups or method-level validation on a service. Two gotchas: nested objects need @Valid on the field to cascade, and since Boot 2.3 you must add the validation starter or the annotations do nothing.”

question: How do you handle exceptions globally in a REST API?
answer:
@RestControllerAdvice — a class of @ExceptionHandler methods applied across all controllers, translating exceptions into consistent JSON and correct status codes. It keeps controllers free of try/catch and guarantees one error contract.

Since Boot 3, prefer ProblemDetail (RFC 7807) as the response body — a standard error shape (type, title, status, detail, instance) instead of a bespoke one. Extend ResponseEntityExceptionHandler to also cover Spring's built-in MVC exceptions.
tip: Say 'never let a raw exception reach the client' — stack traces leak class names, SQL, and internal structure. Log the detail server-side with a correlation id; return a generic message plus that id.
deep:
Handler precedence

Spring picks the most specific exception handler by class hierarchy, so a generic @ExceptionHandler(Exception.class) can coexist with specific ones as a catch-all. Across multiple advices, order with @Order; scope one with @RestControllerAdvice(basePackages="...") or assignableTypes.

ProblemDetail (RFC 7807)

{
  "type": "https://api.example.com/errors/insufficient-funds",
  "title": "Insufficient funds",
  "status": 402,
  "detail": "Balance 30.00 is below the required 50.00",
  "instance": "/accounts/12345/transfers",
  "balance": 30.00                    // custom property
}
var pd = ProblemDetail.forStatusAndDetail(HttpStatus.PAYMENT_REQUIRED, detail);
pd.setType(URI.create("https://api.example.com/errors/insufficient-funds"));
pd.setProperty("balance", balance);

A standard shape means clients (and gateways) parse errors the same way everywhere. Naming RFC 7807 lands well.

Security: what not to return

Default error pages and leaked stack traces are an information-disclosure vulnerability — they reveal frameworks, versions, package structure, and sometimes SQL. Log the exception with a correlation id; return that id to the client so support can trace it without exposing internals.

Covering Spring's own exceptions

@RestControllerAdvice
class Handler extends ResponseEntityExceptionHandler {
  // inherits handling for MethodArgumentNotValidException,
  // HttpMessageNotReadableException, HttpRequestMethodNotSupportedException...
  @Override
  protected ResponseEntity<Object> handleMethodArgumentNotValid(...) { /* customise */ }
}

What @ControllerAdvice does NOT catch

Exceptions thrown in filters (including the Spring Security filter chain) happen before DispatcherServlet, so no advice sees them. Auth errors need AuthenticationEntryPoint / AccessDeniedHandler instead — a genuinely senior distinction, and a common "why isn't my handler firing?" bug.

How to say it in an interview

“@RestControllerAdvice with @ExceptionHandler methods, returning ProblemDetail so the error shape follows RFC 7807. Specific handlers for domain exceptions plus a catch-all that logs the stack trace with a correlation id and returns only that id — never a stack trace. One caveat: it doesn't catch exceptions from the security filter chain, since those fire before the DispatcherServlet; those need an AuthenticationEntryPoint.”

question: What are Spring profiles and how do you use them?
answer:
Profiles are named groups of config and beans that activate conditionally, so one build artifact behaves differently per environment. Two uses:

  • Config — application-dev.yml / application-prod.yml overlay the base application.yml when active.
  • Beans — @Profile("dev") registers a bean only under that profile (an in-memory stub locally, the real client in prod).

Activate with spring.profiles.active, ideally from an env var (SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE=prod) — never hardcoded in the packaged jar.
tip: Hardcoding spring.profiles.active=prod in application.yml defeats the purpose. Supply it from the environment. Also know that profile files overlay — they don't replace — the base file.
deep:
Overlay, not replace

application.yml always loads; application-prod.yml loads on top and only overrides the keys it defines. So common config lives in the base file and each profile file stays small.

Expressions

@Profile("dev")           // active when dev
@Profile("!prod")         // active when prod is NOT active
@Profile({"dev","test"})  // dev OR test
@Profile("prod & aws")     // both (Spring 5.1+)

Profile groups (Boot 2.4+)

spring:
  profiles:
    group:
      prod: [prod-db, prod-mq, metrics]

Activating prod activates all three — much better than a long comma list in a deploy script.

Checking the active profile in code

@Autowired Environment env;
if (env.acceptsProfiles(Profiles.of("prod"))) { ... }

But if (isProd()) branching inside a service is a smell — that's what @Profile on separate bean implementations is for.

Testing

@SpringBootTest
@ActiveProfiles("test")     // loads application-test.yml
class OrderServiceTest { }

Where profiles get abused

Profiles are for environment differences, not feature flags or per-tenant behaviour — profile count explodes and combinations become untestable. Feature flags belong in properties or a flag service. Making that distinction unprompted signals real experience.

How to say it in an interview

“Named groups of config and beans, so the same jar runs everywhere. Profile-specific yml overlays the base file, and @Profile swaps bean implementations — a stub gateway locally, the real one in prod. I activate via SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE from the environment rather than hardcoding it, and I use profile groups to activate related sets. I keep them for environments — feature flags don't belong in profiles.”

question: What does Spring Boot Actuator provide?
answer:
Production-ready HTTP (and JMX) endpoints for operating the app:

  • /actuator/health — aggregated health, with liveness/readiness probes for Kubernetes.
  • /actuator/metrics and /actuator/prometheus — Micrometer metrics for scraping.
  • /actuator/info, /env, /configprops, /beans, /mappings, /loggers (change log levels at runtime, no restart), /threaddump, /heapdump.

Only health is exposed over HTTP by default — you opt the rest in explicitly.
tip: The security point is the real question: /env and /configprops can expose secrets, /heapdump dumps memory. Expose deliberately and protect them — never expose * on a public port.
code:
management:
  endpoints:
    web:
      exposure:
        include: health,info,prometheus   # explicit allow-list, never '*'
  endpoint:
    health:
      show-details: when-authorized
      probes:
        enabled: true                     # /health/liveness, /health/readiness
  server:
    port: 9090                            # separate port, not publicly routed
deep:
Health indicators and aggregation

Boot auto-registers indicators for whatever it detects — DataSource, Redis, disk space, RabbitMQ. Overall status is the worst of them: any DOWN makes the app DOWN. Add your own:

@Component
class PaymentGatewayHealth implements HealthIndicator {
  public Health health() {
    try {
      return gateway.ping()
        ? Health.up().withDetail("latencyMs", 12).build()
        : Health.down().withDetail("reason", "ping failed").build();
    } catch (Exception e) { return Health.down(e).build(); }
  }
}

Liveness vs readiness — get this right

/actuator/health/liveness   -> "am I broken? restart me"
/actuator/health/readiness  -> "can I take traffic right now?"

Putting a DB check in liveness is a classic outage amplifier: the DB blips, Kubernetes restarts every pod, and the restart storm makes recovery worse. A DB dependency belongs in readiness — pull the pod out of the load balancer, don't kill it.

Runtime log level changes

curl -X POST localhost:9090/actuator/loggers/com.example.orders \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"configuredLevel":"DEBUG"}'

Debug a production issue without a redeploy, then set it back. Great practical detail to mention.

Securing it

Common approaches: expose a minimal allow-list; move actuator to a separate management port that isn't publicly routed; and require a role:

http.authorizeHttpRequests(a -> a
  .requestMatchers("/actuator/health/**").permitAll()   // probes need to be open
  .requestMatchers("/actuator/**").hasRole("ADMIN"));

Custom endpoint

@Component
@Endpoint(id = "cache-stats")
class CacheStatsEndpoint {
  @ReadOperation  public Map<String,Object> stats() { ... }
  @DeleteOperation public void evictAll() { ... }
}

How to say it in an interview

“Health, metrics, info, env, loggers, thread and heap dumps — the operational surface. In practice I expose an explicit allow-list rather than *, often on a separate management port, because /env and /heapdump leak secrets. I wire readiness to real dependencies but keep liveness cheap — a DB check in liveness turns a database blip into a cluster-wide restart storm.”

question: What is Spring Data JPA and how does it generate repositories?
answer:
It removes DAO boilerplate: you declare an interface extending JpaRepository<Entity, Id> and Spring creates the implementation at runtime as a proxy. You get CRUD, paging, and sorting for free, plus derived queries parsed from method names (findByEmailAndActiveTrue) and @Query for anything complex.

The hierarchy: Repository → CrudRepository → PagingAndSortingRepository → JpaRepository (adds JPA specifics like flush() and saveAndFlush()).
tip: Method names are parsed and validated at startup — a typo like findByEmial fails the context immediately, not at runtime. That fail-fast property is a genuine selling point.
deep:
How the proxy is built

At startup Spring scans for repository interfaces, and for each method decides: is it a CRUD method from the base interface, does it carry @Query, or should the name be parsed? The name parser strips the prefix (findBy, existsBy, countBy, deleteBy) and maps the rest onto properties and keywords, then builds a JPA Criteria query. An unparseable name fails the context at startup.

Derived query keywords

findByAgeGreaterThanEqual(int age)
findByNameLike / Containing / StartingWith / IgnoreCase
findByCreatedAtBetween(Instant a, Instant b)
findByStatusIn(List<Status> s)
findByDeletedAtIsNull()
findTop10ByOrderByScoreDesc()
findDistinctByCategory(String c)

When to stop using derived queries

findByActiveTrueAndAgeGreaterThanAndCityInOrderByCreatedAtDesc is unreadable. Past ~3 conditions switch to @Query, or to a Specification for dynamic filters:

interface UserRepo extends JpaRepository<User,Long>, JpaSpecificationExecutor<User> {}

Specification<User> spec = Specification.where(null);
if (name != null) spec = spec.and((r,q,cb) -> cb.like(r.get("name"), "%"+name+"%"));
if (active != null) spec = spec.and((r,q,cb) -> cb.equal(r.get("active"), active));
Page<User> page = repo.findAll(spec, pageable);

That's the clean answer to "how do you build a search screen with 6 optional filters?" — the alternative is string-concatenated SQL.

Return types

Optional<User>   // 0 or 1 — preferred over returning null
List<User>       // many
Page<User>       // + total count (extra COUNT query)
Slice<User>      // no count — cheaper for infinite scroll
Stream<User>     // streaming; needs @Transactional + try-with-resources

A non-unique result for a single-valued return throws IncorrectResultSizeDataAccessException.

Custom implementation

Need real logic? Add a fragment interface plus a *Impl class (naming convention matters) and extend both — Spring merges the generated proxy with your code.

How to say it in an interview

“I declare an interface and Spring Data generates the implementation as a proxy at startup — CRUD plus queries derived from method names, validated at startup so typos fail the context rather than a request. For complex queries I use @Query, and for dynamic multi-filter searches I use Specifications with JpaSpecificationExecutor instead of building query strings.”

question: What does @Transactional do, and what's the self-invocation pitfall?
answer:
It declares a transaction boundary: Spring opens a transaction before the method and commits on normal return, or rolls back on a runtime exception. It works via an AOP proxy — the caller talks to a proxy that starts/commits around the real method.

Two consequences everyone must know:

  • Default rollback is unchecked exceptions only. A checked exception commits! Use rollbackFor = Exception.class if you need otherwise.
  • Self-invocation doesn't work. Calling this.otherMethod() bypasses the proxy, so its @Transactional is ignored — silently.

@Transactional on a private or final method is also silently ignored — the proxy can't override it.
tip: Self-invocation is the #1 Spring transaction interview question. The tell: 'my @Transactional method isn't rolling back' — because it was called from inside the same class.
deep:
Why self-invocation fails

caller -> [ OrderService$$Proxy ] -> begin tx -> [ real OrderService ]
                                                      |
                              this.placeOrder(o) -----+  <- stays inside the
                                                         real object; the proxy
                                                         is never crossed

Spring's proxy wraps the bean from outside. Once execution is inside the target object, this is the raw instance, not the proxy — so no interceptor runs. The same reasoning applies to @Async, @Cacheable, and @PreAuthorize.

Three fixes

// 1. BEST — move it to another bean (the proxy is crossed)
@Service class OrderService { 
  private final OrderProcessor processor;
  void bulk(List<Order> os) { os.forEach(processor::placeOrder); }
}

// 2. Self-inject the proxy
@Service class OrderService {
  @Lazy private final OrderService self;      // @Lazy avoids the cycle
  void bulk(List<Order> os) { os.forEach(self::placeOrder); }
}

// 3. AopContext (needs exposeProxy = true — least clean)
((OrderService) AopContext.currentProxy()).placeOrder(o);

The checked-exception rule

@Transactional
void transfer() throws IOException {
  repo.save(x);
  throw new IOException("boom");   // CHECKED -> COMMITS! data saved.
}

@Transactional(rollbackFor = Exception.class)   // now it rolls back

The default (RuntimeException and Error only) is inherited from EJB convention and surprises nearly everyone.

Swallowing exceptions marks the tx rollback-only

@Transactional
void outer() {
  try { inner(); }                 // inner @Transactional threw -> tx marked rollback-only
  catch (Exception e) { log.warn("ignored", e); }
}   // -> UnexpectedRollbackException at commit

Once any participant marks the transaction rollback-only, the commit fails even though you caught the exception. Classic production surprise.

Placement and visibility

Put it on the service layer, not the controller or repository — the service is where a business use case (and its atomic boundary) lives. It must be public: with the default CGLIB proxying, private/final/static methods can't be overridden, so the annotation is silently ignored.

readOnly

@Transactional(readOnly = true)   // Hibernate skips dirty-checking, hints the driver
public List<UserDto> findAll() { ... }

Real benefit: no dirty-check snapshots on the whole result set, and it can route to a read replica.

How to say it in an interview

“It declares a transaction boundary through an AOP proxy — begin before, commit after, rollback on a runtime exception. The two traps: it rolls back on unchecked exceptions only, so a checked exception commits unless I set rollbackFor; and self-invocation bypasses the proxy entirely, so an internal this.method() call gets no transaction. The fix is to move the method to another bean so the proxy is actually crossed.”

question: Explain transaction propagation. When would you use REQUIRES_NEW?
answer:
Propagation decides what happens when a transactional method is called while a transaction is already active:

  • REQUIRED (default) — join the existing one, or start one if none. Everything commits or rolls back together.
  • REQUIRES_NEW — suspend the caller's transaction and run in a genuinely independent one. It commits even if the outer rolls back.
  • NESTED — a savepoint inside the current transaction; can roll back to the savepoint without killing the outer.
  • SUPPORTS / NOT_SUPPORTED / MANDATORY / NEVER — join if present / suspend / require / forbid.

REQUIRES_NEW's canonical use: audit or failure logging that must survive the rollback of the business transaction.
tip: The REQUIRES_NEW answer that lands: 'I want the audit record to persist even when the business transaction rolls back.' Then flag the risk — it holds two DB connections at once and can self-deadlock.
deep:
REQUIRED vs REQUIRES_NEW

REQUIRED:                          REQUIRES_NEW:
outer tx ──────────────┐           outer tx ───┐(suspended)┌────────
   inner joins same tx │              inner: NEW independent tx
both commit/rollback   │              inner commits alone
together ──────────────┘           outer may still roll back

The deadlock risk — the senior-level catch

REQUIRES_NEW holds two connections from the pool simultaneously (the suspended one plus the new one). With a 10-connection Hikari pool and 10 concurrent requests each entering a REQUIRES_NEW, all 10 hold a connection and all 10 wait for an 11th that never comes — pool-exhaustion deadlock. Worse, if the outer transaction locked a row the inner tries to read, the inner blocks on a lock the outer will never release until the inner returns: a real self-deadlock.

NESTED vs REQUIRES_NEW

NESTED        savepoint, SAME tx + SAME connection
              inner rollback -> back to savepoint, outer continues
              outer rollback -> inner is lost too
              (JDBC only; not supported by JPA/Hibernate transaction manager)

REQUIRES_NEW  separate tx + separate connection
              inner commit survives outer rollback, always

"Does the inner work survive the outer rollback?" NESTED: no. REQUIRES_NEW: yes. That's the whole distinction.

Propagation only applies across the proxy

Same trap as always — an internal this.log(...) call ignores REQUIRES_NEW completely and just runs in the caller's transaction. The audit is then rolled back with everything else and the requirement is silently violated. The two beans in the example above are mandatory, not stylistic.

MANDATORY as a design guard

@Transactional(propagation = Propagation.MANDATORY)
void mustJoinCaller() { }   // throws if invoked without an active tx

Useful on internal helpers that must never start their own boundary.

The modern alternative for audit

@TransactionalEventListener(phase = TransactionPhase.AFTER_ROLLBACK)
void onFailed(OrderFailedEvent e) { auditRepo.save(...); }

Often cleaner than REQUIRES_NEW: no suspended transaction, no double connection.

How to say it in an interview

“Propagation defines behaviour when a transaction already exists. REQUIRED joins — the default, everything atomic together. REQUIRES_NEW suspends and runs independently, which I use for audit rows that must survive a business rollback; the catch is it holds two connections at once, so it can exhaust the pool or deadlock against locks the outer transaction holds. NESTED is a savepoint in the same transaction, so it doesn't survive an outer rollback.”

question: What is the N+1 select problem and how do you fix it?
answer:
You load N parents with one query, then touch a lazy association on each — firing one extra query per parent. 100 orders with order.getItems() in a loop = 1 + 100 queries. It passes every test on 5 rows of dev data and destroys production.

Fixes, roughly in order of preference:

  • join fetch in a @Query — load the association in the same query.
  • @EntityGraph — declarative fetch plan on a repository method; composes with derived queries and paging.
  • @BatchSize — collapses N queries into N/batch IN queries; the best option when you also need pagination.
  • Project straight into a DTO so the association is never traversed.
tip: Turn on spring.jpa.show-sql or the datasource-proxy/p6spy log in dev and count queries — 'I assert query counts in tests' is a strong senior answer. Never fix it by switching to EAGER.
deep:
Why EAGER is not the fix

Switching @OneToMany to FetchType.EAGER is the instinct — and it's wrong. It doesn't remove the N+1; it makes it happen on every query that touches the entity, including ones that never needed the association. Worse, it's global and unconditional: you lose the ability to decide per use case. The rule is lazy by default, fetch explicitly where needed. Saying this unprompted is a strong signal.

Note @ManyToOne and @OneToOne default to EAGER — that's a common accidental source of N+1, and worth setting to LAZY explicitly.

The join fetch + pagination trap

@Query("select o from Order o join fetch o.items")
Page<Order> findAll(Pageable p);

// HHH000104: firstResult/maxResults specified with collection fetch;
// applying in memory!

Hibernate cannot paginate a collection join in SQL (the join multiplies rows), so it silently loads the entire table into memory and paginates there. It works in dev and OOMs in prod. This is exactly where @BatchSize is the right tool — page the parents normally, let batching fetch the children in a few IN queries.

MultipleBagFetchException

join fetch on two List collections at once throws it — a cartesian product Hibernate refuses to build. Fixes: make them Set, use @BatchSize, or run two queries against the same persistence context.

Why 'distinct' is needed

A join fetch returns one row per child, so the parent repeats. distinct de-duplicates the object graph in memory (Hibernate 6 does this automatically for entity queries).

DTO projection — often the best answer

@Query("""
  select new com.example.OrderSummary(o.id, o.total, count(i))
  from Order o left join o.items i
  where o.status = :s
  group by o.id, o.total
""")
List<OrderSummary> summaries(@Param("s") Status s);

One query, only the columns you need, no lazy traversal possible, no entities in the persistence context to dirty-check. For read-heavy endpoints this beats fetching entities.

Catching it in CI

Assert query counts in integration tests so a regression fails the build:

// e.g. Hibernate Statistics or the datasource-proxy library
assertThat(statistics.getPrepareStatementCount()).isEqualTo(2);

How to say it in an interview

“One query for the parents, then one per parent when a lazy association is touched — invisible on dev data, fatal in prod. I fix it with join fetch or @EntityGraph, or a DTO projection when it's a read-only view. I don't fix it with EAGER, which just spreads the problem everywhere. And if I need pagination I use @BatchSize, because join fetch with Pageable makes Hibernate paginate in memory — it logs HHH000104 and loads the whole table.”

question: Lazy vs eager loading — and what causes LazyInitializationException?
answer:
Lazy loads an association only when first accessed (via a Hibernate proxy); eager loads it immediately with the parent. Defaults: @OneToMany and @ManyToMany are LAZY; @ManyToOne and @OneToOne are EAGER — which surprises people and quietly causes join explosions.

LazyInitializationException happens when you touch a lazy association after the persistence context closed — typically in the controller or during JSON serialisation, once the @Transactional service method has returned.
tip: The right fix is to fetch what you need inside the transaction (join fetch / EntityGraph) or map to a DTO. The wrong fixes — EAGER everywhere, or open-session-in-view — are what they're testing for.
deep:
Why open-session-in-view is a trap

Boot enables spring.jpa.open-in-view=true by default and even logs a warning about it. It keeps the Hibernate session open for the whole request, so lazy loading in the view/controller "just works" — which is exactly the problem:

  • It hides N+1 problems, moving queries into the serialisation phase where nobody looks for them.
  • It holds a DB connection for the entire request duration, including slow response writing — the pool becomes the bottleneck under load.
  • Queries fire outside any transaction boundary, so you lose atomicity guarantees.

The senior move: set spring.jpa.open-in-view=false, watch the LazyInitializationExceptions appear, and fix each properly by fetching in the service layer. Volunteering this is a genuine differentiator.

Fetch inside the boundary

@Transactional(readOnly = true)
public OrderDto find(Long id) {
  Order o = repo.findWithItems(id).orElseThrow();   // join fetch
  return OrderDto.from(o);                          // map INSIDE the tx
}

Hibernate.initialize and proxies

Order o = repo.getReferenceById(id);   // returns a PROXY, no SELECT yet
o.getId();                             // still no query — id comes from the proxy
o.getTotal();                          // triggers the SELECT now

getReferenceById (formerly getOne) is genuinely useful for setting a FK without loading the row: order.setCustomer(customerRepo.getReferenceById(id)) saves a SELECT.

Serialization landmines

Returning an entity with lazy associations to Jackson causes either a LazyInitializationException or an accidental full graph load. The jackson-datatype-hibernate module patches it — but the real answer is to not serialise entities at all. Bidirectional associations also cause infinite recursion (@JsonManagedReference/@JsonBackReference patch that); DTOs sidestep both.

How to say it in an interview

“Lazy loads on access via a proxy, eager loads with the parent. @OneToMany is lazy but @ManyToOne is eager by default, which I usually override. LazyInitializationException means I touched the association after the session closed. The fix is to fetch what I need inside the transaction with a join fetch or entity graph and map to a DTO there — not to turn on open-in-view, which just hides N+1 and pins a connection for the whole request.”

question: How does pagination work in Spring Data, and Page vs Slice?
answer:
Accept a Pageable in the repository method and Spring Data applies limit/offset and ordering. Return type decides the cost:

  • Page<T> — the content plus a COUNT query for the total, so you can render "page 3 of 47".
  • Slice<T> — content plus a cheap "is there more?" flag (it fetches size+1 rows). No count query — right for infinite scroll.
  • List<T> — just the window, nothing extra.

With @PageableDefault a controller can bind ?page=2&size=20&sort=createdAt,desc automatically.
tip: Page costs an extra COUNT over the whole filtered set — expensive on big tables. If the UI only has a 'load more' button, Slice is strictly better. And always cap the page size.
deep:
Cap the page size — it's an availability bug

spring:
  data:
    web:
      pageable:
        max-page-size: 100
        default-page-size: 20

Without a cap, ?size=1000000 is a free denial-of-service: one request loads the whole table into heap. Interviewers like this being raised unprompted.

Deep pagination is O(n)

SELECT * FROM orders ORDER BY created_at LIMIT 20 OFFSET 500000;

The database must walk and discard 500,000 rows before returning 20. Page 1 is instant, page 25,000 times out. Keyset (seek) pagination is the fix:

-- instead of OFFSET, remember the last row and seek past it
SELECT * FROM orders
WHERE (created_at, id) < (:lastCreatedAt, :lastId)
ORDER BY created_at DESC, id DESC
LIMIT 20;

Constant time on any page, because the index seeks straight to the position. Trade-off: no random page jumps, only next/previous. Spring Data 3.1+ supports it natively via ScrollPosition:

Window<User> window = repo.findByActiveTrue(
    ScrollPosition.keyset(), PageRequest.ofSize(20));

Unstable sort = duplicate rows across pages

Sorting by a non-unique column (created_at) leaves ties in database-defined order, which can differ between the two queries — so a row appears on both page 1 and page 2, and another is never shown. Always append a unique tiebreaker:

Sort.by("createdAt").descending().and(Sort.by("id").descending());

This is a subtle real-world bug that few candidates know; it lands very well.

Pagination + join fetch don't mix

Covered under N+1: a collection join fetch with Pageable makes Hibernate paginate in memory (HHH000104). Use @BatchSize or a two-query approach — page the IDs, then fetch the graph for those IDs.

How to say it in an interview

“Pass a Pageable and return Page when the UI needs a total — that costs an extra COUNT — or Slice for infinite scroll, which just fetches size+1 and skips the count. Things I watch for: cap max page size or ?size=1000000 is a DoS; always add a unique tiebreaker to the sort or rows duplicate across pages; and for deep pagination use keyset instead of OFFSET, since OFFSET makes the DB walk and discard every skipped row.”

question: Optimistic vs pessimistic locking — how do you prevent lost updates?
answer:
The lost-update problem: two transactions read the same row, both modify it, the second write silently overwrites the first.

  • Optimistic locking — add a @Version column. Each update includes WHERE version = ? and bumps it; if the row was changed meanwhile, 0 rows match and Hibernate throws OptimisticLockException. No DB locks, great throughput, but the loser must retry.
  • Pessimistic locking — SELECT ... FOR UPDATE takes a real DB lock so others block until you commit. No retries, but it serialises access and risks deadlock and lock-wait timeouts.

Default to optimistic; use pessimistic only for short, hot, high-contention critical sections (inventory decrement, seat booking).
tip: The decision rule: optimistic when conflicts are rare (usual case), pessimistic when they're common and retrying is expensive. Adding @Version is nearly free — it should be on any entity that gets concurrent edits.
deep:
The lost update, concretely

T1: read balance=100          T2: read balance=100
T1: set balance=100-30=70
                              T2: set balance=100-50=50
T1: commit -> 70
                              T2: commit -> 50   <- T1's withdrawal vanished

With @Version, T2's update carries WHERE version=0, but T1 already moved it to 1 — 0 rows match, T2 throws instead of corrupting the balance.

Retrying an optimistic failure

Optimistic locking converts corruption into an exception — you still have to handle it:

@Retryable(retryFor = OptimisticLockingFailureException.class,
           maxAttempts = 3, backoff = @Backoff(delay = 50, multiplier = 2))
@Transactional
public void withdraw(Long id, BigDecimal amt) {
  Account a = repo.findById(id).orElseThrow();
  a.setBalance(a.getBalance().subtract(amt));
}

Critical detail: the retry must re-read the entity in a new transaction. Retrying inside the same transaction with the stale object just fails again forever. That's why @Retryable goes on the outside of @Transactional, so each attempt is a fresh transaction.

Lock modes

OPTIMISTIC                 check version at commit
OPTIMISTIC_FORCE_INCREMENT bump version even on a pure read
                           (use when a child change must invalidate the parent)
PESSIMISTIC_READ           shared lock — others read, nobody writes
PESSIMISTIC_WRITE          exclusive — SELECT ... FOR UPDATE
PESSIMISTIC_FORCE_INCREMENT lock + version bump

Pessimistic risks

@Lock(LockModeType.PESSIMISTIC_WRITE)
@QueryHints(@QueryHint(name = "jakarta.persistence.lock.timeout", value = "3000"))
Optional<Seat> findByIdForUpdate(Long id);

Always set a lock timeout — without one a stuck transaction blocks everyone indefinitely. And lock rows in a consistent order everywhere: T1 locking A then B while T2 locks B then A is a textbook deadlock. Keep the locked section short and never make a network call while holding a row lock.

Optimistic locking across HTTP

@Version only protects a single transaction. For a user editing a form over minutes, send the version to the client (as an ETag) and require it back with If-Match — a stale version returns 409 Conflict. That's optimistic locking extended to the API layer, and it's a strong senior answer.

How to say it in an interview

“Optimistic uses a @Version column — the update is conditional on the version, so a concurrent change fails with an exception instead of silently overwriting. It's my default because it takes no locks; the cost is handling the retry in a fresh transaction. Pessimistic is SELECT FOR UPDATE — real locks, no retries, but it serialises access and can deadlock, so I keep it to short high-contention sections and always set a lock timeout.”

question: What is the persistence context, and why does an entity update without calling save()?
answer:
The persistence context (Hibernate's Session / JPA's EntityManager) is a first-level cache and unit of work scoped to the transaction. Every entity you load or persist becomes managed — Hibernate keeps a snapshot of its loaded state.

At flush time (before a query, or at commit) it compares each managed entity against its snapshot — dirty checking — and auto-generates UPDATEs for whatever changed. So inside a transaction, mutating a loaded entity persists without any save() call. Calling save() on an already-managed entity is a no-op.

Entity states: transient (new, unknown) → managed (tracked) → detached (context closed) → removed.
tip: 'Why did my entity update when I never called save?' is a favourite. Dirty checking. The flip side is scarier: an accidental setter on a managed entity silently writes to the DB.
deep:
First-level cache

@Transactional
void demo(Long id) {
  User a = repo.findById(id).orElseThrow();  // SELECT
  User b = repo.findById(id).orElseThrow();  // NO SELECT — served from context
  assert a == b;                             // same instance, guaranteed
}

Identity is guaranteed within a persistence context: one row = one object. It's not a cache you configure — it's always on and scoped to the transaction.

Dirty checking has a cost

At flush, Hibernate diffs every managed entity field by field against its snapshot. Load 10,000 entities in one transaction and each flush walks all 10,000 — a real, measurable cost. This is exactly why @Transactional(readOnly = true) matters for reads: Hibernate sets FlushMode.MANUAL and skips snapshots entirely.

The flush-before-query surprise

@Transactional
void demo() {
  user.setStatus(BANNED);              // dirty, not yet flushed
  var count = repo.countByStatus(BANNED);  // triggers auto-flush FIRST
  // -> the UPDATE runs before the count, so the count includes this user
}

Hibernate auto-flushes before a query that might touch dirty state, so queries see your uncommitted changes. Surprising when you expect flush only at commit.

save() vs saveAndFlush() vs merge()

save(new User(...))       // transient -> persist(), INSERT at flush
save(managedUser)         // no-op — dirty checking handles it anyway
save(detachedUser)        // merge() — SELECT then UPDATE, returns a COPY
saveAndFlush(u)           // forces SQL now (e.g. to read a generated id)

merge on a detached entity returns a new managed instance — your original stays detached. Ignoring the return value is a classic bug:

repo.save(detached);            // WRONG — changes to `detached` are lost
User managed = repo.save(detached);   // right — use the returned instance

The detached-null-field disaster

merge copies all fields. Take a DTO from a request, map it to an entity with only the fields the form had, and merge it — every field the DTO didn't populate is overwritten with null. Always load the entity and copy the changed fields onto it inside the transaction.

Batch inserts need clearing

for (int i = 0; i < 100_000; i++) {
  em.persist(new User(...));
  if (i % 50 == 0) { em.flush(); em.clear(); }   // or you OOM
}

Without clear() all 100k entities stay managed, memory grows, and every flush dirty-checks the whole set — quadratic slowdown.

How to say it in an interview

“It's the transaction-scoped first-level cache and unit of work. Loaded entities are managed with a snapshot, and at flush Hibernate dirty-checks them and generates UPDATEs — so mutating a loaded entity inside a transaction persists without save(). That's also why readOnly = true helps: it skips snapshots and dirty-checking. And for bulk work I flush and clear periodically, otherwise every flush re-checks every entity and memory blows up.”

question: @Query — JPQL vs native SQL, and how do you avoid SQL injection?
answer:
@Query writes the query yourself when derived method names run out. JPQL (default) queries entities and fields, is database-portable, and is validated at startup. Native (nativeQuery = true) is raw SQL against tables — needed for vendor features (window functions, CTEs, ON CONFLICT) at the cost of portability and startup validation.

Injection safety: always use named parameters (:email) or positional (?1). These become JDBC bind parameters, which the driver never interprets as SQL. Concatenating user input into a query string is the vulnerability.
tip: Parameter binding — not escaping — is the answer to SQL injection. And note SpEL in a @Query (:#{...}) is NOT parameterised the same way; that's the one place @Query can still be injectable.
deep:
JPQL is validated at startup, native is not

A JPQL typo (u.emial) fails the context immediately. The same typo in native SQL compiles fine and fails at request time in production. That alone is a good reason to prefer JPQL where possible.

@Modifying — the two flags that matter

@Modifying(clearAutomatically = true, flushAutomatically = true)

A bulk update/delete goes straight to the database and bypasses the persistence context. Without these flags, entities already loaded in the context keep their stale values and can even be written back over your bulk update by dirty checking. flushAutomatically pushes pending changes before the bulk statement; clearAutomatically evicts the now-stale entities after. Knowing why these exist is a strong signal.

Bulk operations also skip cascades and lifecycle callbacks — @PreRemove never fires on a bulk delete.

Where injection can still happen

// SAFE — bound parameter
@Query("select u from User u where u.name = :name")

// INJECTABLE — SpEL interpolates into the query string
@Query("select u from User u where u.name = :#{#name}")

// INJECTABLE — dynamic sort column concatenated by hand
String sql = "select * from users order by " + userSuppliedColumn;

Bind parameters can't parameterise identifiers (table/column names), so dynamic sorting must be validated against an allow-list — never passed through. That's the nuance behind "how do you prevent SQL injection" beyond the textbook answer.

Projections

// interface projection — Spring Data generates the proxy, selects only these columns
interface UserSummary { String getName(); String getEmail(); }
List<UserSummary> findByActiveTrue();

// class/DTO projection
@Query("select new com.example.UserDto(u.name, u.email) from User u")
List<UserDto> summaries();

Interface projections are the lightest way to avoid selecting a 40-column entity for a list view.

Native query return mapping

A native query returning an entity's table maps automatically. For arbitrary columns you get Object[] unless you use an interface projection or @SqlResultSetMapping — interface projections work with native queries too and are much cleaner.

How to say it in an interview

“JPQL by default — it's over entities, portable, and validated at startup, so typos fail the context. Native when I need vendor features like window functions or upserts. Either way parameters are bound with :name, which the driver sends as bind variables, so injection isn't possible — the exception is anything concatenated, like a dynamic sort column, which has to be allow-listed. And for bulk updates I set clearAutomatically, since they bypass the persistence context and leave stale entities behind.”

question: How do you manage database schema changes? Why not hibernate ddl-auto?
answer:
With a migration tool — Flyway (versioned SQL files) or Liquibase (XML/YAML/JSON changelogs, DB-agnostic). Boot auto-runs them at startup before Hibernate initialises. Each migration is versioned, checksummed, and recorded in a history table so it applies exactly once, in order, identically in every environment.

spring.jpa.hibernate.ddl-auto=update must never be used in production: it's non-deterministic, it never drops or renames anything, it can't do data migrations, it has no rollback, and it silently diverges between environments. The schema is production state — it needs version control and review, not inference from entity classes.
tip: Say 'ddl-auto=validate in prod' — it fails startup if entities and schema disagree, which catches a missed migration at deploy time instead of at runtime. Use none/validate; never update.
code:
# src/main/resources/db/migration/V1__create_users.sql
# V2__add_email_index.sql   (immutable once applied — checksummed)

spring:
  flyway:
    enabled: true
    baseline-on-migrate: true      # adopting an existing DB
  jpa:
    hibernate:
      ddl-auto: validate           # prod: verify only, never modify

---
spring:
  config:
    activate:
      on-profile: test
  jpa:
    hibernate:
      ddl-auto: create-drop        # throwaway test DB only
deep:
ddl-auto values

none         do nothing                         (prod, with migrations)
validate     compare entities vs schema, fail on mismatch  (prod — best)
update       try to patch the schema            (NEVER in prod)
create       drop + create at startup           (tests)
create-drop  create at startup, drop at shutdown (tests)

Boot defaults to create-drop for embedded DBs and none otherwise — so a real DB won't be touched implicitly.

Why 'update' fails in practice

  • It only adds. Removed a field? The column stays forever. Renamed one? You get a new column and the old data stranded in the old one.
  • No data migrations — you can't backfill or transform.
  • No control over how: no CONCURRENTLY on an index, so it can lock a large table for minutes.
  • Nothing is reviewable. A schema change slips into a PR invisibly, inside an entity diff.
  • Different Hibernate versions can infer different DDL, so environments drift.

Migrations are immutable

Flyway checksums every applied file. Editing V2__...sql after it ran anywhere makes Flyway abort with a checksum mismatch — deliberately. Fix forward with V3. (flyway repair exists for genuine emergencies, not for routine edits.)

Zero-downtime migrations — the senior topic

During a rolling deploy, old and new code run simultaneously against one schema. So every migration must be backward compatible with the currently deployed version. Renaming a column in one step breaks every old pod instantly. Use expand/contract:

Release 1 (expand):  add new column, nullable; write to BOTH columns
Release 2 (migrate): backfill old rows; read from the new column
Release 3 (contract): stop writing the old column
Release 4:           drop the old column

Four deploys to rename a column — and it's the correct answer. Being able to explain expand/contract is a strong differentiator at 5+ years.

Locking

Flyway takes a lock so concurrent instances don't race — important in Kubernetes where 5 replicas start at once. But long migrations on a big table still block: ALTER TABLE can take an exclusive lock while every request queues behind it. Big data migrations belong in a separate job, not in app startup.

How to say it in an interview

“Flyway with versioned SQL in db/migration, run at startup, checksummed and recorded so each applies once. Prod runs ddl-auto: validate so a missed migration fails the deploy rather than a request. I don't use update — it only ever adds, can't rename or backfill, isn't reviewable, and drifts between environments. And with rolling deploys migrations have to be backward compatible, so a rename is expand/contract across several releases.”

question: How does the connection pool work, and how do you size it?
answer:
Boot uses HikariCP by default. Opening a TCP connection and authenticating to a DB costs milliseconds; a pool keeps a set of live connections and hands them out, so a request borrows one and returns it at transaction end.

Sizing is counter-intuitive: small pools outperform large ones. A DB has a finite number of cores and disks; past that, more concurrent connections just add context-switching and lock contention while each query gets slower. HikariCP's own guidance is roughly connections = ((core_count * 2) + effective_spindle_count) — often just 10–20, even for high traffic.

The other critical knob is connection-timeout: how long a thread waits for a connection before failing fast rather than hanging.
