tip: 'We had timeouts so we raised the pool to 200' is the trap answer — it usually makes things worse. Pool exhaustion is nearly always a slow-query or leak problem, not a size problem.
code:
spring:
  datasource:
    hikari:
      maximum-pool-size: 15        # small on purpose
      minimum-idle: 15             # = max: avoid churn in steady state
      connection-timeout: 3000     # fail fast, don't hang the thread
      idle-timeout: 600000
      max-lifetime: 1800000        # < DB/proxy idle kill time!
      leak-detection-threshold: 20000   # log a stack trace for held connections
      validation-timeout: 3000
deep:
Why more connections is slower

An 8-core DB can genuinely execute ~8 things at once. 200 connections don't create parallelism — they create 200 queries time-slicing across 8 cores, each 25x slower, plus more lock contention and more memory per connection. Throughput goes down and latency goes up. The classic demonstration: an Oracle benchmark where dropping from 2048 to 96 connections cut response time from 100ms to 2ms.

Pool exhaustion: find the real cause

HikariPool-1 - Connection is not available, request timed out after 30000ms

This nearly always means connections are being held too long, not that the pool is too small. Usual suspects:

  • open-in-view=true pinning a connection for the whole request, including response serialisation.
  • An HTTP call inside a @Transactional method — you're holding a DB connection for the duration of someone else's outage. Never do I/O inside a transaction.
  • REQUIRES_NEW holding two connections per request.
  • A slow/unindexed query.
  • A leak — leak-detection-threshold prints the stack trace of the borrower.

max-lifetime must be shorter than the DB's timeout

If MySQL's wait_timeout or a cloud proxy/firewall kills idle connections at 30 minutes and Hikari's max-lifetime is 30 minutes or more, the pool will eventually hand out a dead socket and you get random, unreproducible connection-reset errors under low traffic. Keep max-lifetime comfortably below it (e.g. 30s less). This is a real, frequently-missed production bug.

Sizing against DB limits

pods x maximum-pool-size  <=  DB max_connections (minus headroom)
20 pods x 20 = 400 connections vs Postgres default 100 -> outage on scale-up

The pool is per JVM. Autoscaling multiplies it. Count total connections across all replicas — and remember batch jobs and migrations need some too.

Thread pool vs connection pool

Tomcat's 200 threads and Hikari's 15 connections is a deliberate, healthy shape: only 15 requests can touch the DB at once, the rest queue briefly. The queue is a feature — it protects the database from the stampede. Matching them 1:1 pushes the overload onto the DB, which is the one thing you can't easily scale.

How to say it in an interview

“HikariCP by default. Sizing is counter-intuitive — small pools are faster, because the DB only has so many cores; 10–20 is normal even under load, and raising it usually makes latency worse. When I see pool timeouts I look for connections held too long: open-in-view, an HTTP call inside a transaction, or a slow query — leak detection prints the culprit's stack trace. I also keep max-lifetime below the database's idle timeout, or the pool hands out dead sockets.”

question: How does Spring's caching abstraction work?
answer:
@EnableCaching plus annotations, backed by a pluggable CacheManager (Caffeine, Redis, Hazelcast, or a simple ConcurrentHashMap). It's proxy-based, like @Transactional.

  • @Cacheable — check the cache first; on a miss, run the method and store the result.
  • @CachePut — always run the method and update the cache (for writes).
  • @CacheEvict — remove an entry (or everything with allEntries=true).
  • @Caching — combine several.

The abstraction means you switch from Caffeine (local) to Redis (distributed) by changing a dependency and config, not code.
tip: Always set a TTL. A cache without expiry is a memory leak plus a permanent stale-data bug. And remember @Cacheable is proxy-based, so a self-invocation isn't cached at all.
deep:
Local vs distributed

Caffeine  in-JVM, nanosecond reads, no network
          BUT: each pod has its own copy -> evicting on pod A leaves
          pods B..E serving stale data. Fine for reference data with a TTL.

Redis     shared, consistent across pods, survives restarts
          BUT: network hop (~1ms), serialization cost, another thing to run

The stale-across-pods problem is the key trade-off. With a local cache and 5 replicas, an evict only clears one — the others stay wrong until their TTL expires.

TTL config

spring:
  cache:
    type: redis
    redis:
      time-to-live: 600000        # 10 min
      cache-null-values: false
@Bean
CaffeineCacheManager cacheManager() {
  var cm = new CaffeineCacheManager("products");
  cm.setCaffeine(Caffeine.newBuilder()
      .maximumSize(10_000)               // bound it — or it's a memory leak
      .expireAfterWrite(Duration.ofMinutes(10))
      .recordStats());
  return cm;
}

Key generation — the silent bug

The default key is all parameters combined. With no params, the key is constant — so a no-arg @Cacheable method returns the same value forever. And two methods sharing a cache name with the same params collide:

@Cacheable("data") Product findProduct(Long id);
@Cacheable("data") User    findUser(Long id);
// findUser(1) can return a Product -> ClassCastException

Always be explicit: separate cache names, or key = "'user:' + #id".

condition vs unless

@Cacheable(value="p", condition="#id > 100", unless="#result?.price == null")

condition is evaluated before the call (skip the cache entirely); unless is evaluated after, on the result (don't store this). Caching nulls is usually wrong — but not always: deliberately caching a null briefly is how you defend against cache penetration, where requests for a non-existent key hammer the DB.

Cache stampede

A hot key expires and 500 concurrent requests all miss and all hit the DB at once. sync = true makes one thread compute while the others wait — but only within one JVM. Distributed stampedes need a Redis lock or probabilistic early refresh.

Caching + transactions

@CacheEvict runs when the method returns — which may be before the transaction commits. Another thread can then re-populate the cache with the old value, and it stays stale after your commit. beforeInvocation=false doesn't help; the correct fix is to evict after commit via a @TransactionalEventListener(phase = AFTER_COMMIT). This is a subtle bug that reads as very senior.

How to say it in an interview

“@EnableCaching and the annotations over a pluggable CacheManager, so Caffeine locally or Redis when it has to be shared. Things I'm careful about: always bound size and TTL, be explicit with keys because two methods sharing a cache name will collide, and remember it's proxy-based so self-calls aren't cached. The subtle one is evicting inside a transaction — the evict fires before the commit, so another thread can re-cache the old value; I evict on an after-commit event instead.”

question: How does Spring Security work? Explain the filter chain.
answer:
Spring Security is a chain of servlet filters that runs before the DispatcherServlet. A single DelegatingFilterProxy hands off to the FilterChainProxy, which picks a matching SecurityFilterChain and runs its ordered filters.

The key ones: SecurityContextPersistenceFilter (restore the context), UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter (form login), BearerTokenAuthenticationFilter (JWT), ExceptionTranslationFilter (turn auth failures into 401/403), and AuthorizationFilter (enforce access rules) last.

Authentication succeeds → an Authentication lands in the SecurityContextHolder (a ThreadLocal) → authorization checks read it.
tip: 'It's filters, before the DispatcherServlet' is the whole insight — it's why @ControllerAdvice can't catch auth exceptions and why security applies to every request, not just controllers.
deep:
Request flow

request
  -> DelegatingFilterProxy
     -> FilterChainProxy -> picks the first matching SecurityFilterChain
        -> SecurityContextPersistenceFilter   (load context)
        -> ...AuthenticationFilter            (authenticate)
        -> ExceptionTranslationFilter         (catch auth errors -> 401/403)
        -> AuthorizationFilter                (enforce rules)
  -> DispatcherServlet -> your @RestController

Rule order matters — first match wins

// WRONG — anyRequest() matches everything, admin rule is dead code
.anyRequest().authenticated()
.requestMatchers("/api/admin/**").hasRole("ADMIN")

// RIGHT — specific first, anyRequest() last
.requestMatchers("/api/admin/**").hasRole("ADMIN")
.anyRequest().authenticated()

Spring Boot 3 actually throws at startup for unreachable rules — but the principle (specific → general, deny-by-default at the end) still gets asked.

hasRole vs hasAuthority

hasRole("ADMIN")        -> checks for the authority "ROLE_ADMIN"  (prefix added!)
hasAuthority("ADMIN")   -> checks for exactly "ADMIN"

The invisible ROLE_ prefix is a classic source of "my role check always fails" — especially when JWT claims contain bare names like ADMIN.

Why @ControllerAdvice can't catch auth errors

Filters run before the DispatcherServlet, so an authentication failure never reaches Spring MVC's exception handling. You customise it with the security hooks instead:

http.exceptionHandling(e -> e
  .authenticationEntryPoint((req, res, ex) ->     // 401 — not authenticated
      res.sendError(401, "Unauthorized"))
  .accessDeniedHandler((req, res, ex) ->         // 403 — authenticated, not allowed
      res.sendError(403, "Forbidden")));

401 vs 403

401 = "I don't know who you are" (missing/invalid credentials). 403 = "I know who you are and you still can't". Returning 403 for an expired token is a common bug — the client can't tell it should refresh.

SecurityContextHolder is a ThreadLocal

var auth = SecurityContextHolder.getContext().getAuthentication();

Because it's thread-bound, it is empty inside @Async methods and new threads by default. Fix with DelegatingSecurityContextExecutor or MODE_INHERITABLETHREADLOCAL. This catches almost everyone once.

Adding a custom filter

http.addFilterBefore(new JwtAuthFilter(), UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter.class);

How to say it in an interview

“It's a servlet filter chain in front of the DispatcherServlet — authentication filters populate an Authentication in the SecurityContextHolder, and the authorization filter at the end enforces the rules. Because it's filters, auth exceptions never reach @ControllerAdvice — you need an AuthenticationEntryPoint. Two gotchas: rules are first-match so anyRequest() goes last, and hasRole silently prepends ROLE_.”

question: How does JWT authentication work, and what are its trade-offs?
answer:
On login the server verifies credentials and returns a signed JWT (header.payload.signature). The client sends it as Authorization: Bearer <token>; a filter validates the signature and expiry and populates the SecurityContext. Because the token is self-contained and signed, the server stores no session — any instance can validate it, which is why it suits horizontally-scaled and multi-service systems.

The trade-off that defines JWT: you cannot revoke one. It's valid until it expires. Logout, a ban, or a permission change won't stop a token already in the wild. The standard mitigation is short-lived access tokens (5–15 min) plus a long-lived, revocable refresh token stored server-side.
tip: The two things that separate real experience: (1) JWTs are signed, NOT encrypted — anyone can base64-decode the payload, so never put secrets in it; (2) revocation is the hard part.
deep:
Anatomy

eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiJ9  .  eyJzdWIiOiIxMjMiLCJleHAiOjE3MDB9  .  <signature>
     header (alg)              payload (claims)                 signature
     base64url                 base64url — READABLE BY ANYONE

The payload is encoded, not encrypted. Paste any JWT into jwt.io and read it. Putting a PII field or an internal flag in there is a data leak. Signing only guarantees integrity — that nobody changed it.

The revocation problem

Sessions are revocable by definition (delete the row). JWTs aren't — that's the price of statelessness. Options:

short expiry (5-15 min)   -> small blast radius; the usual answer
refresh token in DB       -> revocable; access tokens die quickly
blocklist of jti in Redis -> works, but you're now stateful again
token version claim       -> bump a user's version to invalidate all their tokens
                             (still needs a lookup, but it's a cheap one)

Being honest that a blocklist reintroduces the state JWT was chosen to avoid is exactly the nuance interviewers want.

Where to store it on the client

localStorage      readable by ANY JS -> XSS steals the token
JS-accessible cookie   same problem
httpOnly cookie   XSS-safe, but needs CSRF protection (SameSite=Strict/Lax)

The honest answer: httpOnly + Secure + SameSite cookie, and accept the CSRF work. "localStorage because it's easier" is a red flag.

The alg=none attack

Historically, libraries accepted a token whose header said {"alg":"none"} and skipped verification — trivial forgery. A related attack downgrades RS256 to HS256 and signs with the public key as the HMAC secret. Never trust the token's own header for the algorithm; pin it in the decoder. This is why using Spring's resource server rather than hand-rolling validation is the safe call.

Validate more than the signature

exp   expiry            iss  issuer — is it MY identity provider?
nbf   not before        aud  audience — was it minted for THIS service?

Skipping aud means a valid token for a different service is accepted by yours.

JWT vs sessions — pick honestly

For a single monolith with a load balancer, a plain server session (or Spring Session in Redis) is simpler, instantly revocable, and keeps no secrets client-side. JWT earns its complexity with multiple services or third-party clients. "Always use JWT" is cargo-culting; saying so is a plus.

How to say it in an interview

“Signed self-contained token in an Authorization header, validated per request by a filter, so no server session and any instance can serve any request. The catch is revocation — a JWT is valid until it expires, so I use short-lived access tokens with a revocable refresh token server-side. And the payload is only base64, not encrypted, so nothing sensitive goes in the claims. I validate signature, expiry, issuer and audience — and I use Spring's resource server rather than hand-rolling it, because things like the alg=none downgrade are easy to get wrong.”

question: How do you do method-level authorization?
answer:
@EnableMethodSecurity, then annotate methods:

  • @PreAuthorize — a SpEL check before the call; has access to the arguments and the authentication.
  • @PostAuthorize — checks after, against returnObject (for "you may only read your own record" checks that need the loaded object).
  • @PreFilter / @PostFilter — strip elements from a collection argument/return.
  • @Secured / @RolesAllowed — simple role checks, no SpEL.

This complements URL rules rather than replacing them: URL security is coarse and can be bypassed if another entry point reaches the service, whereas method security sits next to the business rule.
tip: @PostAuthorize runs the method first — so a mutation still happened before the check failed (though the tx rolls back). @PostFilter loads the whole collection then discards rows — never use it for paging.
deep:
Delegating to a bean — the scalable pattern

SpEL strings get unreadable and untestable fast. Reference a bean with @ and keep the logic in plain, unit-testable Java:

@Component("docPermissions")
public class DocumentPermissions {
  private final DocumentRepo repo;
  public boolean canEdit(Long docId, Authentication auth) {
    return repo.findById(docId)
        .map(d -> d.getOwnerId().equals(auth.getName())
               || auth.getAuthorities().contains(new SimpleGrantedAuthority("ROLE_ADMIN")))
        .orElse(false);
  }
}

@PreAuthorize("@docPermissions.canEdit(#id, authentication)")
public void edit(Long id) { }

@PostAuthorize's cost

@PostAuthorize("returnObject.owner == authentication.name")
@Transactional
public Document delete(Long id) {   // DELETE ALREADY RAN before the check
  ...
}

The method executes first. Inside a transaction the rollback saves you; outside one (or with side effects like an email or a queue publish) the damage is done. Use @PostAuthorize for reads, @PreAuthorize for writes.

@PostFilter doesn't scale

@PostFilter("filterObject.owner == authentication.name")
public List<Doc> findAll() { return repo.findAll(); }   // loads 1M rows, keeps 3

Filtering in memory after the query is fine for small lists and catastrophic for large ones — and it completely breaks pagination (you'd return 2 of 20 requested). Filter in the query (where owner = :me) instead. Recognising this is a good senior signal.

Still proxy-based

Same rule as @Transactional: an internal self-call bypasses the check entirely. A "secured" method called from another method of the same class runs unsecured — a genuine vulnerability, not just a bug.

Ordering with transactions

By default method security runs inside the transaction advisor's interception in some setups; if you need the authorization check to happen before the transaction opens, set the interceptor order explicitly. Worth knowing when a @PostAuthorize failure leaves a transaction to roll back.

How to say it in an interview

“@EnableMethodSecurity plus @PreAuthorize with SpEL — I can reference method arguments and the authentication, and for anything non-trivial I delegate to a bean with @perms.canEdit(#id, authentication) so the rule is testable Java. I avoid @PostAuthorize on writes since the method already ran, and @PostFilter on big collections since it filters in memory and breaks paging — that belongs in the query.”

question: How should passwords be stored?
answer:
Hashed with a slow, salted, adaptive algorithm — BCrypt, Argon2, or SCrypt — never encrypted, never plain, and never a fast hash like MD5/SHA-256.

The reasoning: general-purpose hashes are designed to be fast, which is exactly wrong here — a GPU does billions of SHA-256/sec, so a leaked table is cracked in hours. BCrypt is deliberately slow and has a work factor you raise as hardware improves. It also salts every password automatically, so identical passwords produce different hashes and precomputed rainbow tables are useless.

In Spring, use DelegatingPasswordEncoder (the default from PasswordEncoderFactories) — it prefixes the hash with its algorithm id so you can migrate algorithms later without a big-bang reset.
tip: Encryption is reversible — that's why it's wrong for passwords; you never need the original. And the salt is stored inside the BCrypt hash itself, which is why there's no separate salt column.
deep:
Why not SHA-256?

SHA-256   ~10,000,000,000 guesses/sec on a GPU rig
BCrypt(10)        ~10,000 guesses/sec

Six orders of magnitude. That's the entire point: the login path only needs ~100ms, but the attacker needs the same 100ms per guess, across billions of guesses.

The salt lives in the hash

$2a$10$N9qo8uLOickgx2ZMRZoMye  IjZAgcfl7p92ldGxad68LJZdL17lhWy
 ^   ^  ^                        ^
 alg cost salt (22 chars)        hash

So verification re-extracts the salt from the stored string — no separate column, and no "where do I keep the salt?" problem. Candidates who propose a salt column usually haven't used BCrypt.

Work factor

Cost 10 is Spring's default, ~100ms. Each +1 doubles the time. Tune so login takes 200–500ms on your hardware — slow enough to punish attackers, fast enough for users. But note the DoS angle: an unthrottled login endpoint with cost 14 lets an attacker burn your CPU with garbage passwords, so rate-limit it.

BCrypt's 72-byte truncation

BCrypt silently ignores everything past 72 bytes — a genuinely obscure detail that matters if you pre-hash or allow passphrases. Argon2id avoids it and is the modern recommendation (it's memory-hard too, which blunts GPU/ASIC attacks).

Migrating algorithms

The {id} prefix means old and new hashes coexist. On a successful login, re-encode with the stronger encoder and save:

if (encoder.upgradeEncoding(user.getHash())) {
  user.setHash(encoder.encode(rawPassword));   // only time you have the raw password
  repo.save(user);
}

Users migrate transparently as they log in. Naming this pattern is a strong answer.

Timing attacks on the user lookup

// leaks whether the email exists — fast "user not found" vs slow bcrypt compare
var user = repo.findByEmail(email).orElseThrow(() -> new BadCredentials());
return encoder.matches(raw, user.getHash());

Run the encoder against a dummy hash even when the user doesn't exist, so both paths take the same time — and always return the same generic "bad credentials" message rather than "no such user".

How to say it in an interview

“Hashed with BCrypt or Argon2 — never encrypted, never MD5 or SHA, because those are fast by design and a GPU does billions a second. BCrypt is deliberately slow with a tunable work factor and salts each password itself, so the salt lives inside the hash string. I use Spring's DelegatingPasswordEncoder so hashes carry an algorithm prefix and I can upgrade users to a stronger encoder transparently on their next login.”

question: How do you enable CORS, and what problem does it actually solve?
answer:
CORS is a browser mechanism that relaxes the same-origin policy. Your Angular app on localhost:4200 calling an API on localhost:8080 is a cross-origin request; the browser blocks reading the response unless the server returns Access-Control-Allow-Origin naming that origin.

Configure globally via WebMvcConfigurer (or CorsConfigurationSource when Spring Security is present — security must know about it too), or per-controller with @CrossOrigin.

Crucially: CORS is not a server-side security control. It protects users' browsers from other sites reading your responses with their cookies. It stops nothing from curl or a server.
tip: The two things to know: allowedOrigins("*") + allowCredentials(true) is rejected by the browser — use allowedOriginPatterns. And with Spring Security you must register CORS in the security chain or the preflight is 401'd before your MVC config runs.
deep:
Preflight

For anything beyond a "simple" request (non-GET/POST, custom headers like Authorization, JSON content type), the browser first sends an OPTIONS preflight:

OPTIONS /api/users
Origin: https://app.example.com
Access-Control-Request-Method: POST
Access-Control-Request-Headers: authorization, content-type

200 OK
Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://app.example.com
Access-Control-Allow-Methods: GET, POST, PUT, DELETE
Access-Control-Allow-Headers: authorization, content-type
Access-Control-Max-Age: 3600

Only then does the real request go out. maxAge caches that permission so you're not doubling every request — a genuine latency win people forget.

The Security ordering trap

The preflight OPTIONS carries no Authorization header (by design). If Spring Security sees it before CORS is applied, it 401s it — and the browser reports a confusing CORS error rather than an auth error. http.cors() installs the CorsFilter early enough to answer preflights before authentication. This is the classic "CORS works until I add Spring Security" bug.

Wildcard + credentials is illegal

Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *
Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true
-> browser rejects it outright

Otherwise any site could make authenticated requests with your cookies and read the response. Spring throws at startup for this combination. Use setAllowedOriginPatterns(List.of("https://*.example.com")) when you need dynamic subdomains with credentials — it echoes the actual origin back instead of *.

CORS is not authorization

A frequent misconception worth correcting out loud: CORS headers instruct the browser. curl, Postman, and any server ignore them completely. It does not protect your API — it protects your users from a malicious site piggybacking on their session. Authentication and authorization are separate and still mandatory.

CORS vs CSRF

They're often confused. CORS relaxes a restriction on reading cross-origin responses. CSRF is about a cross-origin request being sent with the user's cookies and having a side effect — CORS doesn't prevent that (the request still goes through; the attacker just can't read the reply). That's why a cookie-authenticated API still needs CSRF protection even with strict CORS.

How to say it in an interview

“It's a browser mechanism — the server returns Allow-Origin headers permitting a named origin, and anything non-simple gets an OPTIONS preflight first. I configure a CorsConfigurationSource with explicit origins and register it in the security chain, because otherwise Security 401s the preflight, which surfaces as a misleading CORS error. Wildcard with credentials is rejected by browsers, so I use origin patterns. And it's not an API security control — curl ignores it entirely.”

question: What is CSRF, and when is it safe to disable CSRF protection?
answer:
CSRF tricks an authenticated user's browser into making a state-changing request to your site. Because browsers attach cookies automatically to any request to your domain, a hidden form on evil.com can POST to your bank and the request arrives fully authenticated.

Spring's defence is a synchroniser token: the server issues an unpredictable token, forms echo it, and the server rejects requests without it. The attacker can send cookies but can't read your token (that's the same-origin policy).

Disabling it is safe only when authentication isn't ambient — i.e. a stateless API using an Authorization: Bearer header, since a browser never attaches that automatically. If you authenticate with cookies or a session, CSRF protection is required, API or not.
tip: Everyone writes csrf().disable() by reflex. The correct answer is conditional: no cookies = no CSRF risk. Cookie-based auth (including a JWT in an httpOnly cookie!) absolutely needs it.
deep:
The attack

<!-- on evil.com, while you're logged in to bank.com -->
<form action="https://bank.com/transfer" method="POST">
  <input name="to" value="attacker"><input name="amount" value="10000">
</form>
<script>document.forms[0].submit()</script>

The browser attaches bank.com's session cookie automatically. The server sees a perfectly authenticated request. The attacker never reads the response — they don't need to; the transfer already happened.

Why a Bearer token is immune

Nothing attaches an Authorization header automatically — JavaScript has to add it, and evil.com's JavaScript can't read your token from your origin's storage. No ambient credential, no CSRF. But put that same JWT in a cookie and you've reintroduced the vulnerability, because now the browser sends it automatically again. That nuance is the whole question.

SameSite cookies — good, not sufficient

Set-Cookie: session=abc; HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Lax
Strict  never sent cross-site (breaks inbound links to logged-in pages)
Lax     sent on top-level GET navigation only — blocks the POST attack (modern default)
None    always sent; requires Secure

Lax is now the browser default and blocks classic form-POST CSRF. But it's defence in depth, not a replacement: older browsers, SameSite=None for legitimate cross-site flows, and GET endpoints with side effects all leave gaps.

Never mutate state on GET

@GetMapping("/delete/{id}")   // <- an <img src> tag can trigger this

Spring skips CSRF checks on safe methods (GET/HEAD/OPTIONS) by specification. A side-effecting GET therefore has no CSRF protection at all and can be fired by an image tag. It also violates HTTP semantics and gets pre-fetched by browsers and crawlers.

SPA integration

The cookie repository writes an XSRF-TOKEN cookie that JS can read; the SPA echoes it in the X-XSRF-TOKEN header. Angular's HttpClient does this automatically out of the box — worth mentioning given an Angular background.

How to say it in an interview

“CSRF exploits the fact that browsers attach cookies automatically, so a third-party page can trigger an authenticated state change. Spring defends with a synchroniser token the attacker can't read. Whether to disable it is entirely about the credential: a stateless API with a Bearer header has no ambient credential, so disabling is correct. Cookie or session auth needs it — including a JWT stored in an httpOnly cookie, which people forget. And SameSite=Lax helps but isn't a substitute.”

question: @SpringBootTest vs test slices — how do you structure tests?
answer:
@SpringBootTest loads the whole context — thorough but slow, and it makes failures hard to localise. Test slices load only one layer:

  • @WebMvcTest — controllers, JSON, validation, security filters; no services or repos (mock them).
  • @DataJpaTest — JPA, repositories, and an embedded DB; transactional and rolled back per test.
  • @JsonTest, @RestClientTest, @DataRedisTest, etc.

The pyramid in practice: mostly plain JUnit unit tests with Mockito (no Spring at all — milliseconds), a layer of slices, and a small number of full @SpringBootTest tests for critical end-to-end paths.
tip: The under-appreciated point: Spring CACHES contexts between test classes, keyed by configuration. Every unique combination of @MockBean/properties/profiles creates a NEW context — that's usually why the suite is slow.
deep:
Context caching — the real performance lever

Spring caches an ApplicationContext per unique configuration and reuses it across test classes. Anything that changes the config forks a new context (each costing seconds):

@MockitoBean UserService svc;          // different mocked beans = different context
@TestPropertySource(properties="a=1")  // different properties = different context
@ActiveProfiles("test")                // different profiles = different context

So standardising on a few test configurations makes a suite dramatically faster. "Our test suite takes 20 minutes" is usually 40 distinct contexts. Naming this is a strong senior answer.

@MockBean is deprecated in Boot 3.4

@MockBean     -> @MockitoBean       (org.springframework.test.context.bean.override.mockito)
@SpyBean      -> @MockitoSpyBean

Knowing the current name signals you're on modern Boot.

@DataJpaTest against H2 — a real trap

@DataJpaTest replaces your DataSource with an embedded H2 by default. But H2 is not Postgres: different SQL dialects, different constraint behaviour, no native JSON/array types, different sequence semantics. Native queries that pass on H2 fail in prod. Fix with Testcontainers — a real Postgres in Docker:

@DataJpaTest
@AutoConfigureTestDatabase(replace = Replace.NONE)   // don't swap in H2
@Testcontainers
class RepoTest {
  @Container @ServiceConnection                      // Boot 3.1 auto-wires the URL
  static PostgreSQLContainer<?> db = new PostgreSQLContainer<>("postgres:16");
}

@ServiceConnection is the modern touch — it replaces the old @DynamicPropertySource boilerplate.

Rolled-back tests hide bugs

@DataJpaTest is transactional and rolls back — convenient, but it means the persistence context is never actually flushed unless you force it. A broken constraint or a bad mapping can pass silently because no INSERT ever hit the DB. Use TestEntityManager.flush() (or saveAndFlush) when testing constraints.

The same wrapper also means the test and the code share one transaction — so a test can see uncommitted data that a real caller never would, masking LazyInitializationExceptions.

@SpringBootTest webEnvironment

MOCK (default)   MockMvc — no real server, no real HTTP
RANDOM_PORT      real embedded server + TestRestTemplate/WebTestClient
DEFINED_PORT     real server on the configured port
NONE             no web environment

Important: with RANDOM_PORT the server runs on its own thread, so the test's @Transactional rollback does not cover the server's work — you must clean up yourself.

How to say it in an interview

“Mostly plain JUnit + Mockito unit tests with no Spring, slices for the layers — @WebMvcTest with MockMvc for controllers, @DataJpaTest for repositories — and a few full @SpringBootTest for critical paths. Two things I watch: Spring caches contexts per unique configuration, so scattered @MockitoBean and property overrides silently fork contexts and slow the suite; and I run repository tests against Testcontainers Postgres rather than H2, because H2 dialect differences let broken SQL pass.”

question: How do you unit test a service, and when do you mock?
answer:
Constructor injection means a service is a plain object — instantiate it with Mockito mocks and no Spring context at all. @ExtendWith(MockitoExtension.class) plus @Mock/@InjectMocks wires it in milliseconds.

What to mock: things you don't own or that are slow/non-deterministic — repositories, HTTP clients, message publishers, the clock. What not to mock: value objects, DTOs, the class under test, and (usually) types you don't own directly — prefer wrapping a third-party client in your own interface and mocking that.

Over-mocking produces tests that assert your mocks were called in the order you wrote them — they pass while production is broken.
tip: 'Mock roles, not objects.' If a test needs 8 mocks, the class has 8 dependencies — the test is telling you about a design problem, not a testing problem.
deep:
Test behaviour, not implementation

// BAD — asserts HOW, breaks on any refactor
verify(repo).findById(1L);
verify(repo).save(any());
verify(mapper).toDto(any());

// GOOD — asserts WHAT the caller observes
assertThat(service.placeOrder(1L, cart).status()).isEqualTo(CONFIRMED);
then(mail).should().send(eq("a@b.com"), contains("confirmed"));

Verifying every internal call couples the test to the implementation: rename a private method and 30 tests break though nothing regressed. Verify outgoing effects that matter (an email sent, an event published) and assert on return values/state.

Mocking the clock

// untestable
if (order.getCreatedAt().isBefore(Instant.now().minus(30, DAYS))) { ... }

// testable — inject a Clock
@Service class OrderService {
  private final Clock clock;                     // @Bean Clock.systemUTC() in prod
  boolean isStale(Order o) {
    return o.getCreatedAt().isBefore(Instant.now(clock).minus(30, DAYS));
  }
}
// test: Clock.fixed(Instant.parse("2026-01-01T00:00:00Z"), ZoneOffset.UTC)

Injecting a Clock instead of calling Instant.now() is a small thing that reads as very experienced — no more flaky midnight tests or Thread.sleep.

ArgumentCaptor

var captor = ArgumentCaptor.forClass(Order.class);
then(repo).should().save(captor.capture());
assertThat(captor.getValue().getTotal()).isEqualByComparingTo("99.99");

For asserting on an object your code constructed internally and passed onward.

Strict stubs

MockitoExtension defaults to strict stubs: an unused given(...) fails the test as UnnecessaryStubbingException. That's a feature — it flags stubs left behind by a refactor, i.e. tests that stopped testing what they claim.

Don't mock what you don't own

Mocking a third-party client encodes your assumption about its behaviour. If the library changes (or you were wrong), the mock happily keeps passing. Wrap it in your own thin interface, mock that in unit tests, and cover the real thing with one integration test (WireMock/Testcontainers).

Mock vs Spy vs Fake

mock  — everything stubbed, no real behaviour (default)
spy   — real object, selected methods stubbed (a smell on your own classes)
fake  — a working lightweight implementation (in-memory repo) — often the best
        and most under-used option

How to say it in an interview

“Constructor injection means the service is just an object, so I use @Mock/@InjectMocks with no Spring context — milliseconds per test. I mock things I don't own or that are slow or non-deterministic, and I inject a Clock rather than calling Instant.now() so time-based logic is testable. I try to assert observable outcomes rather than verifying every internal call — over-verified tests break on refactors while missing real bugs.”

question: RestTemplate vs WebClient vs RestClient — which do you use?
answer:
• RestTemplate — the classic blocking client. In maintenance mode since Spring 5: not deprecated, still supported, but receiving no new features.
  • WebClient — reactive and non-blocking, fluent API. Works in a plain MVC app too (call .block()), which is why it became the default recommendation for a while.
  • RestClient (Spring 6.1+) — the modern answer for blocking code: WebClient's fluent API with synchronous semantics and no reactive dependency.

So: RestClient for a blocking MVC app, WebClient if you're on WebFlux or need real concurrency/streaming, and don't start anything new on RestTemplate.

Whichever you pick, the non-negotiable part is timeouts — the defaults are effectively infinite.
tip: Naming RestClient shows you're current — most candidates stop at 'RestTemplate is deprecated' (it isn't; it's in maintenance mode). And 'no timeout' is the #1 production failure with any of them.
deep:
Timeouts are the whole game

Default connect/read timeouts are unbounded. One slow downstream service then holds your Tomcat threads open until all 200 are consumed — your service goes down because someone else's is slow. That cascade is the single most common distributed-systems outage, and "I always set connect and read timeouts" is the answer that matters most here.

connect timeout  — how long to establish the TCP connection
read timeout     — how long to wait for data once connected
(neither bounds the TOTAL call — a slow trickle of bytes can exceed both)

Pair timeouts with a circuit breaker (Resilience4j) so a persistently failing dependency fails fast instead of burning threads.

Where WebClient genuinely wins

// 3 calls in parallel, total = slowest, not the sum
Mono<User>    user  = client.get().uri("/users/{id}", id).retrieve().bodyToMono(User.class);
Mono<Account> acct  = client.get().uri("/accounts/{id}", id).retrieve().bodyToMono(Account.class);
Mono<Prefs>   prefs = client.get().uri("/prefs/{id}", id).retrieve().bodyToMono(Prefs.class);

Mono.zip(user, acct, prefs).map(t -> new Profile(t.getT1(), t.getT2(), t.getT3()));

Also: streaming large responses without buffering, and handling thousands of concurrent outbound calls on a handful of threads.

Never do this

webClient.get().retrieve().bodyToMono(User.class).block();   // in a WebFlux handler
// blocks an event-loop thread -> there are only a few -> the whole app stalls

Blocking on the event loop is the cardinal WebFlux sin. (In an MVC app, .block() is merely pointless overhead — which is precisely why RestClient exists.)

Declarative HTTP interfaces (Spring 6)

public interface UserApi {
  @GetExchange("/users/{id}")
  User byId(@PathVariable Long id);
}

@Bean UserApi userApi(RestClient rc) {
  return HttpServiceProxyFactory
      .builderFor(RestClientAdapter.create(rc)).build()
      .createClient(UserApi.class);
}

Feign-style declarative clients built into Spring — no OpenFeign dependency needed. Mentioning this is a nice current-knowledge signal.

RestTemplate isn't thread-unsafe

A common myth. RestTemplate is thread-safe once configured — define it as a bean, don't new it per request (which leaks connections and skips your timeout config).

How to say it in an interview

“RestTemplate is in maintenance mode — not deprecated, but nothing new lands there. For blocking code I use RestClient, which is Spring 6.1's fluent synchronous client; WebClient when I'm on WebFlux or genuinely need parallel or streaming calls. The thing I always do regardless is set connect and read timeouts — the defaults are infinite, and one slow dependency will otherwise consume every Tomcat thread and take my service down with it.”

question: How does @Async work, and what's wrong with the default executor?
answer:
@EnableAsync + @Async makes a method run on a different thread via a proxy; it returns immediately with void, Future, or CompletableFuture.

The default executor is the problem. Historically Boot used a SimpleAsyncTaskExecutor, which creates a brand-new thread per call and never pools — under load that's unbounded thread creation and OOM. Always define your own ThreadPoolTaskExecutor with an explicit core/max size, a bounded queue, and a rejection policy.

Same proxy caveats as always: self-invocation runs synchronously, and @Async on a private method does nothing — both silently.
tip: The queue is where people get it wrong: with an unbounded queue, maxPoolSize is NEVER reached — the pool never grows past core size because the queue always accepts. That surprises nearly everyone.
deep:
The core/max/queue interaction

1. tasks <= corePoolSize        -> new thread per task
2. core busy                    -> QUEUE the task
3. queue FULL                   -> grow threads up to maxPoolSize
4. queue full AND max reached   -> RejectedExecutionHandler

So maxPoolSize only matters once the queue is full. With queueCapacity = Integer.MAX_VALUE step 3 never happens: your pool sits at core size forever, the queue grows without bound, latency climbs invisibly, and eventually you OOM. "I set max to 200 for throughput" with an unbounded queue means the 200 is decorative.

Rejection policies

AbortPolicy         throw RejectedExecutionException (default)
CallerRunsPolicy    run it on the CALLING thread -> natural backpressure
DiscardPolicy       silently drop  (almost always wrong)
DiscardOldestPolicy drop the oldest queued task

CallerRunsPolicy is usually the right choice: it slows the producer down instead of dropping work or exploding — self-throttling.

@Async void swallows exceptions

@Async
void send(String to) { throw new RuntimeException("smtp down"); }
// caller returns immediately — the exception vanishes into the void

With void there's no channel to report failure, so it goes to the AsyncUncaughtExceptionHandler — and if you didn't register one, it's effectively lost. Return CompletableFuture when the caller needs to know.

ThreadLocals do not propagate

The new thread has none of the caller's context:

  • SecurityContext — empty; authentication is null inside @Async.
  • MDC — your correlation id disappears from async logs.
  • Request scope — request-scoped beans blow up.
  • Transaction — @Async + @Transactional on the same method means the transaction is on the new thread; the caller's transaction has nothing to do with it, and can commit or roll back independently.
// propagate security context
@Bean
Executor taskExecutor() {
  return new DelegatingSecurityContextExecutor(realExecutor());
}

The @Async + @Transactional race

@Transactional
void placeOrder(Order o) {
  repo.save(o);                 // not committed yet!
  events.notifyAsync(o.getId());// async thread may read the DB BEFORE the commit
}                               // -> "row not found" intermittently

A genuinely nasty, load-dependent bug. Fix by publishing on @TransactionalEventListener(phase = AFTER_COMMIT).

Virtual threads (Boot 3.2+)

spring.threads.virtual.enabled=true

Changes the calculus: virtual threads are cheap, so blocking I/O no longer needs a carefully sized pool. Pool sizing was always about the cost of platform threads. Note pinning caveats with synchronized blocks (largely resolved in JDK 24+).

How to say it in an interview

“@Async hands the call to an executor via a proxy. The default doesn't pool, so I always define a ThreadPoolTaskExecutor with a bounded queue and CallerRunsPolicy for backpressure — with an unbounded queue maxPoolSize is never even reached. The traps: @Async void swallows exceptions unless I register an uncaught handler, ThreadLocals like SecurityContext and MDC don't propagate, and combining it with @Transactional races the commit — so I trigger async work from an after-commit event.”

question: How does @Scheduled work, and what breaks when you run multiple instances?
answer:
@EnableScheduling + @Scheduled with fixedRate, fixedDelay, or a cron expression. The method must take no arguments and return void.

Two things bite in production:

  • The default scheduler is a single thread. One long-running job delays every other scheduled task in the app.
  • Every instance runs the job. Scale to 5 pods and your nightly billing job runs 5 times — concurrently. Scheduling is per-JVM and has no cluster awareness at all.

The fix for the second is a distributed lock — ShedLock is the usual lightweight answer, or Quartz with a JDBC store for real job management.
tip: 'What happens with 3 replicas?' is the question. Answering 'it runs 3 times, so I'd use ShedLock' immediately separates you from candidates who've only run one instance locally.
deep:
fixedRate vs fixedDelay

fixedDelay = 5s   [--run 8s--]-----5s-----[--run 8s--]-----5s-----
                  gap measured from the END -> never overlaps

fixedRate = 5s    [--run 8s--]
                       [--run 8s--]     <- next start was due at 5s
                  fires every 5s from the START

Subtle: even with fixedRate, the single-threaded scheduler won't run the same method concurrently — it queues the missed executions and then fires them back-to-back to catch up, which is its own surprise (a burst after a slow run). For genuine concurrency you need a bigger pool and to accept overlap.

For polling something, fixedDelay is nearly always what you want — it can't pile up.

Cron syntax — Spring has 6 fields

second minute hour day-of-month month day-of-week
0      0      2    *            *     *        -> 02:00 every day
0      */15   *    *            *     *        -> every 15 minutes
0      0      9    *            *     MON-FRI  -> 09:00 on weekdays

Unix cron has 5 fields — Spring adds seconds at the front. Pasting a Unix cron string shifts everything by one field and it runs at the wrong time. Also use @Scheduled(cron = "${app.report.cron}") so the schedule is configurable per environment (and can be disabled with -).

Always set the zone

Without zone, cron uses the JVM default — so the same build fires at different real times in different regions, and DST shifts it twice a year. A job at 02:00 local can run twice or not at all on DST-change night. Setting zone explicitly (or running the JVM in UTC) avoids a class of bugs that only appear in October.

ShedLock specifics

lockAtMostFor  — the safety net: if the pod dies mid-job, the lock is released
                 after this. Must EXCEED the longest plausible run, or a second
                 pod starts the job while the first is still going.
lockAtLeastFor — the anti-race floor: stops a fast job from being picked up by
                 another pod whose clock is slightly skewed.

ShedLock guarantees "at most one execution", not "exactly once" — if the holder dies, the run is simply skipped. If you need exactly-once, the job itself must be idempotent and resumable. Making that distinction is a strong answer.

@Scheduled is also proxy-based

It's on a Spring bean or it doesn't run. And an exception thrown from a scheduled method with the default error handler kills subsequent executions in some configurations — always try/catch inside the job and log, rather than letting it propagate.

When to use Quartz instead

ShedLock only prevents concurrent runs. Quartz gives persistent jobs, misfire handling, clustering, dynamic scheduling at runtime, and a job history — worth it when scheduling is a product feature rather than a cron chore.

How to say it in an interview

“@EnableScheduling with cron or fixedDelay — and I use fixedDelay for polling since it measures from the end and can't pile up. Two production issues: the scheduler pool defaults to one thread, so a slow job blocks the others; and every replica runs the job independently, so with 3 pods a nightly job runs 3 times. I use ShedLock for a distributed lock, sized so lockAtMostFor exceeds the longest run — and I still make the job idempotent, because that gives at-most-once, not exactly-once.”

question: How do Spring application events work, and why use @TransactionalEventListener?
answer:
ApplicationEventPublisher.publishEvent(obj) and @EventListener give you in-process pub/sub for decoupling: the order service announces "an order was placed" without knowing that email, analytics, and inventory care.

By default listeners are synchronous and run in the publisher's thread and transaction — so a listener throwing rolls back the publisher's work. That's often surprising.

@TransactionalEventListener ties the listener to the transaction lifecycle instead, most usefully AFTER_COMMIT: the side effect fires only if the transaction actually committed. That solves the classic "we emailed the customer, then the transaction rolled back" bug.
tip: The killer use case: never send an email/publish to Kafka inside a transaction — if it rolls back you've told the world about something that didn't happen. AFTER_COMMIT is the fix.
deep:
The bug it fixes

@Transactional
void placeOrder(Order o) {
  repo.save(o);
  mailClient.send(o.getEmail(), "Order confirmed!");   // sent immediately
  paymentClient.charge(o);                             // throws
}   // rollback -> no order in the DB, but the customer HAS the email

Email can't be rolled back. Neither can a Kafka message or an SMS. Any external side effect must happen after the commit, never inside the transaction.

Phases

BEFORE_COMMIT   still in the tx — can still veto it
AFTER_COMMIT    (default) only on success — the useful one
AFTER_ROLLBACK  on failure — good for audit/alerting
AFTER_COMPLETION either way

AFTER_COMMIT listeners can't write to the DB (by default)

The transaction is already committed, so a repository call in the listener runs outside any transaction and silently does nothing (or fails). If the listener must persist, it needs its own:

@TransactionalEventListener(phase = AFTER_COMMIT)
@Transactional(propagation = Propagation.REQUIRES_NEW)   // required!
void onPlaced(OrderPlacedEvent e) { auditRepo.save(...); }

This exact gotcha — "my after-commit listener's save does nothing" — is a great one to know.

@Async + AFTER_COMMIT: the ordering trap

Without @Async, an AFTER_COMMIT listener runs in the publisher's thread after commit — so a slow email still blocks the HTTP response. Adding @Async fixes that, but now the event is on another thread: no SecurityContext, no MDC, and if the app crashes between the commit and the listener running, the event is lost forever. In-process events are not durable.

When events are the wrong tool

Because they're in-memory and non-durable, they're for decoupling within one app, not for guaranteed delivery. If the side effect must happen, you need the transactional outbox: write the message to an outbox table in the same transaction as the business data, then a separate poller publishes it to Kafka and marks it sent. Atomic with the commit, survives a crash. Reaching for that distinction unprompted is a strong senior signal.

Events can obscure flow

Honest trade-off: overusing events makes control flow invisible — you can't "find usages" of an event publish and see what happens next. Good for genuinely optional side effects (audit, notification, cache invalidation); bad for core sequential business logic, which reads better as a direct call.

Generic events and conditions

@EventListener(condition = "#event.total > 1000")   // SpEL filter
void onLargeOrder(OrderPlacedEvent event) { }

@EventListener  // Spring resolves generics: only fires for Order payloads
void onCreated(EntityCreatedEvent<Order> e) { }

How to say it in an interview

“In-process pub/sub for decoupling side effects. The default listener is synchronous and shares the publisher's transaction, so I use @TransactionalEventListener(AFTER_COMMIT) for anything external — otherwise a rollback leaves you having emailed a customer about an order that doesn't exist. Two gotchas: an after-commit listener needs REQUIRES_NEW to write to the DB, and these events aren't durable — if delivery must be guaranteed I use a transactional outbox instead.”

question: How does Spring AOP work? JDK proxy vs CGLIB.
answer:
AOP factors out cross-cutting concerns (transactions, security, caching, logging, metrics) into aspects applied around your beans. Spring implements it with runtime proxies: at postProcessAfterInitialization, the container wraps the bean and callers get the proxy.

Two proxy mechanisms:

  • JDK dynamic proxy — only works if the bean implements an interface; the proxy implements the same interface.
  • CGLIB — generates a subclass at runtime and overrides methods. Works without interfaces, but cannot proxy final classes or final/private/static methods.

Spring Boot forces CGLIB by default (proxyTargetClass=true) — this is why @Transactional on a final method silently does nothing.
tip: Everything proxy-based shares one rule: the proxy is only crossed on an EXTERNAL call. @Transactional, @Async, @Cacheable, @PreAuthorize, @Retryable — all silently no-op on self-invocation. One insight explains five 'why doesn't this work' questions.
deep:
What a proxy looks like

caller ──> [ OrderService$$SpringCGLIB$$0 ]  (subclass generated at runtime)
              before advice (begin tx)
              super.placeOrder()  ──> [ real OrderService ]
              after advice (commit)

Since CGLIB subclasses, a final method can't be overridden — no advice, no error, no warning. Just silence. Same for private and static.

Why self-invocation fails (the one rule)

@Service
class OrderService {
  @Transactional void save(Order o) { }
  void bulk(List<Order> os) {
    os.forEach(this::save);   // `this` = the REAL object, not the proxy -> no advice
  }
}

Once execution is inside the target, the proxy is out of the picture. This single fact explains why @Transactional, @Async, @Cacheable, @PreAuthorize and @Retryable all silently do nothing on internal calls. Fix: move the method to another bean (best), self-inject with @Lazy, or use AopContext.currentProxy().

Advice types

@Before          before the method
@AfterReturning  on normal return (can read the result)
@AfterThrowing   on exception
@After           finally
@Around          wraps everything — most powerful; can modify args, the return
                 value, swallow exceptions, or skip the call entirely

Forgetting pjp.proceed() in an @Around means the target method never runs — and everything returns null with no error.

Spring AOP vs AspectJ

Spring AOP: runtime proxies, method execution only, Spring beans only,
            no self-invocation, no field access, no constructors.
            Zero build setup. Covers ~95% of real needs.

AspectJ:    compile/load-time weaving, modifies the bytecode itself.
            Works on any object, any method (incl. private/final), fields,
            constructors, and self-invocation. Needs a weaver/agent.

Knowing that Spring AOP is proxy-based and therefore limited, and that AspectJ weaving removes those limits, is the crux of this question.

The interface-injection failure

@Service class UserServiceImpl implements UserService { }

@Autowired UserServiceImpl svc;   // BeanNotOfRequiredTypeException with JDK proxies!
@Autowired UserService svc;       // correct — inject the INTERFACE

With a JDK proxy the object implements the interface but is not an instance of your class. Boot's CGLIB default hides this, but it's a classic error when proxyTargetClass=false. Program to interfaces.

Order matters

@Aspect @Order(1) class SecurityAspect { }   // lower = outer = runs first
@Aspect @Order(2) class TxAspect { }

Without explicit ordering, aspect order is undefined — and "is the security check inside or outside the transaction?" has real consequences.

How to say it in an interview

“Spring AOP wraps beans in runtime proxies — JDK dynamic proxies for interfaces, CGLIB subclasses otherwise, and Boot defaults to CGLIB. That single mechanism explains the framework's most common surprises: advice only applies on external calls, so self-invocation silently skips @Transactional or @Cacheable, and CGLIB can't override final or private methods so the annotation is ignored. If I genuinely need to advise self-calls or private methods, that's AspectJ weaving, not Spring AOP.”

question: What is a circuit breaker and how do you use Resilience4j?
answer:
A circuit breaker stops you from hammering a dependency that's already failing. It tracks the failure rate and moves between three states:

  • CLOSED — calls pass through; failures counted.
  • OPEN — the failure threshold was crossed; calls fail immediately without touching the dependency (fail fast) and the fallback runs.
  • HALF_OPEN — after a wait, a few probe calls are allowed. Succeed → CLOSED; fail → OPEN again.

The point is twofold: protect yourself (a dead dependency shouldn't consume your threads waiting on timeouts) and protect the dependency (stop piling load on a struggling service so it can recover).

Resilience4j is the standard choice — Hystrix is end-of-life.
tip: The insight: without a breaker, a slow dependency exhausts your thread pool and your service dies too — that's how one failure cascades into a full outage. The breaker converts a slow failure into a fast one.
deep:
Config

resilience4j:
  circuitbreaker:
    instances:
      inventory:
        sliding-window-type: COUNT_BASED
        sliding-window-size: 20
        minimum-number-of-calls: 10      # don't trip on the first failure
        failure-rate-threshold: 50       # % failures to open
        wait-duration-in-open-state: 30s
        permitted-number-of-calls-in-half-open-state: 3
        slow-call-duration-threshold: 2s
        slow-call-rate-threshold: 100    # slow calls count as failures too
  retry:
    instances:
      inventory:
        max-attempts: 3
        wait-duration: 200ms
        exponential-backoff-multiplier: 2
        retry-exceptions: [java.io.IOException]

minimum-number-of-calls matters: without it, one failed call out of one is a 100% failure rate and the breaker opens on a fluke.

Only retry idempotent operations

GET / PUT / DELETE   -> naturally idempotent, safe to retry
POST /payments       -> NOT — a timeout doesn't mean it failed!
                        the charge may have succeeded and the RESPONSE was lost
                        -> retrying charges the customer twice

This is the trap. A timeout is ambiguous, not a failure. Retrying a non-idempotent POST creates duplicates. The fix is an idempotency key the server dedupes on — then retries are safe.

Retry storms and jitter

Naive retries make an overload worse: the service slows, everyone retries, load triples, it dies. Always use exponential backoff with jitter — without random jitter all clients retry in synchronised waves and keep re-killing the service on each recovery attempt. Cap total attempts, and put the breaker outside the retry so exhausted retries count as one failure toward opening it.

Annotation order

Retry ( CircuitBreaker ( RateLimiter ( TimeLimiter ( Bulkhead ( call )))))

Resilience4j applies aspects in that default order — retry outermost, so a retry re-enters the breaker. You can override with *-aspect-order properties.

Bulkhead

Named after ship compartments: cap the concurrent calls allowed to each dependency, so a hanging dependency can only ever consume (say) 10 threads instead of all 200. It contains the flooding to one compartment. Semaphore-based by default; thread-pool-based available.

Fallbacks need thought

A fallback must be genuinely degraded service, not a lie. Cached/stale data or a reduced feature is good. Returning an empty list that the UI renders as "you have no orders" is worse than an honest error. And the fallback must not call the same failing dependency.

Monitoring

Resilience4j publishes state transitions to Micrometer — alert on the breaker opening. It's the earliest, clearest signal that a dependency is down, usually before anyone reports it.

How to say it in an interview

“It trips after a failure-rate threshold and fails fast instead of queuing calls against a dead dependency — that's what stops one service's outage cascading into mine by exhausting my threads. I use Resilience4j with a sliding window, a minimum call count so a fluke doesn't open it, and a fallback that serves degraded data. I only retry idempotent operations — a POST timeout is ambiguous, the work may have happened — and always with exponential backoff plus jitter so retries don't synchronise into a storm.”

question: How do microservices communicate? Synchronous vs asynchronous.
answer:
Synchronous (REST/gRPC): the caller blocks for a response. Simple, easy to debug, immediately consistent — but it creates temporal coupling: if the callee is down, you're down. Chains multiply: five services at 99.9% in a chain give you 99.5%, and latency is additive.

Asynchronous (Kafka/RabbitMQ): publish an event and move on. The consumer can be down and catch up later, so the services are decoupled in time and you get natural buffering and fan-out. The cost is eventual consistency, harder debugging, and out-of-order/duplicate delivery you must handle.

The rule of thumb: synchronous when the caller genuinely needs the answer to proceed (checking stock before confirming an order); asynchronous for notifying others that something happened (order placed → email, analytics, inventory).
tip: The strongest framing: sync = a question ('is this in stock?'), async = a statement ('an order was placed'). If nobody is waiting on the answer, don't make it a blocking call.
deep:
The availability maths

A -> B -> C -> D -> E, each 99.9% available
combined = 0.999^4 = 99.6%  -> ~3.5 hours of downtime/month you didn't have alone
latency  = sum of every hop, and the SLOWEST call sets your p99

Synchronous chains multiply failure and add latency. Async breaks the chain: the broker holds the message while a consumer is down.

Dual-write — the bug everyone hits

@Transactional
void place(Order o) {
  repo.save(o);                        // DB
  kafka.send("order.placed", event);   // BROKER — different system!
}
// crash between them, or a rollback after the send:
//   -> DB committed but no event   (silent data loss downstream)
//   -> event sent but DB rolled back (downstream acts on a phantom order)

You cannot atomically write to a DB and a broker — there's no shared transaction. The transactional outbox is the standard fix: write the event into an outbox table in the same DB transaction, then a poller (or Debezium CDC reading the WAL) publishes it and marks it sent. Atomic, crash-safe, at-least-once. Naming the outbox pattern here is a genuine senior signal.

At-least-once means duplicates

Almost every broker delivers at least once — a consumer that processed a message but died before acking will see it again. So consumers must be idempotent: dedupe on an event id, or make the operation naturally idempotent (SET status='PAID' rather than balance = balance - 10). "Exactly-once" is mostly marketing; the real design is at-least-once plus idempotency.

Saga — distributed transactions

You can't 2PC across microservices (it doesn't scale and it blocks). A saga is a sequence of local transactions, each with a compensating action if a later step fails:

reserve stock -> charge card -> ship
         fail at charge:  compensate = release stock

Choreography (each service reacts to events — simple, but the flow is invisible) vs orchestration (a coordinator drives it — explicit, but a central component). Note compensation isn't rollback: you can't unsend an email, only send an apology.

Don't share a database

Two services on one schema aren't microservices — they're a distributed monolith with worse latency. Any schema change breaks both, and neither can deploy independently. Each service owns its data; others access it through its API or its events.

Async isn't free

Honest trade-offs: no stack trace across a broker (you need distributed tracing), "where did this message go?" is genuinely hard, ordering is only guaranteed per partition/key, and eventual consistency leaks into UX — the user places an order and doesn't see it in their list for 200ms. That last one usually needs a product decision, not a technical one.

How to say it in an interview

“Sync when the caller needs the answer to continue, async when I'm just announcing that something happened. Sync chains multiply downtime and add latency; async decouples in time at the cost of eventual consistency. The thing I'd flag is the dual write — saving to the DB and publishing to Kafka isn't atomic, so a crash between them loses the event. I'd use a transactional outbox, and make consumers idempotent, because at-least-once delivery means duplicates are normal.”

question: How do you make an API idempotent, and why does it matter?
answer:
Idempotent = doing it N times has the same effect as doing it once. It matters because network timeouts are ambiguous: when a POST /payments times out, the client cannot know whether the charge happened and the response was lost. Retrying might double-charge; not retrying might drop a real order. Only idempotency resolves it.

By HTTP semantics GET, PUT, DELETE are idempotent; POST is not. The standard fix is an idempotency key: the client generates a unique key per logical operation and sends it as a header. The server records the key with the result; a repeat of the same key returns the stored response without re-executing.
tip: Frame it as: a timeout is not a failure, it's an unknown. That single sentence explains why retries need idempotency and impresses more than any implementation detail.
deep:
The DB unique constraint is the real lock

A check-then-act (findByKey then save) is a race: two concurrent retries both find nothing and both charge. The unique constraint on the key column is what actually makes it safe — the database serialises it, the loser catches DataIntegrityViolationException and reads the winner's result. Relying on the constraint rather than application-level checking is the mark of someone who's been burned.

Store the response, not just the key

Returning 200 with an empty body on a replay isn't good enough — the client needs the payment id it lost. Store the serialised response and replay it verbatim.

The in-flight case

If the original request is still processing when the retry arrives, the key exists but there's no result yet. Return 409 Conflict ("in progress, retry shortly") rather than blocking or double-executing.

Natural idempotency is better

// NOT idempotent — retry double-decrements
UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - 100 WHERE id = 1;

// idempotent — retry is harmless
UPDATE accounts SET status = 'PAID' WHERE id = 1;

// idempotent by design — the client supplies the id
PUT /orders/{client-generated-uuid}

Where you can design the operation to be naturally idempotent (absolute state instead of relative deltas, client-supplied ids), you don't need the key machinery at all. That's the better answer when the domain allows it.

Key scope and expiry

  • The key must be scoped per user/tenant — otherwise one client's key collides with another's, or worse, replays another user's response back to them.
  • Keys need a TTL (24h is typical) or the table grows forever.
  • The client must generate the key once per logical operation and reuse it across retries — generating a fresh UUID on each retry defeats the entire mechanism. This is the most common client-side mistake.
  • Ideally validate that the same key isn't reused with a different body (hash the request) — otherwise a buggy client silently gets the wrong cached response.

Consumers too

The same reasoning applies to Kafka/Rabbit consumers: at-least-once delivery guarantees duplicates, so the handler must dedupe on an event id or be naturally idempotent. Idempotency is what makes at-least-once behave like exactly-once.

How to say it in an interview

“It matters because a timeout is ambiguous — the client can't tell if the work happened, so retrying a payment might double-charge. I'd take an Idempotency-Key header, store it with the response under a unique constraint, and replay the stored response on a repeat. The unique constraint is the important part — a find-then-save check is racy under concurrent retries. Where I can, I prefer designing the operation to be naturally idempotent, like setting absolute state instead of applying a delta.”

question: How do you make a Spring Boot service observable?
answer:
Three pillars, all first-class in Boot 3 via Micrometer:

  • Metrics — Micrometer is the vendor-neutral facade (SLF4J for metrics): counters, gauges, timers, exported to Prometheus via /actuator/prometheus. Good for rates, latency percentiles, saturation.
  • Tracing — Micrometer Tracing (which replaced Spring Cloud Sleuth) propagates a trace id across service boundaries, so you can see a request's full path through 6 services in Zipkin/Tempo/Jaeger.
  • Logging — structured (JSON) logs with the trace id in the MDC, so a trace links directly to its logs.

The thing that ties them together is correlation: one trace id in the logs, the metrics, and the trace.
tip: Without a correlation id, debugging across microservices is guesswork. Boot 3 puts traceId/spanId in the MDC automatically — the win is being able to grep every service for one id and reconstruct the whole request.
deep:
Cardinality will bankrupt you

// CATASTROPHIC — one time series per user, forever
registry.counter("orders", "userId", userId).increment();

// fine — bounded set of values
registry.counter("orders", "channel", "web").increment();

Every unique tag combination is a separate time series in Prometheus, held in memory. A user id, order id, email, or raw URL path as a tag means millions of series — it OOMs your app and your metrics backend. This is the #1 metrics mistake and a great thing to raise unprompted. Same reason URL paths must be templated (/users/{id}, not /users/12345) — Spring does this for you, which is exactly why you use @PathVariable rather than parsing the URI.

Timer vs Counter vs Gauge

Counter  monotonically increasing  — requests, errors
Gauge    a value that goes up/down — queue depth, pool connections (weak ref!)
Timer    duration + count          — latency (gives you percentiles)
DistributionSummary  non-time distributions — payload sizes

Gauges hold a weak reference: if the object is GC'd the gauge reports NaN. A classic "my gauge stopped reporting" bug.

Percentiles, not averages

An average latency of 100ms tells you nothing — it hides that 1% of users wait 5 seconds. Track p50/p95/p99. And never average percentiles across instances; that's mathematically meaningless. Export histogram buckets and let Prometheus compute the quantile with histogram_quantile.

Sampling

100% tracing is enormous data volume and real overhead. 1–10% is normal, with the caveat that you'll miss most rare errors — hence tail-based sampling (decide after the fact, keep all traces that errored or were slow), which needs a collector.

Trace context propagation

The trace id travels in W3C traceparent headers. Auto-instrumented clients (RestClient/WebClient built from the injected builder) propagate it automatically. Two places it silently breaks: @Async / manual threads (MDC is a ThreadLocal — use a ContextPropagating executor), and Kafka (put the context in message headers). Losing the id mid-flow is the usual "tracing doesn't work" cause.

Structured logging

Boot 3.4 has built-in support (logging.structured.format.console=ecs). JSON logs mean you can query fields (level=ERROR AND orderId=123) instead of regexing text. Never log secrets, tokens, PII, or full request bodies — logs are usually far less protected than the database and are shipped to third parties.

The four golden signals

Latency, Traffic, Errors, Saturation. Alert on symptoms users feel (error rate, p99 latency), not causes (CPU 80% is not an incident if nobody's affected). Alerting on causes is how you get pager fatigue.

How to say it in an interview

“Micrometer for metrics to Prometheus, Micrometer Tracing — which replaced Sleuth — for distributed traces, and JSON logs with the trace id in the MDC so all three correlate. The thing I'm most careful about is tag cardinality: putting a user id or raw URL in a metric tag creates a time series per value and OOMs both the app and Prometheus. And I alert on symptoms — error rate and p99 — rather than CPU, using histograms so percentiles are computed properly rather than averaged.”

question: Spring MVC vs WebFlux — when would you actually choose reactive?
answer:
MVC is thread-per-request on a servlet container: a request occupies a thread for its whole life, blocking on I/O. Simple to write and debug; the ceiling is the thread pool (200 threads = 200 concurrent in-flight requests) and each thread costs ~1MB of stack.

WebFlux is an event loop with a handful of threads: a request never blocks — it registers a callback and the thread serves someone else. Huge concurrency on tiny resources, plus backpressure (the consumer signals how much it can take).

When to choose it honestly: many concurrent, mostly-idle connections (streaming, SSE/WebSockets, a gateway fanning out to many services). Not for CPU-bound work, and not for a normal CRUD app — reactive code is markedly harder to write, debug (no usable stack traces), and staff, and one blocking call anywhere poisons the whole event loop.

Since Boot 3.2, virtual threads give MVC most of the scalability benefit with none of the complexity — which weakens the case for WebFlux considerably.
tip: The senior answer is knowing when NOT to use it. 'Reactive is faster' is wrong — it's not faster per request, it's more efficient per thread. And WebFlux over a blocking JDBC driver gains you nothing.
deep:
The whole chain must be non-blocking

@GetMapping("/{id}")
Mono<User> get(@PathVariable Long id) {
  return Mono.just(jdbcRepo.findById(id));   // BLOCKING JDBC on the event loop!
}

There are only a few event-loop threads (typically one per core). Block one and you've removed a large fraction of your entire capacity — under load the app stalls completely. WebFlux over JDBC is strictly worse than MVC: all the complexity, none of the benefit, plus a catastrophic failure mode. You need R2DBC, and R2DBC is less mature: no Hibernate, no lazy loading, no @Transactional semantics you're used to, weaker tooling.

If you must block, isolate it: .subscribeOn(Schedulers.boundedElastic()).

Nothing happens until you subscribe

userRepo.save(user);            // returns a Mono — NOTHING RAN. No save.
userRepo.save(user).subscribe(); // now it runs
return userRepo.save(user);      // best — let the framework subscribe

Publishers are lazy blueprints. A forgotten subscribe is a silent no-op with no error — a signature reactive bug.

Debugging is genuinely worse

at reactor.core.publisher.MonoFlatMap$FlatMapMain.onNext(MonoFlatMap.java:...)
at reactor.core.publisher.FluxMap$MapSubscriber.onNext(FluxMap.java:...)
... 40 more frames of Reactor internals, none of them your code

The stack trace reflects the operator chain, not your logic — the call stack is meaningless because the work moved across threads. You need Hooks.onOperatorDebug() (expensive) or checkpoints. ThreadLocals don't work either, so MDC logging and SecurityContext need the Reactor Context instead.

Backpressure — the real feature

A fast producer overwhelming a slow consumer normally means an unbounded buffer and OOM. Reactive Streams lets the consumer request n items, so demand flows upstream. This genuinely matters for streaming; it's irrelevant for request/response.

Virtual threads changed the calculus

spring.threads.virtual.enabled=true   # Boot 3.2+, Java 21

Blocking code on virtual threads scales like non-blocking code, because a blocked virtual thread costs almost nothing — it unmounts from its carrier. You keep imperative code, real stack traces, working ThreadLocals, debuggers, and JDBC. For most services that used to be WebFlux candidates purely for concurrency, virtual threads are now the better trade. WebFlux still wins for streaming and true backpressure. Saying this shows current thinking.

How to say it in an interview

“MVC is thread-per-request; WebFlux is an event loop, so it holds far more concurrent connections on far fewer threads and gives real backpressure. I'd choose it for streaming or a gateway fanning out to many services — not for CRUD, because the code is much harder to debug and one blocking call on the event loop takes the whole app down. It's also pointless over JDBC; you'd need R2DBC and lose Hibernate. And since Boot 3.2 virtual threads give blocking code most of the same scalability, so the bar for reactive is higher now.”

question: What are virtual threads and what do they change for Spring Boot?
answer:
Virtual threads (Project Loom, Java 21) are lightweight threads managed by the JVM rather than the OS. A platform thread maps 1:1 to an OS thread and costs ~1MB of stack, so ~a few thousand is the practical ceiling. A virtual thread costs a few hundred bytes and millions can exist.

The magic: when a virtual thread blocks on I/O, the JVM unmounts it from its carrier platform thread and runs someone else. So blocking code no longer wastes a thread — you get the scalability of async with ordinary imperative code, real stack traces, working debuggers, and JDBC.

In Boot 3.2+ it's one property: spring.threads.virtual.enabled=true. Tomcat then uses a virtual thread per request and the 200-thread ceiling disappears.
tip: The framing that lands: virtual threads make the thread pool obsolete for I/O-bound work. Pools existed because threads were expensive. They're not anymore — so don't pool virtual threads.
deep:
Don't pool them

// WRONG — defeats the entire point
Executors.newFixedThreadPool(200, virtualThreadFactory());

// RIGHT — a new virtual thread per task, always
Executors.newVirtualThreadPerTaskExecutor();

Pooling exists to amortise the cost of creating a thread. Virtual threads are cheap to create, so pooling them just reimposes an artificial limit. "Never pool virtual threads" is the headline guidance from the JDK team.

Pinning

A virtual thread that blocks inside a synchronized block cannot unmount — it pins its carrier thread. Enough pinned carriers and you deadlock or stall, because the carrier pool is small (one per core). Historically the fix was replacing synchronized with ReentrantLock. JDK 24 largely fixed pinning, but older library code (and some drivers) is still worth checking:

-Djdk.tracePinnedThreads=full

ThreadLocals still work — but reconsider them

A ThreadLocal per virtual thread with a million threads is a million copies. They function correctly (unlike in WebFlux, which is a real advantage) but the memory model assumed few threads. Scoped values are the successor.

The bottleneck moves

This is the crucial nuance. Removing the thread limit doesn't remove the real limits — it exposes them. With 200 threads, the thread pool was accidentally protecting your database: only 200 requests could reach it. With unlimited virtual threads, 10,000 concurrent requests all hit a 20-connection Hikari pool and queue there, or overwhelm the DB entirely. So you must add explicit backpressure — rate limiting, bulkheads, a bounded connection pool — that the thread pool used to provide implicitly. Making this point is a strong senior answer.

Not for CPU-bound work
