Virtual threads help when threads are waiting. CPU-bound work still needs at most ~n-cores parallelism — a million virtual threads doing maths is slower, not faster (more context switching, more memory). Use them for I/O; use a fixed pool or fork-join for computation.

vs WebFlux

Virtual threads  imperative code, real stack traces, ThreadLocals, JDBC works,
                 debuggers work. Scales I/O concurrency. No backpressure.

WebFlux          backpressure, streaming, composable operators.
                 Harder to write/debug; needs a fully non-blocking stack.

For most services choosing reactive purely for concurrency, virtual threads are now the simpler answer. WebFlux keeps its edge for streaming and genuine backpressure.

How to say it in an interview

“JVM-managed threads that unmount from their carrier when they block, so blocking I/O no longer costs a thread. In Boot 3.2 it's one property and Tomcat serves each request on one — imperative code that scales like reactive, with real stack traces and JDBC intact. Two caveats: never pool them, since cheap creation is the point; and the thread pool was implicitly protecting downstream — remove the limit and 10,000 requests now hit the connection pool at once, so you need explicit rate limiting or bulkheads.”

question: How do you deploy without dropping requests? Explain graceful shutdown.
answer:
On SIGTERM, Boot's graceful shutdown stops accepting new requests but lets in-flight ones finish, up to a timeout, then closes the context (running @PreDestroy).

server.shutdown=graceful
spring.lifecycle.timeout-per-shutdown-phase=30s

But that alone still drops requests in Kubernetes, because of a race: the pod gets SIGTERM and the endpoint removal from the load balancer happens concurrently and asynchronously. For a second or two the LB is still routing to a pod that has stopped accepting. The fix is a preStop sleep — keep serving for a few seconds after SIGTERM so the deregistration propagates first.

Combine that with a readiness probe that flips to out-of-service on shutdown (management.endpoint.health.probes.enabled=true).
tip: The preStop sleep is the detail that shows real Kubernetes experience — nearly everyone stops at 'server.shutdown=graceful' and still sees 502s during deploys.
code:
# application.yml
server:
  shutdown: graceful
spring:
  lifecycle:
    timeout-per-shutdown-phase: 30s
management:
  endpoint:
    health:
      probes:
        enabled: true

# deployment.yaml
lifecycle:
  preStop:
    exec:
      command: ["sh", "-c", "sleep 10"]   # keep serving while the LB deregisters
terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 45         # MUST exceed preStop + shutdown timeout
readinessProbe:
  httpGet: { path: /actuator/health/readiness, port: 8080 }
livenessProbe:
  httpGet: { path: /actuator/health/liveness, port: 8080 }
deep:
The race, precisely

t=0   kubectl delete pod
      ├─ SIGTERM sent to the container        ─┐ these happen
      └─ endpoint removed from Service        ─┘ CONCURRENTLY, not in order
t=0.1 app stops accepting new connections
t=0.5 kube-proxy on node A updates its iptables
t=1.2 kube-proxy on node B updates its iptables   <- still routing until now!
      -> requests sent between t=0.1 and t=1.2 hit a closed listener = 502

Kubernetes never promised ordering here. preStop: sleep 10 delays the SIGTERM, so the app keeps serving normally while the deregistration finishes propagating. The sleep does nothing itself — the delay is the whole point.

The timeout hierarchy must nest

terminationGracePeriodSeconds (45s)
  >  preStop (10s) + spring shutdown timeout (30s)

If the grace period expires first, Kubernetes sends SIGKILL — in-flight requests die, @PreDestroy never runs, connections aren't returned. Getting this arithmetic wrong silently reintroduces exactly the dropped requests you were preventing.

Readiness vs liveness, again

Readiness failing → removed from the Service (no traffic, pod stays alive). Liveness failing → restarted. During shutdown you want readiness to fail; a liveness check that fails during shutdown just adds a pointless restart. And never make liveness depend on the database: a DB blip then restarts every pod simultaneously, turning a recoverable incident into an outage.

startupProbe for slow starts

A JVM app can take 30s+ to boot. Without a startupProbe, the liveness probe starts failing during startup and Kubernetes kills the pod — it never finishes booting, crash-looping forever. startupProbe holds liveness off until the app is up.

In-flight isn't just HTTP

Graceful shutdown covers the web server. Also drain: @KafkaListener consumers (finish and commit offsets — otherwise messages redeliver), @Async executors (setWaitForTasksToCompleteOnShutdown(true)), and scheduled jobs mid-run.

Rolling deploys need compatible schemas

During a rollout, old and new pods serve simultaneously against one database — which is why migrations must be backward compatible (expand/contract). Graceful shutdown and schema compatibility are the two halves of zero-downtime deploys.

How to say it in an interview

“server.shutdown=graceful so SIGTERM stops new requests but drains in-flight ones. That's not sufficient on Kubernetes though — SIGTERM and endpoint deregistration happen concurrently, so the load balancer keeps sending traffic to a pod that's already stopped accepting. I add a preStop sleep so the pod keeps serving while deregistration propagates, and make sure the termination grace period exceeds preStop plus the shutdown timeout, otherwise it's SIGKILL and you drop requests anyway.”

question: How do you write a custom auto-configuration / starter?
answer:
Create an @AutoConfiguration class with @Conditional guards and @Bean methods, bind config via @ConfigurationProperties, and register it in META-INF/spring/org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.AutoConfiguration.imports — one fully-qualified class name per line.

That file location is the Boot 3 change everyone trips on: it replaced META-INF/spring.factories. Forget it and your auto-configuration is simply never loaded, with no error at all.

Convention: two modules — acme-spring-boot-autoconfigure (the code) and acme-spring-boot-starter (an empty POM of dependencies). Third-party naming is acme-spring-boot-starter, never spring-boot-starter-acme — that prefix is reserved for the Spring team.
tip: The two things that get you marked as having actually done this: the AutoConfiguration.imports path in Boot 3 (spring.factories no longer works for auto-config), and always using @ConditionalOnMissingBean so consumers can override you.
deep:
Why @ConditionalOnMissingBean is non-negotiable

Without it your library forces its bean on every consumer, and a project that defines its own gets a NoUniqueBeanDefinitionException — your "convenience" library broke their app. With it, you provide a default that silently steps aside. That's the entire contract of auto-configuration: opinionated defaults, always overridable.

Ordering

@AutoConfiguration(after = DataSourceAutoConfiguration.class)
@AutoConfigureBefore(WebMvcAutoConfiguration.class)

Your auto-config may need Boot's DataSource to exist first. Note @ConditionalOnMissingBean evaluates against beans registered so far — so ordering directly affects whether a condition matches. Getting this wrong causes maddening "works in one app, not another" bugs.

All auto-configurations run after user @Configuration classes, which is what makes user overrides reliable.

The conditions toolbox

@ConditionalOnClass / @ConditionalOnMissingClass    classpath
@ConditionalOnBean  / @ConditionalOnMissingBean     context
@ConditionalOnProperty(name=, havingValue=, matchIfMissing=)
@ConditionalOnWebApplication(type = SERVLET)
@ConditionalOnResource(resources = "classpath:acme.json")
@ConditionalOnExpression("${a:false} and ${b:false}")
// or implement Condition / extend SpringBootCondition for custom logic

matchIfMissing = true = opt-out (on by default); omitting it = opt-in. That choice defines your library's default UX.

Metadata for IDE auto-completion

<dependency>
  <artifactId>spring-boot-configuration-processor</artifactId>
  <optional>true</optional>
</dependency>

Generates spring-configuration-metadata.json from your @ConfigurationProperties, so consumers get auto-complete and inline docs for acme.audit.* in their IDE. Javadoc on the fields becomes the tooltip. Small touch, very professional.

Never put @ComponentScan in a starter

It would scan your packages in the consumer's context, silently pulling in beans they never asked for and creating conflicts. Declare beans explicitly with @Bean methods. This is a real rule people violate.

Testing it

new ApplicationContextRunner()
  .withConfiguration(AutoConfigurations.of(AuditAutoConfiguration.class))
  .withPropertyValues("acme.audit.url=http://x")
  .run(ctx -> assertThat(ctx).hasSingleBean(AuditClient.class));

// verify the back-off actually works
new ApplicationContextRunner()
  .withUserConfiguration(CustomClientConfig.class)
  .withConfiguration(AutoConfigurations.of(AuditAutoConfiguration.class))
  .run(ctx -> assertThat(ctx).getBean(AuditClient.class).isSameAs(custom));

ApplicationContextRunner tests conditions in isolation without booting an app — the right tool, and knowing it signals you've built one.

How to say it in an interview

“An @AutoConfiguration class guarded by conditions, properties bound with @ConfigurationProperties, registered in META-INF/spring/...AutoConfiguration.imports — which replaced spring.factories in Boot 3, and if you miss it nothing loads and there's no error. Every bean gets @ConditionalOnMissingBean so consumers can override, I ship the configuration processor for IDE completion, and I test the conditions with ApplicationContextRunner rather than booting a whole app.”

question: Filter vs Interceptor vs AOP — which do you use when?
answer:
Three layers, each seeing progressively more context:

  • Filter (Servlet API) — outermost. Sees every request including static resources and errors, before Spring MVC exists. It can wrap/replace the request and response. Use for: security, CORS, request logging, compression, correlation ids.
  • Interceptor (Spring MVC) — inside the DispatcherServlet, so it knows the handler method that will run. Use for: auth checks needing the target method, adding model attributes, timing controllers.
  • AOP aspect — around any Spring bean method, with typed arguments and the return value. Use for: transactions, caching, business-level logging, retries.

Rule of thumb: the further out, the broader the reach but the less context. Pick the innermost layer that has what you need.
tip: The decision: need the response stream or coverage of everything? Filter. Need to know which controller method? Interceptor. Need method arguments and return values on any bean? AOP.
deep:
The pipeline

request
  -> Filters (servlet container)         — sees static resources, errors, everything
     -> DispatcherServlet
        -> Interceptor.preHandle          — handler is known
           -> [AOP proxy] @Controller
              -> [AOP proxy] @Service     — @Transactional starts here
           <- Interceptor.postHandle      — BEFORE the view/body is written
     <- Interceptor.afterCompletion       — after the response is rendered
  <- Filters (unwind)

Always extend OncePerRequestFilter

A plain Filter can run multiple times per request — on a forward, an include, an async dispatch, or the error dispatch. Your "log every request" filter then logs three times, or a rate limiter counts one request as three. OncePerRequestFilter guards with a request attribute. It's the default choice for a reason.

MDC must be cleared in a finally

Threads are pooled. Leave a value in the MDC and the next request on that thread inherits it — you'll see request B logged with request A's correlation id, which is worse than no id at all because it's actively misleading during an incident. This exact leak is a great war-story detail.

Interceptors can't touch the response body

postHandle     — runs BEFORE the body is written; modifying it here does nothing
afterCompletion— response already committed; you can only observe

To modify a response body you need a Filter with a ContentCachingResponseWrapper, or a ResponseBodyAdvice for @ResponseBody returns. "How do I log the response body?" trips people for exactly this reason — and the answer requires wrapping, because a servlet stream can only be read once.

Reading the request body twice

Read the input stream in a filter and the controller gets an empty body — it's a one-shot stream. Wrap it in ContentCachingRequestWrapper to buffer it (and be aware you're now holding every request body in memory — cap the size).

Ordering

@Order(1)  // filters: lower runs first (outermost)
// Spring Security's chain sits at a specific order — register custom security
// filters relative to it with addFilterBefore/addFilterAfter, not @Order

Why exceptions escape @ControllerAdvice

An exception thrown in a filter is outside the DispatcherServlet, so no @ExceptionHandler sees it — you get the container's default error page. That's the same reason Spring Security auth failures need an AuthenticationEntryPoint. One pipeline diagram explains both.

How to say it in an interview

“Filters are servlet-level and see everything before Spring MVC — I use them for correlation ids and security. Interceptors run inside the DispatcherServlet and know the handler method, so they're for controller-scoped concerns. AOP wraps any bean method with typed args — transactions and caching. I always extend OncePerRequestFilter, because a plain filter re-runs on forwards and async dispatches, and I always clear the MDC in a finally block or the next request on that pooled thread inherits the previous correlation id.”

question: Why use DTOs instead of exposing entities, and how do you map them?
answer:
Never return JPA entities from a controller. Four concrete reasons:

  • Security — the entity has passwordHash, internalNotes, isFraudFlagged. Serialising it leaks them, and any new column is auto-exposed by default.
  • Coupling — your public API contract becomes your DB schema. Rename a column and you break every client.
  • Lazy loading — Jackson touches a lazy association after the session closed → LazyInitializationException, or worse, it silently loads the whole graph.
  • Recursion — bidirectional relationships serialise infinitely.

Mapping options: MapStruct (compile-time generated, fast, type-safe — the standard) or hand-written mappers/static factory methods on records. Avoid ModelMapper-style runtime reflection mapping — it's slow and fails at runtime rather than compile time.
tip: Also use a separate REQUEST DTO. Binding @RequestBody straight to an entity is mass assignment: a client posts {"role":"ADMIN"} and privilege-escalates. That's a genuine CVE class, not a style preference.
deep:
Mass assignment

@PostMapping("/users")
User create(@RequestBody User user) {    // entity bound directly
  return repo.save(user);
}

// attacker:
POST /users {"email":"a@b.com", "admin": true, "id": 1}
// -> Jackson happily sets admin=true. Privilege escalation, and it OVERWRITES user 1.

The request DTO simply has no admin field, so there's nothing to exploit. Allow-listing beats blocklisting: @JsonIgnore on the entity is a blocklist you must remember to update on every new field — and you will forget one.

MapStruct generates plain code

// you write an interface; MapStruct generates at COMPILE time:
@Component
public class UserMapperImpl implements UserMapper {
  public UserDto toDto(User u) {
    if (u == null) return null;
    return new UserDto(u.getId(), u.getEmail());
  }
}

No reflection, no runtime cost, and — the real win — a compile error if a field can't be mapped. Rename a field and the build fails immediately instead of a null appearing in production JSON. Reflection-based mappers give you the null.

Different DTOs per direction

CreateUserRequest   no id, has password, no admin flag
UpdateUserRequest   no password, no email (immutable)
UserDto             no password, has id and createdAt
UserSummaryDto      just id + name — for list endpoints

The instinct is one shared DTO for everything, but they have genuinely different fields and validation. One DTO with half its fields null depending on the operation is worse than four honest ones.

Map inside the transaction

@Transactional(readOnly = true)
public UserDto find(Long id) {
  User u = repo.findById(id).orElseThrow();
  return mapper.toDto(u);      // INSIDE — lazy fields resolve here if needed
}

Map in the controller and any lazy access throws (with open-in-view=false) or silently fires extra queries.

Or skip the entity entirely

@Query("select new com.example.UserDto(u.id, u.email) from User u where u.active = true")
List<UserDto> findActiveSummaries();

For read-only endpoints, project straight to the DTO: only the needed columns, no persistence context, no dirty checking, no lazy risk. Often the best-performing option.

The honest cost

DTOs are boilerplate, and for a tiny internal CRUD service the entity-exposing shortcut is a defensible trade-off. But the moment the API is public or the schema changes independently, DTOs pay for themselves. Acknowledging the trade-off rather than reciting dogma reads better.

How to say it in an interview

“Entities are internal — exposing them leaks fields like password hashes, couples the API to the schema, and drags lazy loading into serialisation. The one I'd emphasise is inbound: binding @RequestBody to an entity is mass assignment, so a client can post admin: true and escalate. I use separate request and response DTOs with MapStruct, which generates the mapping at compile time so a renamed field breaks the build instead of silently producing a null.”

question: How does JSON serialization work, and how do you customise it?
answer:
Boot auto-configures a Jackson ObjectMapper. @RequestBody/@ResponseBody use MappingJackson2HttpMessageConverter to convert between JSON and objects.

Customise at three levels:

  • Properties — spring.jackson.* for global defaults (date format, null inclusion, unknown-property handling).
  • Jackson2ObjectMapperBuilderCustomizer — the right bean to customise globally without discarding Boot's setup.
  • Annotations — @JsonProperty, @JsonIgnore, @JsonFormat, @JsonInclude, or a custom serializer.

Key point: defining your own ObjectMapper bean replaces Boot's entirely, silently losing its modules (JavaTimeModule, sensible defaults). Use the customizer instead.
tip: Replacing the ObjectMapper bean is the classic self-inflicted bug — dates suddenly serialise as {"year":2026,"month":...} because JavaTimeModule is gone. Use Jackson2ObjectMapperBuilderCustomizer.
deep:
Dates: always ISO-8601

write-dates-as-timestamps: true   -> 1735689600000   (unreadable, no zone)
write-dates-as-timestamps: false  -> "2026-01-01T00:00:00Z"  <- this

Boot registers JavaTimeModule and disables timestamps by default, so Instant/LocalDateTime serialise as ISO strings. Replace the ObjectMapper without it and you get either epoch millis or the infamous exploded object. Also: prefer Instant/OffsetDateTime over LocalDateTime in APIs — LocalDateTime has no timezone, so "14:00" is meaningless across regions.

Be strict outbound, lenient inbound

FAIL_ON_UNKNOWN_PROPERTIES defaults to false in Boot (Jackson's own default is true). Lenient is usually right: a client adding a field shouldn't 400 — that's Postel's law and it's what makes independent deployment possible. The counter-argument: a typo'd field name is then silently ignored, so {"amont": 100} becomes a null amount rather than an error. For financial/critical payloads, strict is safer.

Records need no annotations

Jackson supports records natively (2.12+), using the canonical constructor. No @JsonCreator, no getters, no Lombok. Records are the natural DTO in modern Boot.

Bidirectional recursion

class Order  { List<Item> items; }
class Item   { Order order; }        // -> StackOverflowError on serialize

@JsonManagedReference / @JsonBackReference   // one side is skipped
@JsonIdentityInfo(generator = IntSequenceGenerator.class)  // emits ids instead
@JsonIgnore                                  // simplest — cut the back-ref

All of which is a symptom of serialising entities. DTOs don't have back-references, so the problem evaporates.

Custom serializer

public class MoneySerializer extends JsonSerializer<Money> {
  public void serialize(Money m, JsonGenerator gen, SerializerProvider p) throws IOException {
    gen.writeString(m.amount().toPlainString() + " " + m.currency());
  }
}

record Price(@JsonSerialize(using = MoneySerializer.class) Money value) {}

BigDecimal, not double

double  0.1 + 0.2 == 0.30000000000000004    // never for money
BigDecimal — exact; serialises as a JSON number
// consider writing money as a STRING — JS parses JSON numbers as double
// and silently loses precision on large values (> 2^53)

That last point is real: a JavaScript client receiving a large long id as a JSON number corrupts it. Serialising ids/money as strings avoids it — a detail that reads as battle-tested.

@JsonView for shaping

interface Views { interface Public {} interface Internal extends Public {} }

record UserDto(@JsonView(Views.Public.class) String name,
               @JsonView(Views.Internal.class) String email) {}

@JsonView(Views.Public.class)
@GetMapping("/users") List<UserDto> publicList() { }

How to say it in an interview

“Boot auto-configures the ObjectMapper and MVC uses it via message converters. I customise with spring.jackson.* or a Jackson2ObjectMapperBuilderCustomizer — never by defining my own ObjectMapper bean, because that silently drops Boot's modules and suddenly dates serialise as epoch millis. I keep dates ISO-8601 with Instant, use records as DTOs since Jackson handles them natively, and I'm lenient on unknown incoming fields so clients can evolve independently.”

question: What changed in Spring Boot 3, and what's involved in migrating?
answer:
The headline breaking change is Java EE → Jakarta EE: every javax.* package became jakarta.* (javax.persistence → jakarta.persistence, javax.validation → jakarta.validation). It's a package rename, so it's mechanical in your own code — the pain is third-party libraries that haven't published Jakarta-compatible versions.

Also: Java 17 minimum (21 recommended), Spring Framework 6, Hibernate 6, spring.factories auto-configuration registration replaced by AutoConfiguration.imports, Spring Cloud Sleuth replaced by Micrometer Tracing, trailing-slash matching removed, and GraalVM native image support becoming first-class.

Boot 3.x additions worth knowing: virtual threads (3.2), RestClient (3.2), @ServiceConnection for Testcontainers (3.1), ProblemDetail (3.0), structured logging (3.4).
tip: The realistic migration answer: use OpenRewrite to do the mechanical rename, then spend most of the effort on dependency upgrades and Hibernate 6 behaviour changes. Saying 'find/replace javax' understates it badly.
deep:
Why the rename happened

Oracle donated Java EE to the Eclipse Foundation but kept the javax trademark — so Jakarta EE could not evolve those packages and had to rename them wholesale. Pure legal history, zero technical merit, and it fractured the ecosystem for two years. Knowing why is a nice touch.

The real migration cost

your code        mechanical — OpenRewrite does it in one command
your dependencies HARD — an abandoned library on javax simply won't work
                  (no bytecode-level fix short of the Eclipse Transformer)
Hibernate 6      behaviour changes: different SQL generation, stricter type
                  handling, changed ID generation defaults (allocationSize!)
Security 6       antMatchers() -> requestMatchers(); WebSecurityConfigurer-
                  Adapter is GONE — component-based config only

The Hibernate 6 sequence trap

GenerationType.AUTO/SEQUENCE semantics changed: Hibernate 6 honours allocationSize (default 50) consistently, where Boot 2 often behaved as if it were 1. Post-upgrade, ids jump in blocks of 50 — harmless, but alarming — and if the DB sequence's INCREMENT BY doesn't match allocationSize, you get duplicate key violations. A genuinely nasty one that bites real migrations.

Security 6 config

// Boot 2 — deprecated then removed
class Config extends WebSecurityConfigurerAdapter {
  protected void configure(HttpSecurity http) { http.antMatchers("/x").permitAll(); }
}

// Boot 3 — a bean, and lambda DSL
@Bean
SecurityFilterChain chain(HttpSecurity http) throws Exception {
  return http.authorizeHttpRequests(a -> a.requestMatchers("/x").permitAll()).build();
}

Trailing slash

/api/users and /api/users/ used to both match; in Boot 3 the trailing-slash variant 404s. If clients rely on it, you'll break them silently in production — a small change with a big blast radius.

Strategy

Go to the latest Boot 2.7 first, fix every deprecation warning (2.7 was designed as the stepping stone), then jump. Upgrading Java and Boot simultaneously doubles the variables when something breaks. And check every dependency for a Jakarta-ready release before starting — that's usually what determines whether the migration is a week or a quarter.

How to say it in an interview

“The big one is the javax to jakarta rename, plus Java 17 minimum, Spring 6, Hibernate 6, and Security 6 dropping WebSecurityConfigurerAdapter. My own code is mechanical — OpenRewrite handles it — so the real cost is third-party libraries that never shipped a Jakarta build, and Hibernate 6 behaviour changes like allocationSize now being honoured, which can cause duplicate keys. I'd go to 2.7 first, clear all deprecations, then upgrade.”

question: How do you improve Spring Boot startup time and memory footprint?
answer:
First, measure: run with --debug for the condition report, and use ApplicationStartup / BufferingApplicationStartup with /actuator/startup to see exactly where the time goes.

Then, in rough order of payoff:

  • Exclude unused auto-configurations and narrow scanBasePackages — less classpath scanning.
  • Trim dependencies — every starter adds auto-configurations to evaluate.
  • Lazy initialisation (spring.main.lazy-initialization=true) — beans created on first use. Great for dev, risky in prod (it defers failures and moves the cost to the first request).
  • CDS / AppCDS — share a class archive; Boot 3.3 makes this easy for a solid ~20–40% win with no code change.
  • GraalVM native image — the nuclear option: ~50ms startup and a fraction of the memory.
tip: For a normal server app, startup time barely matters — it's a Kubernetes rolling-deploy and scale-up concern, and it's decisive for serverless. Say 'measure first with /actuator/startup' rather than guessing.
deep:
Where the time actually goes

JVM + classloading            ~1s
component scanning            grows with package breadth
auto-config condition eval    ~100+ classes to evaluate
bean instantiation            the bulk of it
Hibernate entity scanning     often the single biggest chunk
connection pool warm-up       minimum-idle connections opened eagerly

Guessing is wasted effort — /actuator/startup gives per-step timings.

Lazy init: understand the trade-off

It defers bean creation, so startup is fast — but the work reappears on the first request that needs each bean (nasty p99 spikes after every deploy), and, more importantly, configuration errors no longer fail at startup. A missing bean or bad property that would have failed the deploy now throws at 3am on a rare code path. That's a bad trade in production and a great one in dev.

AOT and native image

JVM             startup ~2-4s    RSS ~300MB-1GB   peak throughput: best (JIT)
JVM + AppCDS    startup ~1.5-3s  RSS similar      no code change
Native image    startup ~50ms    RSS ~50-100MB    throughput: somewhat lower

Native compiles ahead of time, resolving beans at build time. The costs are real: build times of several minutes, no runtime reflection unless registered (@RegisterReflectionForBinding), no dynamic proxies unless known at build time, no runtime profile switching (profiles must be set at build), and awkward debugging. Worth it for serverless and high-density deployments; usually not worth it for a long-running service where a few seconds of startup is irrelevant.

Memory: the JVM defaults betray containers

-XX:MaxRAMPercentage=75.0    # use 75% of the CONTAINER limit, not the host's

Modern JVMs are container-aware, but the default max heap of ~25% of RAM wastes most of a small container. Set MaxRAMPercentage explicitly. And heap isn't the whole picture — RSS includes metaspace, thread stacks (~1MB each!), code cache, and direct buffers, which is why a 512MB heap limit still gets OOM-killed at a 768MB container limit. That gap is what surprises people.

Don't optimise the wrong thing

For a service that runs for weeks, a 4-second startup is irrelevant — chasing it is premature optimisation. It matters when: scaling up under a traffic spike (slow pods = the spike is over before you're ready), rolling deploys, local dev iteration, and serverless cold starts. Naming when it matters is a better answer than a list of flags.

How to say it in an interview

“Measure first with /actuator/startup and the condition report rather than guessing — it's usually entity scanning or auto-configurations I don't need. Then trim dependencies, narrow the scan, and consider CDS for an easy 20–40%. I'd avoid lazy init in prod because it defers configuration errors from the deploy to a random request later. Native image is the big lever — 50ms startup — but you pay in build time and reflection config, so I'd only reach for it for serverless. And in containers I set MaxRAMPercentage, since the default wastes most of a small container.”

question: What is the IoC container? BeanFactory vs ApplicationContext.
answer:
Inversion of Control means the framework, not your code, creates objects and wires them together. The container reads bean definitions, resolves the dependency graph, and injects collaborators. DI is the mechanism; IoC is the principle.

  • BeanFactory — the basic container: bean instantiation and wiring, lazy by default.
  • ApplicationContext — a superset and what you always actually use: it adds event publishing, i18n, resource loading, BeanPostProcessor/BeanFactoryPostProcessor auto-registration, annotation config, and AOP integration. It eagerly instantiates singletons at startup.

That eagerness is the point: configuration errors fail the deploy rather than the first request that touches a broken bean.
tip: The 'why does it matter' answer: eager singleton creation means a missing bean or bad config crashes at startup — fail fast. That's the practical difference, not the API surface.
deep:
Why IoC is worth it

// without IoC — the class picks its own collaborators
class OrderService {
  private final PaymentGateway gw = new StripeGateway("sk_live_...");
  // - can't test without hitting Stripe
  // - can't swap for a sandbox in dev
  // - the secret is compiled into the class
}

// with IoC — the class declares what it needs
class OrderService {
  private final PaymentGateway gw;
  OrderService(PaymentGateway gw) { this.gw = gw; }   // container decides which
}

The class depends on an abstraction and someone else chooses the implementation — that's the dependency inversion principle, and it's what makes the code testable and environment-aware.

Context hierarchy

ApplicationContext
 ├─ AnnotationConfigApplicationContext   (@Configuration classes)
 ├─ ClassPathXmlApplicationContext       (legacy XML)
 └─ WebApplicationContext
     └─ AnnotationConfigServletWebServerApplicationContext  <- what Boot creates

Eager vs lazy, and why eager wins

BeanFactory        bean created on getBean()  -> a typo'd @Value surfaces
                                                 whenever that bean is first used
ApplicationContext singletons created at startup -> the same typo fails the deploy

This is why spring.main.lazy-initialization=true is a dev-only convenience: it trades fail-fast for startup speed. Prototypes are always lazy — they're created on request by definition.

getBean() is a smell

ctx.getBean(UserService.class);   // service locator — the anti-pattern IoC replaced

Calling getBean in application code puts you back to hidden dependencies and untestable classes. Legitimate uses are narrow: framework/library code, or genuinely dynamic lookup — and even then ObjectProvider is usually better.

BeanFactoryPostProcessor vs BeanPostProcessor

BeanFactoryPostProcessor  runs on bean DEFINITIONS, before any bean exists
                          e.g. PropertySourcesPlaceholderConfigurer (${...})
BeanPostProcessor         runs on bean INSTANCES, around initialisation
                          e.g. AutowiredAnnotationBeanPostProcessor,
                               AbstractAutoProxyCreator (AOP proxies!)

Definitions first, then instances. Nearly every piece of Spring "magic" — @Autowired, @Value, @Transactional proxies — is one of these two extension points. Being able to say that shows you understand the framework rather than just its annotations.

How to say it in an interview

“IoC means the container owns object creation and wiring instead of my classes doing it, so they depend on abstractions and stay testable. BeanFactory is the bare, lazy container; ApplicationContext is what Boot gives you — it adds events, i18n, post-processors, and eagerly creates singletons at startup, so bad config fails the deploy rather than a request later. Under the hood most of Spring's magic is BeanPostProcessors — that's where AOP proxies get created.”

question: What causes a circular dependency and how do you fix it?
answer:
Bean A needs B and B needs A. With constructor injection it's genuinely impossible — neither can be built first — so Spring fails at startup with a clear cycle report. Since Boot 2.6 this is the default behaviour: circular references are prohibited unless you explicitly re-enable them.

The right fix is almost never a Spring trick — it's a design signal. A cycle means the responsibilities are wrong. Options, best first:

  • Extract the shared logic into a third bean both depend on.
  • Invert one direction with an event — A publishes, B listens; the compile-time edge disappears.
  • @Lazy on one injection point — a proxy defers the real resolution.
  • spring.main.allow-circular-references=true — a temporary escape hatch, not a fix.
tip: Don't answer with '@Lazy'. Answer with 'a cycle means my design is wrong, so I'd extract the shared responsibility' — then mention @Lazy as the tactical escape. Interviewers are testing design instinct here.
deep:
Why field injection hides it

@Service class A { @Autowired B b; }
@Service class B { @Autowired A a; }
// This USED to work: Spring constructs A (empty), constructs B, injects A's
// half-built reference into B, then injects B into A. Both exist. Cycle "solved".

Spring papered over it using the three-level cache of early bean references — but only because field injection lets an object exist before its dependencies are set. Constructor injection makes the cycle a hard error, which is the honest outcome. This is one of the strongest arguments for constructor injection: it surfaces design problems the compiler can't.

Why Boot 2.6 banned it by default

Circular references produce fragile startup ordering, break with @Async/AOP proxying in confusing ways (you can get a raw, un-proxied instance injected — so @Transactional silently stops working on it), and almost always indicate a design flaw. The allow-circular-references flag exists purely for legacy migration.

How @Lazy works here

@Service
class UserService {
  UserService(@Lazy OrderService orders) { this.orders = orders; }
  // injects a PROXY; the real OrderService is resolved on first method call,
  // by which time both beans exist
}

It works, but it's deferring the problem, not solving it — the cycle is still there in the design, now invisible to the next reader.

The real diagnosis

A cycle usually means one of:

  • A misplaced responsibility. UserService sending order confirmation emails is order logic living in the wrong class — move it, and the edge vanishes.
  • A missing abstraction. Both need something neither should own — extract it.
  • Wrong layering. A lower layer reaching back up to a higher one. Invert it with an interface owned by the lower layer, or an event.

Self-reference is legal

@Service
class OrderService {
  @Lazy private final OrderService self;   // deliberate — to cross the proxy
  // this is the self-invocation workaround for @Transactional
}

Spring permits a bean depending on itself with @Lazy, which is exactly the self-injection trick for proxy-crossing.

How to say it in an interview

“Two beans requiring each other via constructors can't be built, so Boot fails at startup — and since 2.6 it's prohibited by default. I treat it as a design smell rather than something to work around: usually a responsibility is in the wrong class, so I extract the shared piece into a third bean or invert one direction with an event. @Lazy works but just hides it. It's also a good argument for constructor injection — field injection lets Spring paper over the cycle, so the design flaw stays invisible.”

question: How does the embedded server work, and what is a fat jar?
answer:
Instead of deploying a WAR into an installed Tomcat, Boot puts the servlet container inside your application as a library. SpringApplication.run starts it programmatically, so the app is a self-contained process: java -jar app.jar.

The fat (uber) jar packages your classes plus every dependency jar. Boot uses a nested-jar layout with its own JarLauncher as the manifest Main-Class, because standard Java can't load a jar-inside-a-jar.

Why it matters: the artifact is the deployable. No container to install, version, or configure; identical execution locally, in CI, and in prod. It's also what makes Boot fit containers so naturally — FROM eclipse-temurin:21-jre plus one jar.
tip: Swap Tomcat for Undertow/Jetty by excluding one starter and adding another — auto-configuration follows the classpath. That's the clearest demonstration of how starters and auto-config work together.
code:
<plugin>
  <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
  <artifactId>spring-boot-maven-plugin</artifactId>
</plugin>
<!-- mvn package -> app.jar with a nested BOOT-INF/lib/*.jar layout -->

<!-- app.jar
     ├── META-INF/MANIFEST.MF        Main-Class: org.sf.boot.loader.JarLauncher
     │                               Start-Class: com.example.App
     ├── org/springframework/boot/loader/   (the launcher)
     └── BOOT-INF/
         ├── classes/                (your code)
         └── lib/                    (every dependency jar, nested)
-->
deep:
Why a custom launcher

The JVM's classloader cannot read a jar nested inside a jar. Boot's JarLauncher installs a custom LaunchedURLClassLoader that can, then hands off to your Start-Class. That's why Main-Class in the manifest isn't your class — a detail that surprises people the first time they look.

Note this differs from a shaded jar (which flattens everything into one namespace and can collide on duplicate resources); Boot's nesting keeps each dependency jar intact and signed.

Layered jars for Docker

FROM eclipse-temurin:21-jre AS builder
COPY app.jar .
RUN java -Djarmode=layertools -jar app.jar extract

FROM eclipse-temurin:21-jre
COPY --from=builder dependencies/ ./          # changes rarely  -> cached layer
COPY --from=builder spring-boot-loader/ ./
COPY --from=builder snapshot-dependencies/ ./
COPY --from=builder application/ ./           # changes every build -> tiny layer
ENTRYPOINT ["java", "org.springframework.boot.loader.launch.JarLauncher"]

Copying the whole 60MB fat jar into one Docker layer means every one-line code change pushes 60MB. Layering separates rarely-changing dependencies from your ~200KB of classes, so rebuilds and pulls are near-instant. This is a strong practical detail.

Swapping the container

<dependency>
  <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-web</artifactId>
  <exclusions><exclusion>
    <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-tomcat</artifactId>
  </exclusion></exclusions>
</dependency>
<dependency><artifactId>spring-boot-starter-undertow</artifactId></dependency>

No code change: @ConditionalOnClass sees Undertow instead of Tomcat and configures it. Tomcat is the default and fine for almost everything; Undertow is lighter on memory; Netty is used by WebFlux.

Tuning it

server:
  port: 8080
  tomcat:
    threads:
      max: 200            # max concurrent requests (irrelevant w/ virtual threads)
      min-spare: 10
    accept-count: 100     # OS-level queue once all threads are busy
    max-connections: 8192
  compression:
    enabled: true

accept-count is the subtle one: when all 200 threads are busy, further connections queue here — and when that fills, the OS refuses connections and clients see connection-refused rather than a slow response.

Can you still deploy a WAR?

Yes — extend SpringBootServletInitializer, set packaging to war, and mark Tomcat provided. Only relevant for legacy corporate environments that mandate an app server; the ecosystem has moved to jars + containers.

How to say it in an interview

“The servlet container is a library inside the app, so the jar is the deployable — java -jar, no external Tomcat to install or version. It's a nested-jar layout with Boot's JarLauncher as the manifest main class, since the JVM can't load jars inside jars. In Docker I use layered jars so dependencies and application classes land in separate layers — otherwise every one-line change rebuilds and pushes the whole 60MB.”

question: How do you run code at startup?
answer:
Several hooks, each firing at a different point:

  • CommandLineRunner / ApplicationRunner — run after the context is fully initialised. The difference is only the argument type: raw String... vs parsed ApplicationArguments (which understands --key=value options).
  • @EventListener(ApplicationReadyEvent.class) — after runners, when the app is genuinely ready to serve traffic.
  • @PostConstruct — per-bean, right after that bean is initialised. Too early for anything needing proxies or other beans to be ready.

For anything that touches the database, calls a service, or needs @Transactional, use a runner or ApplicationReadyEvent — never @PostConstruct, where AOP proxies don't exist yet.
tip: The distinction that matters: @PostConstruct runs before the bean is proxied, so @Transactional/@Async inside it silently do nothing. Runners run after the whole context is built, so everything works.
deep:
Startup order

1. context refresh: beans created, @PostConstruct, proxies applied
2. embedded server starts (port opens)
3. ApplicationStartedEvent
4. ApplicationRunner / CommandLineRunner   (in @Order)
5. ApplicationReadyEvent                   <- readiness probe flips to UP
6. ApplicationFailedEvent (only if something threw)

Note the server is already accepting connections at step 2 — so a slow runner means requests arrive while your warm-up is still going. If ordering matters, use the readiness probe: it only reports UP after ApplicationReadyEvent, so Kubernetes won't route traffic until your runners finish. That interaction is the genuinely useful detail here.

A throwing runner kills the app

@Override
public void run(String... args) {
  throw new IllegalStateException("seed failed");
}
// -> the application context closes and the JVM exits with a non-zero code

Usually desirable (fail fast on bad state), but it means a runner doing something optional — like warming a cache from a flaky service — will take down your app on a transient error. Wrap optional work in try/catch.

ApplicationRunner vs CommandLineRunner

// java -jar app.jar --mode=batch file1.csv

CommandLineRunner: args = ["--mode=batch", "file1.csv"]   // raw, parse yourself
ApplicationRunner: args.getOptionValues("mode") -> ["batch"]
                   args.getNonOptionArgs()      -> ["file1.csv"]

ApplicationRunner is the better default — the parsing is done for you.

Seeding data properly

Runners are a common way to seed dev data, but guard them so they can't fire in prod:

@Component
@Profile("dev")                              // or @ConditionalOnProperty
class DevDataSeeder implements ApplicationRunner { }

For real reference data, use a Flyway migration instead — it's versioned, runs once, and is recorded. A runner re-executes on every restart and needs its own idempotency.

Batch/CLI apps

@SpringBootApplication
public class Job implements CommandLineRunner {
  public static void main(String[] a) { System.exit(SpringApplication.exit(SpringApplication.run(Job.class, a))); }
  public void run(String... args) { /* do the work, then exit */ }
}
// with spring.main.web-application-type=none

SpringApplication.exit propagates an ExitCodeGenerator's code — that's how you make a Boot batch job report failure to a scheduler.

How to say it in an interview

“ApplicationRunner or ApplicationReadyEvent for anything real, because they fire after the context is fully built and proxied. I avoid @PostConstruct for that work — it runs before AOP proxies exist, so @Transactional in there silently does nothing. Worth knowing that the server opens its port before runners execute, so if the warm-up must finish first you rely on the readiness probe, which only flips UP after ApplicationReadyEvent.”

question: How does logging work in Spring Boot?
answer:
Boot uses SLF4J as the API and Logback as the default implementation (via spring-boot-starter-logging, included in every starter). You code against SLF4J, so the backend is swappable — spring-boot-starter-log4j2 replaces it without touching code.

Configure levels per package in properties, or drop in logback-spring.xml for full control. Use logback-spring.xml rather than logback.xml — the Boot-prefixed name is loaded by Boot, so it can use <springProfile> and <springProperty>.

Two rules that matter more than config: always use parameterised messages ({}) not string concatenation, and never log secrets or PII.
tip: log.debug("user " + user) builds the string even when debug is off. log.debug("user {}", user) doesn't. In a hot path that's a real, measurable cost for zero benefit.
deep:
Parameterised logging

log.debug("order " + order);        // toString() ALWAYS runs, even at INFO level
log.debug("order {}", order);       // toString() only runs if DEBUG is enabled

The concatenation happens before the call, so the work is done and thrown away. In a hot loop with a large object's toString(), that's meaningful CPU burned for output nobody sees.

The exception goes last, with no placeholder

log.error("failed for {}", id, e);      // correct — full stack trace
log.error("failed for {} {}", id, e);   // WRONG — logs e.toString(), NO stack trace

SLF4J special-cases a trailing Throwable. Giving it a placeholder demotes it to an ordinary argument and you lose the stack trace — the exact thing you needed. Extremely common bug.

Never log secrets or PII

log.info("login attempt: {}", request);   // request contains the password!
log.debug("headers: {}", headers);        // Authorization: Bearer ...

Logs are usually far less protected than the database, are shipped to third-party aggregators, and are read by many people. A password or token in a log is a breach — and under GDPR, logged personal data is subject to deletion requests you can't easily honour in an append-only log store.

Profile-specific logging

<!-- logback-spring.xml -->
<springProfile name="dev">
  <root level="DEBUG"><appender-ref ref="CONSOLE"/></root>
</springProfile>
<springProfile name="prod">
  <root level="INFO"><appender-ref ref="JSON"/></root>
</springProfile>

Only works in logback-spring.xml — Logback loads plain logback.xml too early for Spring to have any say.

Changing levels at runtime

curl -X POST localhost:8080/actuator/loggers/com.example.orders \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"configuredLevel":"DEBUG"}'

Debug a live incident without a redeploy, then set it back to null to inherit again. One of Actuator's most useful features.

Log to stdout in containers

Don't write files inside a container: the disk is ephemeral, rotation is someone else's problem, and it breaks the twelve-factor contract. Log JSON to stdout and let the platform (Fluentd/Loki/CloudWatch) collect it. Boot 3.4 has native structured logging: logging.structured.format.console=ecs.

Levels, used correctly

ERROR  something broke that needs human attention — should be alertable
WARN   recoverable/unexpected but handled (a retry, a fallback fired)
INFO   significant business events — startup, an order placed
DEBUG  diagnostic detail, off in prod
TRACE  firehose

The most common failure is logging every handled exception at ERROR, which makes ERROR meaningless and trains everyone to ignore the alert.

How to say it in an interview

“SLF4J as the facade with Logback behind it, levels per package, and logback-spring.xml when I need profile-specific appenders. I always use {} placeholders so the message isn't built when the level is off, and I pass the exception as the last argument with no placeholder — with one, you lose the stack trace. In containers I log JSON to stdout with the trace id in the MDC, and I'm strict about never logging tokens or PII.”

question: What does Spring Boot DevTools do?
answer:
Developer-experience tooling, active only at development time:

  • Automatic restart — the context restarts when classpath files change (much faster than a manual restart thanks to a two-classloader trick).
  • LiveReload — an embedded server that refreshes the browser automatically.
  • Sensible dev defaults — disables template and static-resource caching, so a Thymeleaf edit shows up on refresh.
  • Global settings in ~/.config/spring-boot/ shared across projects.

Crucially, it disables itself automatically when running from a fat jar (java -jar) or a fully-packaged app, and the Maven/Gradle plugin marks it non-transitive so it never leaks into downstream projects.
tip: The restart speed trick is worth knowing: two classloaders — one for jars (never reloaded), one for your classes (thrown away and rebuilt). Your code changes; the 200 dependency jars don't get re-read.
code:
<dependency>
  <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
  <artifactId>spring-boot-devtools</artifactId>
  <optional>true</optional>          <!-- non-transitive -->
  <scope>runtime</scope>
</dependency>

# tuning
spring:
  devtools:
    restart:
      enabled: true
      exclude: static/**,public/**   # asset changes shouldn't restart
      poll-interval: 2s
    livereload:
      enabled: true
deep:
The two-classloader restart

base classloader     -> all the dependency jars (loaded once, kept)
restart classloader  -> YOUR classes (discarded and recreated on change)

A cold start re-reads and re-verifies every dependency jar; DevTools only rebuilds the small classloader holding your code. That's why a restart is ~1s rather than ~5s. It's a genuine restart though, not hot-swapping — state is lost. (JRebel does true hot-swap; it's commercial.)

It's a restart, so triggers matter

DevTools watches the classpath — i.e. compiled output, not source files. In IntelliJ nothing happens until the class is actually compiled: enable "build project automatically" plus auto-make on frame deactivation, or hit build. "DevTools isn't working" is nearly always this.

Automatic disabling

java -jar app.jar        -> DevTools disabled (it detects the launcher)
mvn spring-boot:run      -> enabled
IDE run                  -> enabled

So a production jar is unaffected even if the dependency is present. Belt and braces: <optional>true</optional> stops it propagating to anyone depending on your artifact, and you can force it off with -Dspring.devtools.restart.enabled=false.

The security caveat

DevTools also enables a remote debug/update tunnel if you configure spring.devtools.remote.secret. That feature lets you push code to a running remote app — genuinely dangerous, and the docs are explicit that it must never be enabled in production. It's the one part of DevTools with a real security footprint.

Dev-time property defaults

DevTools quietly flips a set of caching properties: Thymeleaf/Freemarker cache off, static resource cache period 0, H2 console on, and more logging. Handy — but it means "it worked in dev" can hide a caching bug that only appears in prod. Worth knowing when template caching suddenly bites you after deploy.

Alternatives

DevTools            free, restart-based, ~1s, loses state
Spring Loaded       dead
JRebel              commercial, true hot-swap, keeps state
native IDE hotswap  method bodies only — no new methods/fields

How to say it in an interview

“Auto-restart on classpath change, LiveReload, and dev-friendly defaults like disabling template caching. The restart is fast because it uses two classloaders — dependencies stay loaded and only my classes get rebuilt. It disables itself when run from a fat jar, and I mark it optional so it can't leak downstream. The one thing to be careful about is the remote update tunnel — that must never be enabled outside dev.”

question: How do you document a REST API?
answer:
springdoc-openapi — add the starter and it introspects your controllers, DTOs, and validation annotations to generate an OpenAPI 3 spec at /v3/api-docs, plus a Swagger UI at /swagger-ui.html. (SpringFox is dead — it never properly supported Boot 3.)

Most of it is inferred: return types become response schemas, @PathVariable/@RequestParam become parameters, and @NotNull/@Size become schema constraints. You add @Operation, @ApiResponse, and @Schema for the intent the code can't express — error cases, examples, descriptions.

The real value isn't the pretty UI: it's a machine-readable contract you can generate clients from, diff for breaking changes in CI, and import into Postman.
tip: Because it's generated from the code, it can't drift out of date — that's the whole argument over a hand-written wiki page. And returning ResponseEntity<?> erases the type, so your docs show an empty schema.
deep:
Lock it down in prod

springdoc:
  api-docs:
    enabled: false          # prod — don't publish your full API surface
  swagger-ui:
    enabled: false

An exposed Swagger UI hands an attacker a complete, interactive map of every endpoint, parameter, and schema — free reconnaissance. Either disable it in prod, restrict it to an internal network, or put it behind auth. For a public API it's intentional; for an internal one it's an information leak.

ResponseEntity<?> destroys the docs

ResponseEntity<?> get(...)          // schema: {} — useless
ResponseEntity<OrderDto> get(...)   // schema: OrderDto — correct

The wildcard erases the type at runtime, so springdoc has nothing to introspect. Another good reason to return the concrete type or a properly-typed ResponseEntity.

Code-first vs design-first

code-first    annotate controllers -> generate the spec
              fast; the spec always matches the code
              BUT: the API is designed by accident, one endpoint at a time,
              and consumers can't start until you've written it

design-first  write the OpenAPI spec -> generate server stubs + client SDKs
              the contract is agreed up front; frontend and backend work in
              parallel against a mock; breaking changes are visible in a diff
              BUT: more ceremony, and the spec can drift from the impl

Design-first with openapi-generator is the stronger answer for a public or multi-team API. Being able to compare the two — rather than assuming code-first is the only way — is what marks experience.

Catch breaking changes in CI

# fail the build if the API breaks its consumers
openapi-diff old-spec.json new-spec.json --fail-on-incompatible

Removing a field, tightening a type, or adding a required parameter breaks clients silently. A spec diff in the pipeline turns that into a failed build. This is the point where OpenAPI stops being documentation and becomes a contract.

Generate clients, don't hand-write them

From the spec, openapi-generator emits a typed client for TypeScript/Angular, Java, Python — so a field rename in the backend becomes a compile error in the frontend rather than a runtime undefined. Given an Angular background, that's a very relevant point to make.

Group multiple APIs

@Bean GroupedOpenApi publicApi() {
  return GroupedOpenApi.builder().group("public").pathsToMatch("/api/public/**").build();
}
@Bean GroupedOpenApi adminApi() {
  return GroupedOpenApi.builder().group("admin").pathsToMatch("/api/admin/**").build();
}

How to say it in an interview

“springdoc-openapi, since SpringFox never made it to Boot 3 — it generates an OpenAPI 3 spec from the controllers and validation annotations, so it can't drift from the code. The value is the machine-readable contract more than the UI: I generate typed clients from it and diff the spec in CI so a removed field fails the build instead of breaking consumers silently. I disable the UI in prod, though — it's a complete map of the attack surface.”

question: How do you consume Kafka messages in Spring Boot, and how do you handle failures?
answer:
@KafkaListener on a method; Spring Kafka manages the consumer, polling, and offset commits. Publish with KafkaTemplate.

The critical concepts:

  • Offsets — the consumer's position. Committing marks a message processed. Auto-commit is dangerous: it commits on a timer, so a crash mid-processing loses messages.
  • Consumer groups — each partition goes to exactly one consumer in the group, so parallelism is capped by partition count.
  • Ordering is only guaranteed within a partition — so messages that must stay ordered need the same key.

For failures: a DefaultErrorHandler with exponential backoff, and a dead letter topic for what won't ever succeed — otherwise a single poison message blocks its partition forever.
tip: The poison-message question is the one they want: a message that always throws will retry forever and block that partition — every message behind it stalls. A DLT is not optional.
deep:
Turn off auto-commit

spring:
  kafka:
    consumer:
      enable-auto-commit: false
      auto-offset-reset: earliest
    listener:
      ack-mode: MANUAL_IMMEDIATE    # or RECORD — commit after each success

With auto-commit, offsets advance on a 5s timer regardless of whether processing succeeded. Crash after the commit but before finishing and the message is gone — silent data loss. Manual ack after successful processing gives at-least-once.

At-least-once means duplicates

process(msg);      // succeeds
// crash HERE, before ack
// -> on restart the message is redelivered and processed AGAIN

There is no way to make processing and offset-committing atomic across two systems. So consumers must be idempotent — dedupe on an event id, or make the operation naturally idempotent. "Exactly-once" via Kafka transactions only holds when the output is also Kafka; the moment you write to a database or call an API, it's at-least-once plus idempotency.

Partitions cap your parallelism

topic with 3 partitions, consumer group with 5 instances
  -> 3 consumers get 1 partition each
  -> 2 sit COMPLETELY IDLE, forever

Scaling consumers past the partition count does nothing. And you can add partitions but never remove them — and adding them breaks key-based ordering, because the hash-to-partition mapping changes and a key's messages land somewhere new. So partition count is a decision to get roughly right up front.

Ordering and keys

send("orders", order.customerId(), event);   // same key -> same partition -> ordered

Without a key, messages round-robin across partitions and OrderCreated can be processed after OrderCancelled. Keying by the entity id keeps a single entity's events ordered while still parallelising across entities. Note concurrency = "3" gives 3 threads, one per partition — ordering still holds per partition.

The poison message

Without an error handler, a message that always throws is retried forever, and because the offset never advances, everything behind it on that partition stops. One malformed payload can halt a whole shard of your pipeline. The DLT is the release valve: retry a few times with backoff, then publish to orders.DLT, commit, and move on. Then alert on DLT depth — a silently-filling DLT is just a slower data loss.

Distinguish retryable from not: a network blip is worth retrying; a validation failure will never succeed and should go straight to the DLT.

Rebalancing and max.poll.interval

max.poll.interval.ms (default 5 min)

If processing a batch takes longer than this, the broker assumes the consumer is dead, kicks it from the group, and rebalances — the work is redone by someone else, and can loop forever. Slow processing (an HTTP call per message) needs either a longer interval, a smaller max.poll.records, or offloading the work.

Don't publish inside a transaction

Same dual-write problem as always: repo.save() then kafka.send() isn't atomic. Use the transactional outbox.

How to say it in an interview

“@KafkaListener with manual acks — I turn off auto-commit, because it advances offsets on a timer and a crash mid-processing loses the message. That gives at-least-once, so consumers have to be idempotent. Parallelism is capped by partitions, and ordering only holds within a partition, so anything order-sensitive shares a key. The failure case I'd design for first is the poison message: without a DLT and backoff, one bad message retries forever and blocks its whole partition.”

question: How do you rate limit an API?
answer:
Rate limiting protects you from abuse, runaway clients, and accidental self-DoS by capping requests per client per window. Where to put it matters:

  • Gateway/ingress (nginx, Spring Cloud Gateway, an API gateway) — best default. Bad traffic never reaches your app or costs you a thread.
  • In-app (Resilience4j RateLimiter, Bucket4j) — when limits are per-user/per-plan and need business context the gateway doesn't have.

The key design decision is local vs distributed: an in-memory limiter on 5 replicas means the real limit is 5× what you configured, and it resets on every deploy. A shared store (Redis) gives one true limit across the fleet.

Algorithm: the token bucket is the usual answer — it allows a controlled burst while enforcing a steady average rate.
tip: The question behind the question: 'you have 4 pods and a 100/min limit — what does each pod allow?' If your limiter is in-memory, the answer is 400/min. That's why it needs Redis.
deep:
Algorithms

Fixed window     count per clock minute. Simple, but allows 2x at the boundary:
                 100 at 10:00:59 + 100 at 10:01:00 = 200 in one second.
Sliding window   fixes the boundary burst; more state to track.
Token bucket     tokens refill at a steady rate; a full bucket allows a burst.
                 Best fit for real traffic — bursty but bounded. Most common.
Leaky bucket     smooths output to a constant rate; queues rather than rejects.

Token bucket is usually right because real clients are bursty: a page loading 10 resources at once is legitimate, and a strict per-second limit would reject it.

Return 429 properly

HTTP/1.1 429 Too Many Requests
Retry-After: 30
X-RateLimit-Limit: 100
X-RateLimit-Remaining: 0
X-RateLimit-Reset: 1735689660

Retry-After is the important one — without it, well-behaved clients guess, and usually guess "immediately", which keeps you saturated. Returning 503 or 500 instead of 429 tells the client to retry as if it were your fault.

What do you key on?

API key / user id   best — real identity, supports per-plan tiers
IP address          crude: corporate NATs and mobile carriers share one IP,
                    so you'd throttle a whole office as if it were one user;
                    and attackers rotate IPs trivially
endpoint + user     lets you protect expensive endpoints specifically

Login endpoints need per-account and per-IP limits: per-IP alone misses a distributed credential-stuffing attack; per-account alone lets one IP enumerate thousands of accounts.

Fail open or closed?

If Redis is down, does everything get blocked (fail closed) or unlimited (fail open)? For rate limiting, usually fail open — losing your limiter shouldn't take down the API. For a limiter guarding something genuinely dangerous (payments, auth), fail closed. Being asked this and having a reasoned answer is the differentiator.

Rate limiting vs bulkhead vs circuit breaker

Rate limiter   caps requests per unit TIME       — protects from abuse
Bulkhead       caps CONCURRENT requests          — protects from resource exhaustion
Circuit breaker stops calling a FAILING dependency — protects from cascades

They solve different problems and compose. Confusing them is common.

Virtual threads make this more important

The 200-thread Tomcat pool used to be an accidental rate limiter — it physically capped concurrency. With virtual threads there's no such ceiling, so 50,000 concurrent requests all reach your database. Explicit rate limiting and bulkheads now have to do the job the thread pool was doing implicitly.

How to say it in an interview

“Ideally at the gateway so bad traffic never costs me a thread, and in-app with Bucket4j when the limit is per-user or per-plan. Token bucket, because real clients are legitimately bursty. The thing I'd flag is that it must be distributed — an in-memory limiter across 4 pods means the effective limit is 4x and resets on deploy, so the counters live in Redis. And I return 429 with Retry-After, since without it clients just retry immediately and keep you saturated.”

question: How do you manage configuration and secrets across many services?
answer:
Options, in rough order of modernity:

  • Spring Cloud Config Server — a central service serving config from a Git repo. You get versioning, audit, and per-profile/per-service resolution, plus refresh without redeploy.
  • Kubernetes ConfigMaps and Secrets — mounted as env vars or files. No extra service to run; the platform does it.
  • A dedicated secrets manager — Vault, AWS Secrets Manager. This is the right answer for secrets: encryption at rest, access policies, audit logs, and rotation.

The non-negotiable part: secrets never go in Git, not even in a private repo — and a Kubernetes Secret is only base64, not encryption, so it needs encryption-at-rest and RBAC to mean anything.
tip: Config Server is less common now — Kubernetes ConfigMaps plus a secrets manager cover most of it without another service to keep highly available. Saying that shows current thinking rather than 2018 Spring Cloud reflexes.
deep:
Config Server's own problem

It becomes a single point of failure: if it's down when a service starts, the service can't boot. Mitigations are a local fallback, retry with backoff, and running multiple replicas — but you've added a highly-available service just to serve YAML. That trade-off is exactly why many teams moved to ConfigMaps, where the platform handles distribution and there's nothing extra to keep alive.

@RefreshScope and its limits

POST /actuator/refresh    -> re-binds @RefreshScope beans with new values

Genuinely useful for feature flags and thresholds. But it doesn't refresh everything — a DataSource or a pool sized at startup won't change, and a bean that read a value into a local field at construction keeps the old one. Partial refresh can leave the app in a mixed state: half the beans on the new config, half on the old. For anything structural, redeploy. Knowing the limits is more valuable than knowing the annotation.

Kubernetes Secrets are base64, not encrypted

kubectl get secret db-creds -o jsonpath='{.data.password}' | base64 -d
# -> your production password, in one command, from anyone with get-secret RBAC

They're stored in etcd — plaintext unless encryption-at-rest is enabled. So: turn on encryption at rest, lock down RBAC, and for anything serious use Vault or a cloud secrets manager with an external-secrets operator syncing them in. Calling out "base64 is encoding, not encryption" is a small thing that lands well.

Rotation

A static DB password in a ConfigMap is never rotated because rotating it means a coordinated redeploy. Vault issues dynamic, short-lived credentials — the app leases a DB password valid for an hour and renews it. A leaked credential expires by itself. That's the argument for a real secrets manager over files.

Config drift

Central config in Git gives you the thing ad-hoc env vars never do: a reviewable history. "Why does prod behave differently?" becomes a diff instead of an archaeology project. That auditability is Config Server's most durable benefit, and it's also what GitOps (ArgoCD/Flux) gives you for ConfigMaps — which is another reason the dedicated server is less needed now.

Never commit secrets

A secret committed to Git is compromised permanently — it's in every clone and in the reflog; deleting the file doesn't remove it from history. It must be rotated, not just deleted. Use git-secrets or a pre-commit scanner so it never lands. If you must keep encrypted values in Git, SOPS or Sealed Secrets encrypt the value so the repo holds ciphertext only.

How to say it in an interview

“Config Server backed by Git gives you versioned, reviewable config and refresh without redeploy — but it's another highly-available service and a startup dependency, so on Kubernetes I'd usually just use ConfigMaps with GitOps for the same audit trail. Secrets are separate: Vault or a cloud secrets manager, because a Kubernetes Secret is only base64 and Vault gives short-lived rotating credentials. And a secret committed to Git has to be rotated, not deleted — it's in the history forever.”

question: How do services find each other, and what does an API gateway do?
answer:
Service discovery solves "where is the order service?" when instances come and go with autoscaling. Options: a registry like Eureka/Consul (services register on startup, clients look up and load balance client-side), or — on Kubernetes — DNS-based discovery built into the platform: http://order-service resolves via the Service, which load balances for you. No Eureka needed.

An API gateway (Spring Cloud Gateway) is the single entry point in front of your services. It handles the concerns you don't want duplicated in every service: routing, authentication, rate limiting, TLS termination, CORS, request aggregation, and canary routing.

The modern reality: on Kubernetes, the platform provides most of this — Services for discovery, Ingress for the gateway. Eureka and Ribbon are largely legacy.
tip: Saying 'on Kubernetes I wouldn't run Eureka — Services give me DNS discovery and load balancing for free' is a much stronger answer than reciting the Netflix stack.
code:
# Spring Cloud Gateway — routing + cross-cutting concerns
spring:
  cloud:
    gateway:
      routes:
        - id: orders
          uri: lb://order-service          # lb:// = load-balanced via discovery
          predicates:
            - Path=/api/orders/**
          filters:
            - StripPrefix=1
            - name: CircuitBreaker
              args: { name: ordersCb, fallbackUri: forward:/fallback }
            - name: RequestRateLimiter
              args:
                redis-rate-limiter.replenishRate: 100
                redis-rate-limiter.burstCapacity: 200

# on Kubernetes, discovery is just DNS:
#   http://order-service.default.svc.cluster.local  -> the Service load balances
deep:
Client-side vs server-side load balancing

Client-side (Eureka + Spring Cloud LoadBalancer)
  client fetches the instance list, picks one itself
  + no extra network hop, client can be smart (zone-aware, weighted)
  - every client needs the logic; a stale registry sends traffic to dead pods

Server-side (Kubernetes Service, a load balancer)
  client calls one stable address; the infrastructure routes
  + language-agnostic, nothing in the client
  - one more hop

Eureka's eventual consistency bites

Registration, heartbeats, and client cache refresh each have their own interval — so a dead instance can stay in a client's list for tens of seconds, and traffic keeps hitting it. Eureka deliberately chooses availability over consistency (AP): during a network partition it keeps serving a stale registry rather than refusing. That's usually the right call, but it means retries and circuit breakers are mandatory, not optional — you will be routed to dead instances. This is the nuance that shows real experience.

Gateway is not a monolith

The failure mode: business logic creeps into the gateway. Suddenly it knows about order validation, every team needs a gateway change to ship, and you have a distributed monolith with a shared bottleneck. Keep it to genuinely cross-cutting concerns: routing, auth, rate limiting, observability. Anything domain-specific belongs in the service.

It's also a single point of failure and a latency hop on every request — so it needs to be replicated and boring.

Gateway must be non-blocking

Spring Cloud Gateway is built on WebFlux/Netty for exactly the right reason: it's I/O-bound proxying, holding many concurrent connections while doing almost no CPU work. That's the textbook reactive use case. (Zuul 1 was blocking and thread-per-request — which is why it was replaced.)

BFF pattern

One gateway serving web, mobile, and partners ends up compromising for all three. The Backend for Frontend gives each client its own gateway shaping responses for that client's needs — mobile gets a compact aggregated payload, web gets a richer one. Worth naming.

Service mesh

Istio/Linkerd push discovery, retries, circuit breaking, mTLS, and tracing into a sidecar, out of your application entirely — so it works uniformly across languages with no library. The trade is real operational complexity. It's the logical end of the trend that started with Netflix libraries and moved into the platform.

How to say it in an interview

“Discovery is a registry like Eureka with client-side load balancing, or — on Kubernetes — just DNS through a Service, which is what I'd use; I wouldn't run Eureka on a platform that already solves it. The gateway is the single entry point for cross-cutting concerns: routing, auth, rate limiting, TLS. I'd keep business logic out of it or it becomes a shared bottleneck every team has to change. And with any discovery you still need retries and circuit breakers, because registries are eventually consistent and will hand you dead instances.”

question: How do you test code that calls an external HTTP service?
answer:
Three levels, and you want a mix:

  • Unit — mock your own client interface. Fast, but proves nothing about the actual HTTP/JSON.
  • WireMock — a real HTTP server returning stubbed responses. Exercises the real client, serialisation, headers, timeouts, and error handling. This is the sweet spot for testing your integration code.
  • Contract tests (Spring Cloud Contract / Pact) — the only thing that catches the provider changing its API.

The gap WireMock leaves is important: your stub encodes your belief about the provider's responses. If the provider changes, your tests still pass and production breaks. That's what contract testing exists to solve.
tip: The insight: mocks and stubs verify your assumptions, not reality — they can't fail when the provider changes. Naming that limitation, then reaching for contract tests, is the senior answer.
deep:
Test the failures, not the happy path

The happy path is the one that already works. What breaks in production is:

withFixedDelay(10_000)              // does your timeout actually fire?
withStatus(500)                     // do you retry? how many times?
withFault(Fault.CONNECTION_RESET_BY_PEER)   // mid-response connection drop
withBody("not json")                // malformed payload
withStatus(429).withHeader("Retry-After", "60")

WireMock can simulate all of these; a mocked interface can't simulate a socket timeout at all. This is the concrete reason to prefer WireMock for client code — and "I test that my timeout actually fires" is a great thing to say.

Why mocks give false confidence

// your test — passes forever
given(paymentApi.charge(any())).willReturn(new Receipt("ok"));

// reality — the provider renamed the field 6 months ago
{"result": "ok"}     // your DTO expects "status" -> null -> NPE in prod

Both the mock and the DTO reflect a belief you formed once. Nothing re-validates it. That's the structural weakness of all test doubles.

Contract testing closes the loop

Consumer-driven (Pact):
  1. consumer's test declares: "I POST /charge and expect {status, id}"
  2. that expectation is published as a pact file
  3. the PROVIDER's CI replays it against the real provider
  4. provider breaks the contract -> the PROVIDER's build fails

The failure lands on the team making the change, before they ship — which is the entire point. Spring Cloud Contract works the other way (provider-defined contracts generate consumer stubs), and guarantees the stubs you test against are the ones the provider actually verifies.

Testcontainers when the real thing is runnable

@Testcontainers
class OrderIT {
  @Container @ServiceConnection
  static PostgreSQLContainer<?> db = new PostgreSQLContainer<>("postgres:16");
  @Container @ServiceConnection
  static KafkaContainer kafka = new KafkaContainer("confluentinc/cp-kafka:7.5.0");
}

For infrastructure you own (Postgres, Kafka, Redis, LocalStack for AWS), run the real thing — no dialect surprises, no stub drift. @ServiceConnection (Boot 3.1) wires the URLs automatically. Use containers for infrastructure, WireMock for third-party APIs you can't run.

Never call the real API in tests

Flaky (their outage fails your build), slow, rate-limited, non-deterministic, and it can create real side effects — real charges, real emails. A test suite that depends on a third party's uptime isn't a test suite.

How to say it in an interview

“WireMock for third-party HTTP, so I exercise the real client, serialisation, and error mapping — and importantly I test the failure modes: delays to prove my timeout fires, 500s to prove the retry works, connection resets. Testcontainers for infrastructure I can actually run. The limitation I'd flag is that a stub only encodes my assumption about the provider — it can't fail when they change their API. That's what consumer-driven contract tests are for: the provider's build breaks instead of my production.”

question: How do you version a REST API?
answer:
Main strategies:

  • URI path — /api/v1/orders. Ugly to purists (the resource didn't change, its representation did), but overwhelmingly the most common: obvious, cacheable, browsable, trivial to route at a gateway.
  • Header / content negotiation — Accept: application/vnd.acme.v2+json. Theoretically cleanest — the URI stays a stable resource identifier — but invisible, hard to test in a browser, and easy for caches and proxies to get wrong.
  • Query param — ?version=2. Simple; muddles caching and filtering.

But the more important answer: the best versioning is not needing to version. Most changes can be made backward compatible — add fields, never remove or repurpose them, and never change a field's type or meaning. Version only on a genuine breaking change.
tip: Lead with 'I'd avoid a new version if I can — additive changes don't need one'. Then URI versioning for pragmatism. Candidates who jump straight to debating headers vs paths miss the actual design question.
deep:
What's actually breaking?

NON-breaking (no version needed):
