  + adding an optional response field   (clients must ignore unknown fields)
  + adding an optional request field
  + adding a new endpoint
  + relaxing a validation rule

BREAKING (needs a version):
  - removing or renaming a field
  - changing a type: "total": 10 -> "total": "10.00"
  - making an optional request field required
  - tightening validation
  - changing the MEANING of a field while keeping its name  <- the worst one

That last is genuinely dangerous: status: "complete" now meaning "paid" rather than "shipped" breaks every client silently, with no error and no type change. It's why field semantics deserve as much care as field names.

Version the representation, not the logic

The failure mode is copying the service layer per version — now a bug fix must land in three places and they drift. Keep one domain service and put versioning at the edge, in DTOs and mappers. V1's mapper just projects the current model into the old shape. That's the design point of this question.

Deprecation is the hard part

HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Deprecation: Sun, 01 Jun 2026 00:00:00 GMT
Sunset: Wed, 01 Jan 2027 00:00:00 GMT
Link: <https://api.acme.com/docs/v2-migration>; rel="deprecation"

Anyone can add v2. Removing v1 is the real work: you need per-version usage metrics to know who's still on it, direct outreach to those consumers, a long sunset window, and ideally a "brownout" — deliberately failing v1 for a few minutes on announced dates so silent consumers discover their dependency before the shutdown. Without a removal plan you accumulate versions forever, and every one is maintenance and attack surface.

Internal vs public

For internal services you control both sides, so you can coordinate a deploy and often skip versioning entirely — expand/contract across two releases instead. A public API has anonymous consumers who will never upgrade; versioning and long sunsets are unavoidable. Mobile is the extreme: users run a two-year-old build forever, so "just update the client" isn't available.

Don't version the whole API for one endpoint

Bumping everything to v2 because one resource changed forces every consumer to migrate for a change that didn't affect them. Per-resource versioning is finer-grained but messier to reason about. Most teams accept a coarse version and lean hard on additive changes.

How to say it in an interview

“First I'd ask whether it needs a version at all — additive changes are backward compatible if clients ignore unknown fields, and most changes can be shaped that way. When something genuinely breaks, URI versioning, because it's explicit and trivial to route. The key design point is versioning the representation, not the logic: one service, different DTOs and mappers per version, or you end up fixing bugs in three places. And the hard part is retiring the old version — that needs usage metrics, Sunset headers, and a real deprecation window.”

question: How do you configure multiple datasources?
answer:
Once there's more than one DataSource, Boot's auto-configuration backs off — it can't guess which is which. You define them manually:

  1. A DataSource bean per database, one marked @Primary.
  2. A separate EntityManagerFactory and TransactionManager per datasource.
  3. @EnableJpaRepositories per datasource, each scoped to its own package of repositories and entities.

The package separation is what makes it work: each repository package binds to exactly one entity manager.

The big caveat: a transaction spans one datasource only. Writing to both in one @Transactional gives you two independent transactions — one can commit while the other rolls back.
tip: Before answering 'how', ask 'why'. Two datasources for read/write splitting is fine; two because one service owns two databases usually means the service boundary is wrong.
deep:
You must name the transaction manager

@Transactional                        // -> the @Primary tx manager. Silently the wrong DB!
@Transactional("reportingTx")         // -> explicit, correct

With two transaction managers, a bare @Transactional picks the primary. A service writing to the reporting DB under the orders transaction manager gets no transaction at all for its writes — autocommit, no rollback, no error. A brutal, silent bug.

No atomicity across datasources

@Transactional
void transfer() {
  ordersRepo.save(o);       // tx A
  reportingRepo.save(r);    // tx B — SEPARATE transaction
}   // A commits, B throws -> A stays committed. Inconsistent.

JTA/XA two-phase commit can do this (Atomikos, Narayana), but it's slow, operationally painful, and locks resources across a network — most teams have moved away. The modern answer is to not need it: eventual consistency via an outbox, or reconsidering why one service writes to two databases.

Read/write splitting — the legitimate case

public class RoutingDataSource extends AbstractRoutingDataSource {
  protected Object determineCurrentLookupKey() {
    return TransactionSynchronizationManager.isCurrentTransactionReadOnly()
        ? "replica" : "primary";
  }
}

@Transactional(readOnly = true)   // -> routed to the replica automatically
public List<OrderDto> findAll() { }

AbstractRoutingDataSource switching on readOnly is elegant — and it's the clearest payoff for marking read methods readOnly = true. The catch is replication lag: write then immediately read and the replica may not have it yet, so the user doesn't see what they just saved. Read-after-write has to go to the primary.

Multi-tenancy uses the same machinery

AbstractRoutingDataSource keyed on a tenant id from a ThreadLocal (set by a filter from a header or the JWT) gives database-per-tenant isolation. Watch two things: the ThreadLocal doesn't propagate to @Async threads, and connection pools are per datasource — 100 tenants × 10 connections = 1000 connections. That scaling wall is why schema-per-tenant or a discriminator column is more common past a handful of tenants.

Migrations too

Flyway auto-configures for the primary datasource only. A second database needs its own Flyway bean with its own migration location — easy to forget until the second DB's schema is missing in prod.

How to say it in an interview

“Auto-configuration backs off, so you define a DataSource, EntityManagerFactory, and TransactionManager per database, with @EnableJpaRepositories scoped to separate packages. The trap is that @Transactional silently uses the primary transaction manager, so writes to the second DB run with no transaction — you must name it. And there's no atomicity across the two without XA, so I'd rather avoid the situation: for read replicas I'd use AbstractRoutingDataSource keyed on readOnly, and for two real databases I'd question the service boundary.”

question: How do you handle file uploads safely?
answer:
MultipartFile on a @PostMapping(consumes = MULTIPART_FORM_DATA_VALUE). Boot auto-configures multipart resolution; set limits with spring.servlet.multipart.max-file-size and max-request-size.

The interesting part is the security, because upload is one of the most attacked endpoints:

  • Never trust the filename — getOriginalFilename() is attacker-controlled and can contain ../../ (path traversal). Generate your own name.
  • Never trust the content type — the client sets it. Sniff the real type from magic bytes.
  • Cap the size, or one request fills the disk or heap.
  • Store outside the webroot — ideally object storage (S3), never a directory the server will execute from.
tip: Path traversal via filename is the classic: a file named ../../../etc/cron.d/x overwrites system files. Generating a UUID name and ignoring theirs entirely kills that whole class of bug.
deep:
Path traversal

filename: "../../../../etc/cron.d/pwn"
Paths.get("/uploads").resolve(file.getOriginalFilename())
  -> /etc/cron.d/pwn      // resolve() happily escapes the base directory

Generating a UUID and storing the original name only as a display label in the database eliminates it entirely — no sanitising, no blocklist to get wrong. Allow-listing beats escaping.

Content-Type is a client-supplied string

Content-Type: image/png        <- attacker types this
<?php system($_GET['c']); ?>   <- actual content

file.getContentType() just echoes the header. Sniff the real type from the bytes (Apache Tika), and cross-check that the extension matches the sniffed type. And never derive the stored extension from the uploaded filename.

Never store uploads in a servable/executable directory

If uploads land somewhere the web server serves, an uploaded .jsp/.php becomes remote code execution — the classic web shell. Object storage (S3) with a separate domain avoids it structurally: S3 doesn't execute anything. Serve files back with an explicit Content-Type and Content-Disposition: attachment plus X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff, or an uploaded HTML file becomes stored XSS on your domain.

Streaming vs buffering

file.getBytes()          // ENTIRE file into heap — 10 concurrent 100MB uploads = OOM
file.getInputStream()    // stream it through — constant memory
spring.servlet.multipart.file-size-threshold: 2KB   # spill to disk above this

Boot buffers small files in memory and spills larger ones to a temp dir. Always stream to the destination rather than materialising bytes.

Big files: presigned URLs

For large uploads, don't proxy the bytes through your app at all — issue a presigned S3 URL and let the client upload directly. Your service never holds the file, never burns a thread for minutes, and doesn't need the bandwidth. It's the standard answer for anything over a few MB, and it also removes the need to worry about request timeouts on slow mobile connections.

Zip bombs and scanning

If you decompress uploads, a 42KB zip can expand to petabytes. Cap the decompressed size and the entry count, never trust the declared size. For anything users download again, run a malware scan (ClamAV) — otherwise you're a distribution channel.

Limits are enforced in layers

max-file-size is Spring's check — but nginx's client_max_body_size (default 1MB) will reject the request first, with a confusing 413 that never reaches your app. All layers must agree.

How to say it in an interview

“MultipartFile with size limits, but the important part is treating everything from the client as hostile: I generate a UUID filename and ignore theirs, because ../../ in a filename is path traversal; I sniff the type from magic bytes rather than trusting the Content-Type header; and I store in S3 rather than anywhere servable, since an uploaded JSP in a servable directory is remote code execution. For large files I'd hand out a presigned URL so the bytes never touch my service.”

question: How do you handle sessions when running multiple instances?
answer:
The problem: an HTTP session lives in one JVM's memory. Add a second instance and a user's next request may land on a pod that's never heard of them — they're logged out at random. Restarts and rolling deploys log everyone out too.

Options:

  • Stateless (JWT/token) — no session at all. Scales trivially; the revocation trade-off applies.
  • Spring Session + Redis — sessions move to a shared store; every instance sees them, and they survive restarts. Usually the best of both: real revocable sessions and horizontal scaling. It's one dependency and one property.
  • Sticky sessions — the load balancer pins a user to one instance. Works, but that instance dying loses their session, it defeats even load distribution, and it makes rolling deploys disruptive.
tip: Sticky sessions are the answer that sounds fine and ages badly — it makes your load balancer stateful and your pods non-disposable. Spring Session + Redis is the one-line fix people forget exists.
code:
<dependency>
  <artifactId>spring-session-data-redis</artifactId>
</dependency>

spring:
  session:
    store-type: redis
    timeout: 30m
  data:
    redis:
      host: redis

// that's it — HttpSession now transparently reads/writes Redis.
// Existing code is unchanged:
@GetMapping("/cart")
public Cart cart(HttpSession session) {
  return (Cart) session.getAttribute("cart");   // now shared across all pods
}
deep:
How Spring Session works

A servlet Filter wraps the request and replaces getSession() with an implementation backed by Redis. Your code — and Spring Security — keep using HttpSession unchanged. That transparency is why it's such an easy win: no code change, no architecture change.

Everything in the session must be serializable

session.setAttribute("cart", cart);   // Cart must implement Serializable
// -> otherwise it silently fails or throws on write

And it now crosses the network on every request, so keep sessions small. Storing a fat object graph means serialising and deserialising it on every single request — a real latency cost people don't anticipate. Store an id, not the object.

Session fixation

http.sessionManagement(s -> s
    .sessionFixation().migrateSession());   // Spring Security's default — good

The attack: an attacker sets a known session id in your browser (via a link or XSS), you log in, and the server keeps that id — now they share your authenticated session. The defence is issuing a new session id on authentication. Spring Security does it by default; knowing why it exists is the point.

Concurrent session control

http.sessionManagement(s -> s
    .maximumSessions(1)
    .maxSessionsPreventsLogin(false));   // new login kicks the old session out

"Only one device at a time" — and it needs the shared store to work across instances, since one pod can't see another's sessions.

Cookie security

server:
  servlet:
    session:
      cookie:
        http-only: true      # JS can't read it -> XSS can't steal it
        secure: true         # HTTPS only
        same-site: lax       # CSRF mitigation

Stateless vs sessions — the honest comparison

Sessions (+ Redis)   revocable instantly, nothing sensitive on the client,
                     server can change permissions immediately
                     - needs Redis (another dependency, another failure mode)
                     - a Redis lookup per request (fast, but not free)

JWT                  no lookup, no shared store, works across services
                     - CANNOT be revoked before expiry
                     - permission changes don't take effect until refresh

The reflex is "stateless is more scalable", but Redis-backed sessions scale horizontally perfectly well — the trade is really revocability vs a shared store, not scalability. For a single application with a browser front end, sessions are often the better and simpler engineering choice. Being able to argue that against the JWT default is a strong signal.

How to say it in an interview

“In-memory sessions break the moment you have more than one instance — requests land on a pod that doesn't know the user. I'd avoid sticky sessions, since that makes the load balancer stateful and pods non-disposable. Spring Session with Redis is a dependency and a property: HttpSession keeps working unchanged, sessions are shared and survive restarts. The real trade against JWT isn't scalability — Redis sessions scale fine — it's that sessions are instantly revocable at the cost of a shared store.”

question: How do you register a bean conditionally?
answer:
The @Conditional* family — the same mechanism Boot's own auto-configuration uses:

  • @ConditionalOnProperty — the workhorse: enable a bean from config, with matchIfMissing deciding opt-in vs opt-out.
  • @ConditionalOnClass / @ConditionalOnMissingClass — classpath-driven.
  • @ConditionalOnBean / @ConditionalOnMissingBean — context-driven; the back-off mechanism.
  • @ConditionalOnWebApplication, @ConditionalOnExpression, or a custom Condition.
  • @Profile — really a specialised @Conditional on active profiles.

Important limitation: these are evaluated once, at startup. They decide whether a bean exists — they are not runtime feature flags.
tip: The distinction that matters: @ConditionalOnProperty is a startup decision, so flipping the property needs a restart. If you need to toggle without a restart, that's a runtime flag check, not a conditional bean.
deep:
@ConditionalOnBean is order-dependent — and dangerous

It evaluates against beans registered so far. So the same code can match or not depending on configuration-class ordering, which is not guaranteed for your own @Configuration classes. Spring's docs explicitly say @ConditionalOnBean/@ConditionalOnMissingBean are only reliable in auto-configuration classes, where @AutoConfigureAfter gives a defined order. Using them in application code produces "works on my machine" bugs. Genuinely good detail to know.

matchIfMissing defines your default UX

matchIfMissing = true    -> ON unless explicitly disabled  (opt-out)
matchIfMissing = false   -> OFF unless explicitly enabled  (opt-in, default)

For a library, opt-out means adding the dependency changes behaviour immediately — sometimes delightful, sometimes a nasty surprise. For anything with cost or risk, opt-in.

Not a feature flag

@Bean
@ConditionalOnProperty("app.new-checkout.enabled")
CheckoutService newCheckout() { }
// flipping the property at runtime does NOTHING — needs a restart

Real feature flags need a runtime check, ideally against a flag service (Unleash, LaunchDarkly) so you can toggle per-user, do percentage rollouts, and kill a bad feature in seconds without a deploy:

if (flags.isEnabled("new-checkout", userId)) { newFlow(); } else { oldFlow(); }

The strategy-pattern middle ground: register both implementations as beans and pick at call time — you keep polymorphism and get runtime switching.

Custom condition

public class OnKubernetesCondition implements Condition {
  public boolean matches(ConditionContext ctx, AnnotatedTypeMetadata md) {
    return ctx.getEnvironment().containsProperty("KUBERNETES_SERVICE_HOST");
  }
}

@Bean
@Conditional(OnKubernetesCondition.class)
PodInfoContributor podInfo() { return new PodInfoContributor(); }

Extend SpringBootCondition instead of implementing Condition to get your reasoning into the --debug condition report — otherwise nobody can tell why your bean vanished.

Debugging a missing bean

--debug prints the Condition Evaluation Report with exactly which condition failed. It's the answer to "why isn't my bean there?" and beats adding print statements.

How to say it in an interview

“The @Conditional family — mostly @ConditionalOnProperty, with matchIfMissing choosing opt-in versus opt-out, and @ConditionalOnMissingBean for overridable defaults in a library. Two things I'd flag: @ConditionalOnBean depends on evaluation order, so it's only reliable in auto-configuration classes, not application code. And they're startup-time decisions, not feature flags — flipping the property needs a restart, so real toggles need a runtime check.”

question: How do you control the HTTP status and headers of a response?
answer:
Three ways, in increasing explicitness:

  • Return the object — 200 OK automatically. Cleanest when the status never varies.
  • @ResponseStatus(HttpStatus.CREATED) — a fixed non-200 status on the method (or on an exception class).
  • ResponseEntity<T> — full control over status, headers, and body. Use it when the status varies or you must set headers like Location.

Status codes carry real meaning — clients, proxies, caches, and monitoring all act on them. Returning 200 OK with {"success": false} in the body is the anti-pattern: it throws away everything HTTP gives you for free.
tip: Know 201-with-Location for POST, 204 for DELETE, and 401 vs 403 (unauthenticated vs unauthorised). Those three distinctions catch most candidates out.
deep:
The codes that matter

200 OK           GET, or PUT that returns the updated body
201 Created      POST — MUST include a Location header pointing at the new resource
202 Accepted     async — "I took it, it's not done yet" (return a status URL)
204 No Content   DELETE, or PUT with nothing to return
400 Bad Request  malformed / validation failed
401 Unauthorized WRONGLY NAMED — it means UNAUTHENTICATED (who are you?)
403 Forbidden    authenticated, but not allowed
404 Not Found    absent — or deliberately hiding a 403 from an unauthorised user
409 Conflict     duplicate, or optimistic lock failure
422 Unprocessable syntactically fine, semantically invalid
429 Too Many Requests   + Retry-After
500 Internal     you broke
503 Unavailable  overloaded/down — + Retry-After

401 vs 403

The names are historically backwards. 401 = I don't know who you are (no or invalid credentials — the client should authenticate or refresh). 403 = I know who you are and you still can't (re-authenticating won't help). Returning 403 for an expired token is a real bug: the client sees "forbidden", doesn't refresh, and the user is stuck.

404 as a security choice

Returning 403 for someone else's document confirms that it exists — an enumeration oracle. Returning 404 leaks nothing. GitHub does this for private repos: they're indistinguishable from nonexistent ones. A deliberate, defensible trade of clarity for privacy.

Don't tunnel errors through 200

HTTP/1.1 200 OK
{"success": false, "error": "user not found"}

Consequences: every client must parse the body to know if it worked; monitoring reports 100% success while users see failures; proxies cache the "error"; retry logic can't work; and your error-rate dashboards are fiction. The status line exists precisely so intermediaries don't need to understand your payload.

Location on 201

HTTP/1.1 201 Created
Location: /api/users/1042

Part of the REST contract — the client learns the new resource's URI without guessing. Widely skipped, and easy credit.

202 for long work

If the work takes 30s, don't hold the connection: return 202 Accepted with a Location pointing at a status resource the client polls. Holding a thread (and risking a proxy timeout) for 30s is how you turn a slow feature into an outage.

How to say it in an interview

“Return the object for a plain 200, @ResponseStatus for a fixed one, and ResponseEntity when the status varies or I need headers — a POST returning 201 with a Location header, for instance. I care about getting these right because monitoring, caches, and client retry logic all key off the status: returning 200 with success: false means your dashboards show 100% success while users are failing. And 401 is unauthenticated, 403 is authenticated-but-not-allowed — sending 403 for an expired token stops clients refreshing.”
